["[WomansExponent | Vol28 | 1899 | August | Issue: 5 | Title: R. S. REPORTS. | Author: Unknown]

R. S. REPORTS.
	DENVER, COLORADO. 
Editor Woman's Exponent:
	The members of the Relief Society of
Denver wish me to write you, to let you
know how we are getting along. 
	Our Relief Society has only a small
membership, but we endeavor to do our
duty as best we can, with our limited
means. During the past summer we have
assisted in caring for some of the poor in
this city, and we have furnished a room
complete in “The Haven,” which is a home
and industrial training school for young
girls. They come from all parts of the
State; some are orphans, and others might
better be, and some are half orphans. This
room is occupied by two girls as a bed-
room. It is furnished with a nice iron
bedstead and bedding, also chairs, wash-
stand, glass, rug,s and all things necessary
for a girl's bedroom; and we have the
privilege of placing our literature there for
them to read, and on the door we have a
nice plate with the name of our Church on
it: “Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day
Saints.” 
	We have a good band of sisters here, if
they are few in number. They feel like
pressing on in the good work, and we en-
joy the EXPONENT and have articles from
it read in our meetings.
	Ever praying for the health of the
editor of the EXPONENT, Sister Wells, we 
remain,
	Yours in the cause of truth,
		EVA HALEY, Pres. 
		MARY E. WOOLLEY, Cor. Sec.
		ST. JOHNS STAKE, KANSAS. 
	Our society was organized July 8, 1899,
by our mission president, Elder Wm. Jack,
and we now have seventeen members en-
rolled. 
	We have in our treasury, twenty dollars.
We have given one ice cream social, from
which we cleared five dollars and seventy
cents. We have pieced two quilts, and
quilted three; also made three comforts and
sewed carpet rags. We have held twenty-
five working and testimony meetings.
Those held on the first Thursday of each
month are devoted to testimony bearing.
	Following are the minutes of our first
regular monthly meeting:
	Saint John’s Relief Society held in the
Saint’s Church, Thursday, August 3, 1899, 
at 2 o’clock p. m.  presided over by our president, Sister Lucy Brekenridge. 
	The meeting was opened by singing the


[Column 1]
hymn, “We thank Thee, O God, for a
prophet.” Prayer by Sister Breckenridge.
Singing, “Come let us anew, our journey
pursue.” Roll was called and minutes of
previous meeting read and approved.
Members present, eleven. 
	Sister Oden spoke for a short time. Said
that she was thankful that she belonged to
our society; that she felt like going ahead
and doing what she could.
	Sister Hawkins said that the Lord would
bless us if we would be faithful, and prayed
that we might continue in the Gospel.
	Sister Baker bore her testimony with
much feeling. Desired always to be faith-
ful and do her part, both in the Church
and in the society.
	Sister Dixon spoke of the true prophet of
God. Said we were just starting and must
have a foundation—trust in the Lord. Ex-
horted us to keep ourselves pure, and bore
a strong testimony to the truthfulness of
the Gospel.
	Sister Gooing was thankful to meet with
us again. Expressed her willingness to do
all she could to aid the society.
	Singing, “O, say what is truth.”
	Sister Breckenridge felt thankful that she
lived in this day of the restoration of the
Gospel. She hoped that we would all go
ahead and be faithful, and we would be
blessed.
	Sister Miller felt well and thought the
Lord would bless us if we did what we
could. Testified that she knew that we
had the true Gospel.
	Sister Smith, of Thistle, Utah, who was
visiting with us, said she would soon have
to leave for her home, and might never see
us again in this life; but she felt we would
meet in the next. Knew the Lord would
bless us, He had blessed her.
	The sisters then sang, “Earth with her
ten thousand flowers.
	Sisters Breckenridge, Gooing, Dixon and
Budge each donated ten cents.
	Singing, “O, my Father.” Benediction
by Sister Hawkins.
			LUCY BRECKENRIDGE, Pres.,
			MARGARET BUDGE, Sec.
HAMBURG, GERMANY.
	The Relief Society of Hamburg is in a
very flourishing condition. It is presided
over by Sister Reicht, with Sister Ahers as
first counselor and another good sister,
whose name I do not remember, as second
counselor. They meet twice a month, a
work meeting and a testimony meeting
alternately. They have not many poor,
for they are nearly all poor, but through
untiring efforts, they succeed in almost pay-
ing the rent of the hall where the Relief
Society meetings are held.
	They are earnest workers in the cause,
and desire to do all they can to roll on the
great work of the Latterdays. They enter-
tain the Elders all it is possible for them to
do, some inviting them to take a meal a
week with them, some do part of their
washing and others mend their clothes.
My heart went out to them in their great
efforts to bless the Elders when sent to
them, out of their scanty store.
	They desire to come to Zion, it being
their dream by day and by night. They
love the sisters in Zion. They knit socks
and stockings, let their president buy
towels and other necessary articles, on
which she gets ten per cent off, they paying
the regular store price, so as to let that
much go into the treasury. They have a
secretary and treasurer.

[Column 2]
	Of course they do not use quilts in
Germany, so those avenues we have at
home are not open to them to assist them in
gathering means. The light feather bed,
which, by the way, is decidedly comfortable
on a cold night, and a spread is all they
have on their beds. But they are so earn-
est and intelligent, the love of Zion quick-
ens their understanding and the cause they
work in makes it a labor of love.
	Three meetings were held and one
officers’ meeting. President Reicht enter-
tained some of the Elders and myself, as
also did Sister Ahers. Both of these fami-
lies are getting near the day when they
may come to Zion. Some of the other sis-
ters invited us to their homes also, among
them being Sister Schroder, who puts every
spare ten penny into a box, thus saving
the money to get to Zion.
	I spoke to the first German meeting of
sisters on July 24, 1899. They send their
love to President Zina D. H. Young and
all the other sisters in Zion. They would
have all come down to the boat when I
left, only that it did not go out until 11:30
p. m. Still some came with the brethren,
who would not say good bye, but “We’ll
meet again in Zion.”
WE’LL MEET AGAIN IN ZION.
	To the Saints in Hamburg and my friends.

We’ll meet again in Zion, fair Zion far away,
And clasp each friendly hand on that gladsome
	day;
We’ll see the mountains rising, snow-capp'd to
	the skies,
And the lake on whose fair bosom the evening
	sunset lies.

We’ll meet again in Zion, where God’s own Tem-
	ple stands,
And tell how we as strangers, glad meet in fore-
	ign lands;
As comrades in the Vineyard, where hearts so
	closely meet,
Brave toiling on midst trials, to tell the story
	sweet.

How Christ will come to Zion, His church to teach
	anew,
And choose His very own, His jewels not a few;
Then may we meet in Zion, though oceans inter-
	vene
And roll forever onward, that land and us be-
	tween.

Then, too, in that blest Zion, the city paved with
	gold,
May we amid its splendors, our Savior’s face be-
	hold;
O, there where Saints are gathered from every 
	land and clime,
May we hear the welcome plaudit, “Beloved you
	are mine.”

And through the countless ages, eternities un-
	born,—
When the purple hills of Paradise, gently kiss the
	morn;
And Life’s flowing river, for aye glides smooth
	along,
May we join in the chorus of the New, Unchang-
	ing Song.
						LYDIA D. ALDER.
	North Sea, August 10, 1899,
			FREMONT.
	A delightfully successful tour through
this (the Fremont) Stake has just been
completed by the Stake presidency of our
Relief Society and others, accompanied by
Sister L. L. Greene Richards, of Salt Lake
City.
	Sister Richards came to Rexburg on the
23rd of July, and went on to Haden, in
Teton Valley, forty miles from here, where
she spent Pioneers’ Day with her sister,
Mrs. M. G. Homer, and daughter. On the
25th Sister Homer and daughter accompan-
ied Sister Richards back to Rexburg, where
they were joined by Relief Society Stake

[Column 3]
President Temperance Hinckley, and First
Counselor Mary S. Anderson.
	These sisters commenced their trip
through the Stake on Wednesday, July 26.
Their first meeting was held with the Re-
lief Society of Salem Ward. Continuing
their travels and visits, they held meetings
with the sisters in each of the following
named wards: Island, Lyman, Burton,
Rexburg, Hyatt, Egin, Parker, Ora, Wil-
ford, Twin Groves, Fall River, Mary’sville,
Teton City, Pratt, Chapin, Darby, Ray-
mond, Leigh and Haden.
	With the exception of Jackson’s Hole
and, I think, one or two other small, scat-
tered settlements, where as yet not enough
women reside to form a Relief Society,
every ward in the Stake was visited. 
	Besides the Relief Society meetings, the
sisters met with the Young Ladies’ and
Primary associations in some instances,
where such meetings could be arranged.
	Sisters Flamm, Black and Atkinson
joined the company of visiting sisters in
some of the wards. In several instances
they were also favored with the presence
and support of Stake President Thomas E.
Ricks, his counselor, Wm. F. Rigby, and 
Patriarch A. E. Hinckley. The Bishops
and other leading brethren also showed a
kindly interest in the work of the sisters in
most of the wards.
	There were two wards in which it was
found the removal and resignation of
the Relief Society president rendered re-
organization necessary; and several of the
branch societies were needing to be aroused
and reminded that the duties required at
the hands of the sisters in the society were
of vast importance, and must be diligently
attended to. But in the majority of cases,
good, harmonious work was being done;
and an eagerness to be taught how to im-
prove and progress was manifested by the
brave, noble women who are pioneering
their portion of the work of the Lord in
these comparatively new settlements.
	In all the meetings held by the sisters in
this annual visit through the Stake, the
teachings were of a remarkably elevating
and purifying nature, combining temporal
and spiritual matters in a way to be clearly
understood by all.
	The Relief Society throughout, and the
Young Ladies’ and Primary Associations,
were urged to take an active interest in all
each other’s welfare; to visit back and forth,
encouraging and sustaining each other with
their united faith and prayers, as well as by
good words and works. The law of tithing
and kindred topics were impressively ex-
plained and illustrated, together with near-
ly, if not all, the principles and doctrines
belonging to the Gospel of salvation, both
for the present and the future life.
	“Sister Lula” (as we like to designate
L. L. Greene Richards) being the principal
speaker in all the meetings which have
been mentioned here, took especial care
never to neglect presenting a loving mes-
sage and blessing to the sisters of the Fre-
mont Stake from the honored and beloved
president of the Sisterhood, Sister Zina D.
H. Young, and her counselors; in response
to which, on behalf of the Sisterhood of the
Fremont Stake of Zion, we sincerely re-
turn love and gratitude to dear “Aunt
Zina” and her associates; and thank her
and them for sending us the motherly
benedictions and instructions by so worthy
and capable a messenger as our dear Sister
Lula.
	May the peace and love of heaven ev


[Column 1]
abide with Sister Zina D. H. Young and
the great and grand organization over
which she presides.
			SARAH A. BARNES,
					Stake Sec. R. S.
		SALT LAKE STAKE.
	The Relief Society conference of Salt
Lake Stake, was held in the Assembly
Hall, Saturday, September 2, 1899, com-
mencing at 10 a. m., President M. Isabella
Horne presiding. On the stand, beside
President Horne, were her counselors,
Annie T. Hyde and Clara C. Cannon,
Corresponding Secretary Maria W. Wilcox,
and Aid Julia C. Howe; also President B.
W. Smith, of the General Board, and Sis-
ter Elizabeth J. Stevenson.
	Meeting opened by singing, “We thank
Thee, O God, for a prophet.” Prayer by
Counselor C. C. Cannon. Continued by
singing, “For the strength of the hills,”
etc. Roll called: city wards represented,
20; country wards, 20. Minutes of pre-
vious conference read and accepted.
	The secretary stated that she had great
difficulty in getting in reports in time to
make Stake report; urged greater prompt-
ness.
	President Horne gave valuable instruc-
tions to the sisters on the duties of mothers
in training their children, and duties in the
Church. “Out of the 2717 members re-
ported, we should have a much larger at-
tendance at meeting this morning.” Said
she was pleased to see such a good repre-
sentation of officers. Felt sure that all the
sisters could make arrangements to come to
conference once in three months, if they
had sufficient interest.
	President Bathsheba W. Smith said:
“The word of the Lord to the Saints is that
the sisters and the Saints should pay tith-
ing and donations, and not let the names
of the brethren alone appear on the books.
Teach your children to keep the Sabbath
day holy; teach them to pray and observe
the Word of Wisdom.”
	Counselor Annie T. Hyde said: Every
one comes here to gain instruction, and be
able to carry home to her society something
that will be of benefit. Our report shows
that the attendance at the ward meetings is
not so large as it should be. There is a great
responsibility resting upon the officers.
There are just as good women outside the
society as there are in it, but they have
not the spirit of Relief Society work. How
many are going to work to help bring them
in and inspire them? The younger sisters
should be more interested, for it is a noble
work of sacrifice and doing good. Many
of our nurses are doing a noble work of
charity among the sick and needy. We ex-
pect soon to organize classes for young
ladies and young mothers, where lectures
on hygiene, physical development, etc.,
will be given. Impress upon your children
at your knee the divinity of the mission of
Joseph Smith, that when they go out
among the nations, they may have the
spirit of truth burning in their hearts.”
	Counselor M. M. Daniels, of the Relief
Society of Utah Stake, expressed great
pleasure in meeting with us, this being her
first opportunity of doing so. Rejoiced in
all she had heard here. Spoke of the good
results of paying tithing, and gave many
good instructions. “The young people
have too much amusement, for it keeps
them in a state of excitement. One amuse-
ment follows so quickly upon another, that

[Column 2]
their minds never have a chance to calm
down. It is really injurious to their
nervous system.” Thanked the Lord that
she had seen and known the Prophet Joseph
Smith, and had a testimony and knowledge
of the divinity of his mission. She was
healed of chills and fever when she was
baptized at the age of nine years.
	President Sarah J. Cannon, of the Gener-
al Board, said if we would follow out the
instructions we had heard this morning, we
would certainly see good results. Spoke of
her son being healed by the administration
of the Elders. This morning he had left
home to go on a mission. When people
who are outside our Church asked her what
we believe in, she told them that we be-
lieve in what the Savior taught when He
was here upon the earth. Some had said to
her that they did not think we wished to
talk about our religion. She told them
this was a mistake, for we wished every
man, woman and child on the earth to have
a complete knowledge of our faith, that all
the human family might be benefited
	President Mary Ann Hyde, of the Relief
Society of Sanpete Stake, said: “The
progress of this work is marvelous when
we think of the small number at the com-
mencement, and the great extent of the
work now. The mission of President Snow
is wonderful, and he is awakening the
Saints to a sense of duty, and doing a great
work. Do not fall into a state of lethargy.”
	President Horne felt that the time had
been well occupied. Desired a larger at-
tendance in the afternoon to hear the in-
structions that were to be given. She had
received a letter from Sister Lydia D. Alder
from London, and she sent her love to the
sisters and expressed her interest in the
work.
	Conference adjourned until 2 p. m. Sing-
ing, “The time is far spent.” Benedic-
tion by Sister Maria W. Wilcox.
	Afternoon session. Meeting opened by
singing, “Come, come ye saints.” Prayer
by Sister Elizabeth Webb. Singing,
“Zion stands with hills surrounded,” by
Prof. C. J. Thomas, Brother Charles Bar-
rell, Sisters Maggie C. Hull and Lizzie
Thomas. Minutes of afternoon session of
June conference read and accepted. Finan-
cial report read and accepted also.
	Counselor Clara C. Cannon gave much
good instruction to young mothers. Said:
“Motherhood is the grandest mission on
earth. We, as mothers, should lead lives
such as our children should follow. If
there are some who are wayward in youth,
by this good example they may be influ-
enced to return to the fold. The Gospel is
the same that was preached by the Savior,
and is just as beautiful now as it was then.
Teach your children not to form acquain-
tances with those who are not of our faith
that will lead to marriage; ‘be ye not un-
equally yoked together.’ There is much
unhappiness results from such unions.”
	Sister Julia C. Howe felt her soul lifted up
in gratitude to the Lord for the instructions
given here. “In listening to them I often
wish that we might have had such in-
structions when I was young and rearing
my children. But our Heavenly Father
will excuse our ignorance. The young
women should identify themselves with the
Relief Society and assist the older sisters,
for they have had many advantages that
some of the older ones have not enjoyed.”
	Sister Elizabeth J. Stevenson said: “It is
a great pleasure to me to meet with the sis-
ters, to look upon their faces. I am thank-

[Column 3]
ful to live in the day of prophets and
apostles. Our children should never be
permitted to forget the life of the Prophet
Joseph, and his work. The youth should
have correct principles instilled into their
minds. They should be taught economy,
that, through their extravagance, their
parents may not be called upon to mort-
gage their homes. They should be willing
to minister to the sick, go and read to them,
if they wish it. How much better the
young sisters can do this than the older
ones! Do not desire to be freed from the
care of looking after your children. They
will all be grown up, and you will be left
alone soon enough.”
	The quartette sang “I know that my Re-
deemer lives.”
	President Horne spoke on the subject of
tithing. Said: “It should make no differ-
ence to us what is done with the tithing.
How much does it take to keep the Tem-
ple open? to bring the missionaries home?
to pay clerks? and many other things. We
should not try to dictate to the Church
authorities. The Lord will pour out
blessings upon us if we pay our honest
tithing. At the annual meeting in the
branches, the presiding sisters should
be invited to come; it will increase the in-
terest in the respective society. A great
many of the branches of the society in this
Stake are leaving off their work meetings,
but this should not be done. Our work is
temporal as well as spiritual. These
meetings should be held once in two
weeks, and clothing should be made and
repaired and collected for the poor.”
Mentioned the shooting of a young lady in
the Southern States, who was trying to
protect the Elders from a mob.
	President Elmina S. Taylor, of the Y. L.
M. I. A., felt that she had lost much by
not being able to attend our meetings.
“The Relief Society has been the greatest
blessing of my life. No true and faithful
worker in the Relief Society is in danger of
apostatizing.” Was sorry to hear that
some of the branches had stopped their
work meetings, and President Horne had
not given any instruction for this
change.
	Sister Mary Freeze was the next speaker.
She felt thankful for the spirit of obedience
with which she had been blest. There is
a certain reverence due those who are
placed to preside over us. Felt to love
and honor those whom the Lord loved and
honored. Said all should live nearer to
their Heavenly Father, for the reins will be
drawn tighter, and those desiring to partici-
pate in the blessings of the Temple must
live better. Believed that the people were
endeavoring to do better in the matter of
tithing. When we do learn to obey our
Father, we will then be prepared to receive
fully the teachings that are given from
time to time.
	Sister Camilla C. Cobb felt humble, and
felt as though the best women on earth were
associated here. Desired to be active in
the discharge of her duties. Was engaged
now, and always had been engaged in the
training of children. Bore a faithful testi-
mony to the truth of the Gospel, and de-
clared herself to be anxious to do her duty
in every way. Hoped we would be pre-
pared for the sifting time, which is surely
near at hand.
	Conference adjourned to meet on the 2nd
of December. Singing, Doxology. Pray-
er by Elder C. J. Thomas.

GENERAL RELIEF SOCIETY CONFERENCE.
	The General semi-annual Conference of
the Relief Society was held in the Salt
Lake Assembly hall, Thursday, October
5, commencing at 10 a. m., President
Bathsheba W. Smith presiding. Open-
ing hymn, “Glory to God on high,” pray-
er by Dr. Ellis R. Shipp. Sister White
took charge of the singing, which was
furnished by the Granite Stake sisters.
Roll call. showing a large attendance of
representatives from the several Stakes.
	President Bathsheba W. Smith made
an address in which she commended the
sisters for their good works and felt to
congratulate all on the splendid attend-
ance, as it was larger in numbers than at
any previons conference, felt proud of the
labors of the faithful sisters, and urged
them to take care of the youth of Zion
as well as to assist the needy and nurse
the sick. She realized the magnitude of
the work and its importance, and exhorted
one and all to maintain their integrity to
the Gospel and bear testimony of the
truth.
	Reports from presidents were called for
and President Mary John of Utah Stake,
gave a summary of the financial condi-
tion of the Society in that Stake, and re-
ferred to her recent serious illness and her
gratitude for her restoration to health
which enabled her to be present with us
today; spoke of the false teachers and
preachers rising up to lead the weak and
doubting ones astray.
	Sister Leah Blackburn reported Wayne
Stake in excellent condition.
	The sisters from Granite Stake rend-
ered the hymn composed by Sister Emily
H. Woodmansee, for the Relief Society
Jubilee, “Daughters of Zion, the friends
of the poor,” Sister Green leading, Sis-
ter Emilie Maeser accompanist.
	Relief Society Los Angelos branch was
reported by President Eliza Woollocott,
she stated they were differently situated
there from the sisters in Zion and conse-
quently their labors differed from ours,
they assisted the Elders and mission in
various ways, and were doing everything
possible to promote Gospel work; had
some hindrances, yet were prospered in
many respects.
	President Jane S. Richards, Weber
Stake, spoke upon “Visiting and Healing
the Sick.” She referred first to the Sav-
ior's baptism, then related some of her
experiences in the Church and the inci-
dent concerning her baptism, and the
opposition that was raging at that time;
she had been given up by the physician,
etc., she was as one raised from the

[Column 2]
dead after the baptism; told many inter-
esting circumstances in her journeyings
in the Church when the power of God
was made manifest. and appealed to those
present to train their children in the true
Gospel, of which she bore a strong and
earnest testimony; believed that faithful
mothers' prayers would be answered in
regard to their children, when they do all
in their power to teach them right; en-
couraged all present to take part in the
work of the Relief Society; it would be of
the greatest benefit and would make
their daily duties lighter.
	Solo, Beatrice Thomas of Granite
Stake, “Not, Not There, my Child.”
	Singing, “Oh, Jesus, the Giver.” Ben-
ediction President Margaret A. Romney.
	Afternoon session, 2 o'clock, President
B. W. Smith presiding. Singing, Sister
Margaret C. Hull leading, “Glorious
things of thee are spoken.” prayer Sister
Julia P, M. Farnsworth, singing, “For
the strength of the hills we bless Thee,”
accompanist, Sister Lizzie Thomas.
	President B. W. Smith greeted all with
a happy greeting, she was especially
pleased to see so many Relief Society
workers assembled, wanted them all to be
strong in the faith of the Gospel and teach
its principles to their children, generally
I think they do know it is right, remem-
ber we have it direct from the holy an-
gels through the Prophet Joseph Smith,
with the power and authority, and we
have the privilege to bless the sick and to
comfort those who are cast down, and it
is a great thing to have this testimony
ourselves, and that we keep the com-
mandments of God; let our religion be
first and foremost, and manifest that we
belong to the Church and kingdom of
God, I take more pleasure in hearing
others speak instead of speaking myself
and I believe in short speeches and short
prayers, I pray you may be comforted
and blessed, and continue faithful in our
most holy faith, and I bless you all.
Amen.
	President Williamina Erickson, North
Sanpete Stake, gave quite a definite ac-
count of the conditions existing among
the people in that locality, there had been
much sickness and some deaths, spoke of
the financial status of the Relief Society;
they have two good halls and one in
course of erection, some wards own lots,
and expect to build in the near future;
felt the mothers' work an advantage and
hoped through that to reach the fathers
as well as the young mothers.
	President Annie R. Duke, Wasatch
Stake, made a fine report of work done;
she referred to leaving Nauvoo in 1846 at
the time of the exodus, crossed the plains
in 1852, her father's family had endured
much hardship in the experiences of those
days.
	President Lilyus C. Page, of Nebo
Stake, reported the Stake in a flourishing
condition in a temporal way, building
halls, saving grain, making quilts and
homemade carpets; had visited the whole
Stake regularly; and the Tintic district
recently added to Nebo from Juab had
been visited once; the poor and sick were
well cared for and the union meetings
were held regularly on the same day as
the Priesthood meetings
	Duet, two young girls from Ensign
Stake, fine singers, Misses Poulton.
	Sister Hattie Blair, recently returned,
reported the branch in New York City,


[Column 1]
the work they do there is very important,
and has been very effective, she gave a
good account of the meetings and the
spread of the Gospel.
	President Addie Cazier of the Juab
Stake said they had taken up the Book
of Mormon since they got through the
mothers' work, she testified it had been a
blessing, they were anxious the young
mothers should join the society.
	President Ann C. Woodbury, St. George
Stake, referred to the great distances
they had to travel in visiting—a thousand
miles—and not a mile of railroad, they
were very proud of their Stake, much
credit is due the people for energy and
perseverance, they had a good opening in
the Muddy Valley, she had great hopes of
that country, the Lord had blessed them,
etc.
	Sister E. B. Wells spoke on the spirit
of Relief Society work, giving her views
of the importance of entering into it with
one's whole soul, felt it was a wonderful
development for the sisters, the greatest
and grandest organization of women in
the world.
	Sister Julina L. Smith spoke upon
"Business Methods in the Home," Sister
Smith discussed this subject in all its
phases; asked the question, "What does
it take to establish any kind of business?"
Union and order, everyone should have
a share in the home, to do some of the
work; each one doing the part allotted to
her, etc.; thought there was a very great
difference between economy and stingi-
ness, spoke particularly upon the art of
bread making., and gave many useful,
suggestive hints on housekeeping.
	Sister Margaret C. Hull sang a solo,
"There is no song but has a minor
strain," "The Echo."
	Choir sang "The trials of the present
day," etc. Benediction, President Annie
S. Musser.
	Conference  continued in the Salt Lake
Assembly Hall, Monday, October 9, com-
mencing at 2 p.m., President Bathsheba
W. Smith presiding. Singing. (Sister
Lizzie Thomas Edward having gracious-
consented to take charge of the singing.)
"Come let us anew," prayer by Sister
Mary A. C. Lambert, singing "Redeemer
of Israel."
	President B. W. Smiith, in her opening
remarks, welcomed all most cordially,
felt it a great pleasure to see the large
congregation of sisters, wished she could
take every one by the hand, but that
would take too long; she felt a great love
for them, and a desire to know each one
personally; spoke of her feelings when-
ever she heard the hymn, "Redeemer of
Israel," it reminded her of the time when
they were wandering on the desert land,
she and her sister, they had been sent
from the state, and felt indeed they were
wanderers and in sorrow, for the Prophet
Joseph was in prison, and no one knew
what would transpire—it was a time of
sadness. "I feel to bless you all, and say
Peace be unto you and in your homes,
and may we all prove faithful and true."
	President Annie Wells Cannon report-
ed the Relief Society of the Pioneer stake
for the first time, though they were or-
ganized a year ago last April, spoke of of
ficers' meetings being well attended,
thought President M. I. Horne and her
co-laborers had left the branches in such
order, that it had been comparatively easy
for those coming after them to take hold

[Column 2]
and carry on the work; thought the ward
conferences were a great benefit in get-
ting the society work before the people,
hoped that every woman in Zion would
have her name enrolled, "because it is a
roll of honor;" prayed we might increase
in numbers and have the blessing of the
Lord to help us in our labors.
	President Margaret A. Romney felt it
a great honor to represent the Ensign
stake, for it was in a thriving condition.
She used to think the 20th ward was the
best society, now she thought the seven
branches were all the best; they had
grand lectures on mother's work. Sister
Romney spoke highly of all the work in
the stake and of the help and support the
Relief society was to the Bishops, and
the appreciation they expressed of its
benefits.
	Solo by Sister Lizzie Thomas Edward,
"The Divine Redeemer," music by Gounod.
	President Sarah A. Colton was glad to
be present and sorry not to have been
able to attend with us on Thursday; said
she had been called to preside in the
stake a year ago last May, but had been
President in Vernal ward sixteen years;
had a desire to assist in the upbuilding of
the society and to minister to the dis-
tressed and those bowed down with sor-
row and trials. They were reading chap-
ters from the Bible and giving Book of
Mormon lectures in the society meetings,
the sisters asking questions; they had tes-
timony bearing, and were striving to
make some progress; she was interested
in saving grain, thought some had been
wasted because of not having places to
keep it secure.
	President Alice J. Call, Star Valiey
stake, said in that stake they were in a
very scattered condition, some sisters
walk two and three and some six miles
to attend meetings. The attendance did
not average half the membership, but she
had great charity for those who did not
attend, because she knew the circum-
stances; it was not a grain country, the
wheat was often frozen before it was ripe,
and generally speaking the people had to
buy their flour; the Bishops all realize
the benefits of the Relief Society.
	President Annie S. Musser, of Liberty
stake, gave an excellent report of the
work in that stake, and how much the
sisters enjoyed their labors, praised the
teachers, and spoke enthusiastically of
the work of the Church in general, and
blessed the presiding sisters and all who
were laboring in the glorious work of the
Lord in these latter days; rejoiced in all
that was being done for the upbuilding of
Zion in the earth.
	Sister Emily J. Anderson, late president
of Tooels stake, reported the affairs of
the Relief Society there as being in good
condition; she had visited around the
stake recently; a number of quilts had
been made and sold to good advantage,
and some given to the poor, the money
used to build with of those sold, felt the
society was doing a good work.
	Sister Rebecca E. Little gave an in-
structive lecture on the "Life of Christ,"
which we may hereafter publish in brief
if not in complete form.
	President B. W. Smith, at the close of
Sister Little's lecture, related an incident
in her own life. She said once when she
was real sick, the Prophet Joseph came
to see her; she was too bashful to ask
him to administer to her, but as he left

[Column 3]
he laid his hand on her head and she was
healed and got up well.
	Dr. Romania B. Penrose spoke upon
"Loyalty." Said to her the word had a
golden sound, felt that it is a great thing
to be true; in the family loyalty should
stand guard and each one true to the
other, and not allow the failings in the
home to go out. Referred to the course
pursued by some mothers of telling their
boys' bad actions; felt it was a bad prac-
tice; the boy's faults should be kindly re-
proved aside, not corrected before others.
In our Relief Society we should be true
and sustain each other, and be loyal. I
sometimes wonder if we are as loyal as
we ought to be to each other. It is our
bounden duty to pray earnestly for our
beloved president, Sister Bathsheba W.
Smith, and her counselors, and all who
work with her. It [-]s a pleasure to listen
to those who can stand up and say, I
knew the Prophet Joseph Smith, and we
hope Sister Smith may live many years
to tell us of him, for he was a prophet
and a wonder. Now we are all filled with
loyalty to him and the work he estab-
lished. Think of the sorrow of the
Prophet Joseph Smith when he looked
among those who should have been his
friends and knew they were false. I am
sure it would have melted a heart of stone
to have listened to President Joseph F.
Smith yesterday at the close of the Con-
ference, when we had a baptism of bless-
ing from his mouth," closed by exhorting
all to be faithful and true.
	Counselor Annie T. Hyde felt she owed
an apology for coming in late, but she
had been talking with strangers at the
Bureau of Information; said she had al-
ways attended the Conferences of the
Church ever since the days of "the bow-
ery," but had never attended a confer-
ence like the one yesterday. Surely
President Smith's testimony showed the
power of the Lord; thought no trials
were counted too great by her parents.
Let us teach our children the Gospel and
to attend to all the duties, and let us be
at our post always, and see that good men
are elected to govern, and use all our op-
portunities, and try to be a benefit; strive
to remember the blessings that come to
us through faith.
	"The spirit of God like a fire is burn-
ing" was sung by the congregation, led
by Sister Lizzie Thomas Edward. Ben-
ediction by Sister Annie C. Woodbury.
	Conference adjourned until seven
o'clock at the same place.

		(To be continued.)
		EMERY STAKE.
	The Relief Society conference of the
Emery stake of Zion was held at Price,
October 31, 1905. The stake officers
were all present, also President John H.
Pace and Counselor Henry G. Mathis.
The conference began at 10 a.m., Presi-
dent Mary A. Sorenson presiding. Sing-
ing' "How Firm a Foundation." Open-
ing prayer was offered by President John
H. Pace.
	President Mary A. Sorenson welcomed
all and gave a short report of the Relief
society of Emery stake.
	President Henrietta Hill reported the
Wellington society. A vocal solo, "Lord
Thou Wilt Hear Me When I Pray," was
rendered by Sister Nellie Wilson of Price.
	A lecture on "Infancy," from Mother's


Plan Work, was ably and interestingly
given by Sister Lucinda C. Curtis, of Or-
angeville. A report of the Castle Gate
society was given by President Elizabeth
Young, who also gave some of her early
experiences in the Church.
	President Henry G. Mathis spoke of
Relief society work in general and the
good that is being done by the same.
Singing, "Praise to the Man who Com-
muned with Jehovah." Benediction by
Secretary Maria Allen.
	Afternoon session at 2 p.m.  Singing,
"Redeemer of Israel." Invocation by
Sister Anna Miller. Singing, etc. Roll
called and seven Relief society branches
represented. Secretary Allen read the
minutes of the previous conference, also
the stake financial report for the year
ending Dec. 31, 1904, which were unani-
mously accepted.
	Instrumental music rendered by Sister
Violet Flack of Price.
	Stake Counselor Mary J. King spoke
on the duties of officers and members of
the Relief society. President Rhoda
E. Robertson reported the Orangeville
society. A vocal solo was rendered by
Sister Griffiths of Castle Gate. Counselor
Maria Guymon spoke on a mother's du-
ties to her family, and keeping the Sab-
bath day holy. Elder  Robert Green-
wood, of Price, on missionaries and Re-
lief society work. Elder H. E. Curtis, of
Orangeville, on the raising and training
of children.
	Secretary Maria Allen spoke of the
General Conference and visiting the Dr.
Groves' L, D. S. hospital.
	President John H. Pace spoke on punc-
tuality. 
	Bishop Ernest S. Horsley, of Price, ad-
vised all to teach children to remember
the important events of their lives.
	Stake President Mary A. Sorenson
thanked all present, and especially the
Price sisters, for helping to make the con-
ference a success.
	Singing, "The Spirit of God Like a Fire Is Burning," etc. Benediction by Sister
Isabella Bryner.
	MARIA ALLEN, Secretary.

RELIEF SOCIETY REPORTS.
	ST. GEORGE.
Dear Editor:
	We held our Relief Society conference
June 26, 1904. Apostle Rudger Clawson
and Brother Henry McCune, also the presi-
dency of the stake, Edward Snow and coun-
selors, present, and gave us some good
instructions. President Snow gave us a
great deal of eneouragement, he thought
that once a year we could be represented
in the tabernacle, so you see we are gain-
ing ground. The Lord has the work in
hand; all we have to do is to do right. He
will do the rest. My heart swells with
gratitude to my heavenly Father for His
loving kindness that He has preserved us in
the truth, that He has counted us worthy to
have a name and standing with His people.
I often wonder how many of this people
realize what a blessing it is to them and
their children to live in these peaceful vales.
The trials of life are nothing when we en.
joy the Spirit of the Lord. When we think
of the condition of the world and the little
hope they have for the future.
	Give my kind love to Annie and family,
and accept the same yourself and Sister
Lambert when you see her. I trust you
are well. I know you are busy. May
the Lord bless you with life and health and
every righteous desire of yonr heart is the
wish of your loving sister.
		ANN C. WOODBURY,
President of the Relief Society St. George
		Stake.
	NEBO STAKE.
	The annual conference of the Relief So-
ciety of the Nebo Stake was held in the
Payson tabernacle, President Lylius C. Page
presiding. Visiting sisters present—Mary
Alice C. Lambert and Carrie S. Thomas
from Salt Lake City. Sister Josephine
Peterson reported Payson First ward. Own
a meeting house and had started to build a
granary. Sister Christina Ellworth reported
the Second ward, Payson. Sisters donated
freely, owned granary, had 750 bushels of
wheat. Sister Carrie Holt reported the
First ward society, Spanish Fork. Sister
Pace Second ward, Spanish Fork, reported
that society—owned a meeting house and
have granary nearly completed. Sister
Mary Hallam, Spanish Fork Third ward
society— have about 27 widows, have about
199 bushels of wheat on hand, have donat-
ed $8.00 to the Woman's Building. Sister
Swenson reported Fourth ward society,
Spanish Fork—own meeting house and
granary. Paid $25.00 for Woman's Build-
ing, gathered Sunday eggs to the amount
of $92.00.
	Sister Mary A. Lambert, missionary of
the General Board, was pleased with the
reports given in, and especially those about
the wheat, encouragod the sisters to teach
the children to observe the Sabbath day,
teach them by example as well as precept;
wives should respect their husbands, teach
the children to pay their tithing and honor
their parents. Sister Bathsheba W. Smith
sends her love to the sisters, she is a lovely

[Column 2]
woman; spoke of the blessings received in
working in the Temple.
	Counselor Henry Gardner spoke of the
great benefit of the Relief Society, and en-
couraged the sisters to keep on in the good
work.
	Sister Carrie S. Thomas, member of the
General Board, spoke of the beautiful flow-
ers; but thought they were not half so beau-
tiful as the faces of the sisters; was pleased
with the reports; said how pleased Sister
Smith would be to know how united the
sisters all are.
	Sister Page invited all to stay for lunch.
Benediction by President Page.
	Afternoon session, Sister Page presiding.
After the opening exercises a song was
sung by Esther Coombs. Sister Page called
for more reports. Sister Ferguson reported
the Lake Shore society. The Leland so-
ciety reported by Sister Chisholm—the sis-
ters are all united, it is a new ward. Ben-
jamin society, reported by Sister Bingham
—the sisters are all united, but are so
scattered; own a granary, and are quite
earnest in taking care of the sick. Sister
Ball recited "Somebody's Mother."
	Sister Carrie S. Thomas said she would like
to speak about the nurses' class, which she
did at some length, hoped we would be
able to send some from our stake; read a
letter of instructions from Sister Bathsheba
W. Smith; spoke of woman's work.
	Sister M.A. Lambert spoke of the nurses'
class and the Woman's Building. Said we
should have confidence in our children and
impress upon their minds the value of
prayer; we should be saviors upon mount
Zion to bless every one.
	Sister Lucy Dixon recitsd “At a Stylish
Church.”
	The officers of the General Board and of
the Nebo Stake Relief Society were unan-
imously sustained.
	Stake Counselor Hyrum Lemmon spoke
of the duties of the Relief Society in taking
care of the sick and poor, also the plan of
having nurses to take care of our sick,
should all be united.
	Sister Dixon said the sisters of the Lake
Shore ward had invited us to come there
for our next conference, and we would ad-
journ this conference for three months, to be
held at Lake Shore. Read a sentiment and
urged all to think of the nurses' class, and
try and get some to go and take that course.
	Benediction by Sister Mary Roberts.
		HATTIE WRIDE, Cor. Sec.
	BEAR LAKE.
	The annual conference of the Relief So-
ciety of Bear Lake Stake convened in the
Bloomington meeting house at 2 p.m., July
9, 1904, President Julia P. Lindsay presid-
ing.
	President Lindsay addressed the sisters:
“We have claim upon the blessings of the
Lord. and we shall not be left to ourselves;
though we have not any visitors, we can
hear many good things from our sisters
in Bear Lake: It is necessary to obtain
that which we need for our sustenance, but
we should not allow all our time to be used
in our temporal labors, the spiritual food is


[Column 1]
also necessary for our welfare. God reigns
supreme.” She felt grateful for the pros-
perity that surrounded the Saints.
	Verbal reports as follows: Bloomington,
President S. Patterson; Liberty, President
M. Morgan; Sharon, President E. Miles;
Montpelier, President Agnes Pearce; Ben-
nington, President M.Van Orman; George-
town, Jeanet Thibets; Raymond, President
Martha Hart.
	Counselor Mary Bagley had noted the de-
votion manifested by the presidents. Would
not be deprived of attending Relief Society
if it were possible to attend for all this
world’s goods. The influence felt in asso-
ciating together is a power for good; enu-
merated many of the blessings received by
attending and being energetic in the per-
formance of duties, such as visiting the
sick, administering comfort to those around
us both by word and deed. Had the priv-
lege recently of listening to a sister who
lived in the days of the Prophet Joseph and
who was also a member of the first Relief
Society.
	Sister Sarah A. Allred, of the Stake Pres-
dency of Young Ladies, thought we were
all laboring for the same goal, should be
willing to divide our substance with those
who are less fortunate, charitable to all
God’s children, and pray for all regardless
of color or creed.
	Stake Missionary Elnora Austin felt that
it is good to have a clear conscience; it is a
great satisfaction to feel that we are trying
to keep the laws of God, Felt like trying
to overcome her weaknesses.
	Bishop Alma Findlay spoke on the object
of this probation. There is no reason why
we should not be happy, the Gospel brings
joy to the heart.
	President J. P. Lindsay expressed her
gratitude for the good advice and instruc-
tion given and for the Holy Spirit made
manifest. Prayer by Elder John Bagley.
	Relief Society officers’ meeting, July 10,
1904.
	Verbal reports not given on Saturday
were given: Fish Haven,by President Jessie
Nelson; St. Charles by President Sarah A.
Young; Wardboro and Dingle were report-
ed by President J. P. Lindsay, who with
other stake officers had recently visited, had
also filled vacancies where needed; Ovid,by
President Mary Jensen; Paris, First ward,
by President Jane Stucki; Paris. Second
ward, by President Sarah Ashley; Lanark,
by Counselor Sarah A. Ludlum; Cokeville,
by President J. Lindsay.
	President William Budge was pleased to
see the interest manifested by the attend-
ance. The reports have been interesting.
Should take care of the grain; good grana-
ries should be built. It would not be wis-
dom to donate wheat unless it can be pre-
served for our use in time of need. The
spirit of disobedience and independence is
growing among our children and young
people; they neglect their meetings and
other duties. Felt pleased to offer words of
encouragement to all who are devoted to
this noble work.
	Sunday afternoon, July 10.1904, 2 p. m.
	Meeting was presided over by Bishop
Alma Findlay until after the administration
of the Sacrament.
	President Julia Lindsay then conducted
the meeting and addressed a large congre-
gation in relation to our object in meeting
in conference. She earnestly solicited the
prayers of those present that she might
have wisdom and strength to instruct her
sisters. We have additional work in our

[Column 2]
society—that is, the Mothers Work. Asked
the young ladies to come and take advan-
tage of these opportunities, they are espec-
ially prepared for those who are raising
children. We can accomplish much good
if we are only united.
	President Nancy E. Pugmire, of the Y.
L. M. I. A. said, ‘‘It is a pleasant thought
to know that we are all true to the Gospel.
If we endure to the end we will be saved;
we should try and get all the sunshine into
our souls that is possible. We will be
known by the company we keep. Thought
that many of the parents were too indulg-
ent; we are ambitious for our children.
There is nothing that will bring us greater
satisfaction than a well spent life.
	Sister Elnora Austin said the Relief So-
ciety has a work among the young people
in trying to keep them in subjection to the
proper influences and not allow them to run
at large at all hours of the night.
	Sister Amelia Hoge endorsed the re-
marks made upon the government of our
children. Mothers should instruct their
daughters to become good wives and moth-
ers, that they may understand the rules
and regulations of a model home.
	Bishop W. W. Clark felt like supporting
the organization; they are indeed filling
their mission as a Relief Society. Missed
from our midst Sister E. Hart.  Pleased to
see sisters uncover their heads in religious
services. Many other valuable remarks
were given.
	Sister Mary Bagley said we should look
after those who are weak in the faith.
Jesus came to heal the sick. It is our duty
to look after those who are sick in body,
also in spirit, and comfort the distressed by
kind and encouraging words.
	Closing prayer by Bishop Alma Findlay.
LOUISE TUELLER, Cor. Secretary-
	PIONEER STAKE.
The first quarterly conference of the
Pioneer Stake Relief Society was held on
Jnne 9th, in the Seventh Ward Assembly
rooms, President Annie Wells Cannon pre-
siding. Commenced at 10 a.m. There
were present on the stand besides the reg-
ular stake officers, Sisters Phebe Y. Beatie
and Carrie S. Thomas of the General
Board and President Annie S. Musser and
counselors of Liberty Stake, President Mar-
garet Romney of Ensign Stake, Sister Mary
Alice Lambert and others. There was a
fine attendance of members and a splendid
spirit prevailed.
	After the opening exercises, the ward
presidents reported the condition of the
several branches of the stake, and then fol-
lowed some interesting remarks by Sister
Beatie, pertaining principally to the Relief
Society nurse work. She gave much valu-
able instruction in regard to the selecting
of young women for this work and also as
to the care of them by friends, in homes
when they come from outside settlements.
	Sister Carrie S. Thomas spoke on the
same subject, and referred to the charges of
nurses. Said she knew “the laborer was
worthy of his hire,” still the Latter-day
Saint women must have care, and the
nurse ought not to expect more than
the husband’s wage's for her services; that
she thought there should be a reasonable
arrangement made concerning the prices
for nurses. Spoke interestingly on the
mother's work and said by Relief Society
work we associated ourselves with the very
best women, and always received fine op-
portunities of mingling where the Spirit of

[Column 3]
the Lord abounded.
	President Musser addressed the meeting,
and spoke quite interestingly about her own
stake work and her newly aroused interest
in Relief Society work.
	President Annie W. Cannon urged the
sisters to heed the good counsel given and
spoke especially about the progress of the
Relief Society work, and how we kept on
reaching after new ideas and introducing
new lines of work; that the women of Zion
were always bringing forth new interests,
and that our work was given us through
inspiration, even as we were organized by
inspiration; that when President Brigham
Young called Sister Romania B. Pratt to go
away and study medicine, it was the open-
ing of a new era for our women. He saw
the great necessity among the people for
intelligent women doctors, and he placed
this mission upon her, to prepare herself
and to teach others, that there might be less
suffering and sorrow among the women of
Zion, and now we not only have women
physicians, but trained nurses throughout
the stakes of Zion. It is not in the City
where the want of good, intelligent care is
felt so much as in the outlying settlements,
and we must open our homes to these girls
who come here to learn nursing for the
benefit of our sisters.
	After singing and prayer, the meeting
took a recess until two o’clock.
	At two in the afternoon the sisters re-
assembled, and the hall was filled. On the
stand, beside the stake officers, were Pres-
dent McLachlan and Counselers Sylvester
Q. Cannon and Charles H. Hyde of Pioneer
Stake, and we were also honored by the
presence of the General President of the
Relief Society, Bathsheba W. Smith, and
the General Secretary, Emmeline B. Wells,
also Sister Julina L. Smith of the General
Board, besides Presidents Musser and Rom-
ney, Sister Thomas and others.
	After the opening exercises, SisterCannon
introduced Sister Bathsheba W, Smith, the
General President, snd the only person liv-
ing who was present at the organization of
the Relief Society by the Prophet Joseph
Smith. President Smith spoke briefly of her
great interest and devotion to Relief Society
and Temple work, and read some instruc-
tions given by the Prophet Joseph to the
Saints. She bore a strong testimony of the
truth of the Gospel.
	Sister Julina Smith followed and spoke
about raising our children in the princi-
pses of the Gospel, and the great help we
had now through the Primary, Sunday
School and Improvement work.
	Sister Emmeline B. Wells then spoke at
some length about Relief Society work in
its different and interesting branches. Spoke
of the great good the sisters had done;
related some reminiscences of Kirtland and
Nauvoo. Spoke oi the Relief Society as
the first auxiliary organization in the
Church, therefore standing next to the
priesthood.
	Miss Ruth Dahlquist then sang a solo,
after which President McLachlan spoke to
the sisters very interestingly indeed, nnd
gave excellent advice about attending their
meetings punctually and regularly, and
said he fully realized the great benefit of
the Relief Society organizations. He was
followed by Counselor Sylvester Q. Can-
non, who also made interesting and instruc-
tive remarks.
	The names of the General Officers of the
Relief Society and the Stake Officers were


then presented to the meeting and sus-
tained, and six missionaries were appointed
to assist the stake officers and sustained.
	After singing and prayer, the conference
adjourned until September 9, 1904.
	CLARA LITTLE CLAWSON, Secretary.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
']

['[WomansExponent | Vol24 | 1895 | September | Issue: 7 | Title: REFLECTIONS AND MEMORIES. | Author: Unknown]

REFLECTIONS AND MEMORIES.
[A Tribute of Love.]
TO MRS. MARY A. FREEZE.
How strange a thing is life!
	How the years come and go,
Bearing us onward forever,
	Now swift and now slow;
Bringing changes—oft sorrow
	On some bright "To Morrow."
Yet the stream bears us swiftly 
	Along the strange way,
Each morn changed to even,
	Each night follows day;
'Till at last we find peace
Where all quarrels cease.

O, Watchman what of the Night?
	Do you keep the bell tolling,
To tell of fierce breakers
	Where wild waves are rolling?
Yet hark! From far o'er the sea
Sweet music is wafting ever to me.

And so the tide flows gently on,
	Now tears, now laughter on the way;
The fair young face soon changed and worn,
	With sorrow set e'er early day;
Sweet faded faces oft we meet,
And silently each other greet.

The journey long must needs have end,
	The mile-stones close and closer grow;
Yet each brings back some memory dear
	Of those we loved so long ago:
Tho' from our lives they've passed away
To dwell in realms of endless day.

O, dream awhile, sweet friend of mine!
	Bid memory all her treasures bring!
May those so loved of old by thee
	Press near, and loud thy praises sing;
For Zion's daughters love thee well,
And high and loud the echoes swell.

When Zion shall with joy be crowned
	With the shining ones may you be found;
May each fond wish you have in life.
	Your full heart know—while joys abound,
The Master smiling on thy face,
A faded look will never trace.

All things are theirs who win the crown,
	All heights all depths, to them are known;
The Savior's love will dry all tears,
	Of those He's chosen for His own;
Dear one, His love is strong and deep,
Whom thus He loves, no more need weep.

Now flowers we bring of fragrance rare,
	And crown thee Queen this happy night;
Glad music swells the perfumed air,
	Thy friends all greet thee with delight,
May white winged Peace, her pinions spread
	And choicest blessings crown thy head.
June, 1895.	LYDIA A. ALDER.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol21 | 1892 | August | Issue: 4 | Title: THE WORLD OF THE HEART. | Author: Unknown]

THE WORLD OF THE HEART.

"What is the world to thy heart, O, boy
	With the glowing, earnest eyes?"
"'Tis a broad fair field my strength to prove
	Where fame shall be my prize,
Where I shall battle unstained with wrong;
	Shall strengthen the cause that's pure,
'Till my deeds shall live in the poet's song
While men and books endure."

	And then I asked of the aged man:
		"O what is this world to thee?"
He said: "My world is not very large,
	The past has faded from me;
But my heart leans out to where, far away
	My sons and my daughters be,
With their flocks and herds on the mountain
		sides,
	Their homes in the vale below,
My heart would rest in their happy midst—
	It is there that I long to go.
I walk alone in the city's streets,
	O, what is the world to me!
When silence reigns in the empty home—
	My own, are the world to me."

And then I asked of a mother pale:
	"How wide is the world to thee?"

[Column 2]

She said: "One child lies buried where
	The oaks of Old England grow,
And an ancient castle threw its spell
	Where oft we have walked below;
And one lies rocked by ocean waves,
	Far down in its waters deep,
And one sleeps where, by desert road
	No stone doth a record keep;
Aud I am here, and those three graves
	That lie in the land and sea,
Have measured by their dear links, the length
	Of my heart's sad world, to me."

And then I asked of a lady bright
	With youth and with heart full free;
"O, tell me what thy world is like
	And what it doth hold for thee?"
"O, my world is very wide and fair,
	For my Captain lover goes
With his three good ships, where'er he choose
	And all the world he knows;
And I am with him in heart and song
	Wherever he guides his sails,
'Mid the balmy isles of the Orient
	Or the far North's chilling gales.
And the world of my heart is where he is,
	It is bright where the spices blow;
And oh! 'tis brave where he guides his ships
	Through the seas of ice and snow."

And then I asked one happier still:
	"How large is thy world to thee?"
"Not very large, but it holds enough
	That others might smile for me;
It holds not wealth or honors high
	But no happier hearts can beat,
We are king and queen in our own cot
	With our babe so fair and sweet;
'Tis a fair, bright world all peace and joy,
	With its blessings for us three,
And the future holds no thought of fear
	In my heart's world for me."

And last I asked of the sorrowing:
	"Like what is the world to thee?"
For thine arms are empty, thine eyes are sad,
	Come share thy sorrow with me."
She said: "As the heart, so is the world,
	In youth, or when it is old.
God, who gave the whole earth, gave to each
	A world of its own to mold.
Where the toiler heeded the still; small voice,
	For him the path was clear;
Where the pilgrim heeded his own heart's calls
	It hath led where the way was drear.
And ever ascending to the skies
	The cry of the creature pleads;
"Look Thou and help us, behold our work
	Our weakness and all our needs!"
And he sees the spirit amid the wreck
	Of human idols brought unto dust,
Ashes of roses' where the heart
	Had in the mortal placed its trust.
For my empty arms, my heart is full,
	Mine eyes through sadness see
One, only, who can my sorrows bear
	And walks unseen with me.
Some see but joy and know but half,
	Like flowers that bloom and die;
But souls that are made for usefulness
	Must stand where the storms go by.
To gain and yield, to endure, not mourn,
	No idol twixt God and thee—
To hope and wait for bliss, where? Beyond!
	This, friend, is the world to me.
			AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.


', "[WomansExponent | Vol25 | 1896 | September | Issue: 6 | Title: THOUGHTS, | Author: L. D. A]

THOUGHTS,

On the death of Mrs. Ellen C. Clawson.
SING, dear one sing, and shout for very joy!
In the world above the stars, where strife cannot
	annoy;
The incense from this lower world, of work so 
	nobly done,
Will rise and bless thee ever, while countless
	ages come. 
Thy heart so full of heavenly joy, shall now be 
	satisfied;
And in His presence thou wilt bask, who for us
	bled and died;
And tho’ we drop the tear of love and sigh that 
	thou art gone,
Yet joy is bubbling uppermost, that thou a crown
	hast won. 
I think of how you humbly stood, and of the 
	Master spoke,—
The echoes in the listeners’ ears, such rapturous
	strains awoke—
The light of angels hovering near, thy trembling
	form embraced,
And “Well beloved of the Lord,” was on each 
	feature traced.
Forget thee? No! Though all thy friends again 
	may meet;
We’ll miss thy saintly face, thy presence calm
	and sweet;
And a sigh of love will rise, from every faithful
	heart,
And a tear of sweet remembrance, from saddened
	eyes will start. 
But O, for thee a glorious day has been revealed
 	to view;
Where Cherubim and Seraphim, and other 
	glories new.
Will greet thy stately entrance, where all the 
	Saints will meet,
As the fragrance rare of flowers most fair, arise
	beneath thy feet.
‘‘Well done!” Come where the rippling waters
	softly flow along;
Where on the ambient air, in triumph sounds the
	victor’s song;
And hear the blessed music, thy heart has 
	yearned to know,
While wandering thro' the darkness, that besets
	us here below. 
Faithful unto death! Thy Covenant vows un-
	broke;
And singing in thy heart the song the Gospel
	sound awoke;

[Column 2]
O, who would shrink the burden, when the crown
	is sure to come;
‘Who bears the cross, will wear the crown,” the
	prophets all have sung. 
And peace doth come when the journey’s o’er—
The warrior must not rest when the battle is be-
	fore!—
But when the victory’s won, the ease is sure to 
	come;
As a well of lasting pleasure are the welcome 
	words ‘‘Well done.”
So, tolling bells ring out a joyous sound!
And tell the gathered Saints who weeping stand
	around,
’Tis well with her, though the world is closed to
	view,
For she has fought the fight, to God has proven
	true. 
Brave hearts, true hearts, who walk the way in
	tears,
And only trace the journey by the slowly, chang-
	ing years,
Be comforted! though strife and sorrow too, be 
	rife;
Hear thro’ the gloom and darkness, “I am the
	Resurrection and the Life.” 
Dear one adieu! adieu! But O, ’twill not be long,
Till we who mourn thee now, must learn the 
	changing song;
For fairer in the East dawns the Resurrection
	morn—
The grave will yield its sleepers up, and of its 
	power be shorn. 
Then in thy snowy robes, gems sparkling on thy
	brow,—
The Past beneath thy feet, and only pleasure
	now,—
Wilt in thy shining mansion, that ever will be
	home,
Throw open wide the portals, and bid thy loved
	ones “Come.”
					LYDIA D. ALDER.
August 25, 1896.
	*At a gathering of the sisters in the home of
the writer on July 2, 1896, while speaking Sister 
Clawson said, “I sing in my heart all the time,
and sometimes I could shout for very joy I feel so
happy; but I never was a singer.”
	On resuming her seat, Sister Zina D. H. Young,
the venerable president of the Relief Society who 
presided at the meeting, uttered the follow-
ing prophetic words, “Sister Clawson, you shall 
sing until your heart is satisfied; you shall 
be one of the sweetest singers when you go beyond
the Veil.”
								L. D. A.


[Column 1]
"]
