['[WomansExponent | Vol27 | 1898 | March | Issue: 20 | Title: MARGARET CHERRY. | Author: Unknown]

MARGARET CHERRY.
	In memory of Mrs. Margaret Cherry,
who with her husband and six children
came to Utah in 1847 from Kentucky,
where she was born February 11, 1811.
They moved in the summer of 1848 to
Centerville, where she has lived ever since
until her death, which occurred October 18,
1898.
	Sister Cherry was a widow for thirty-
four years. When the Relief Society was
organized she became a member and officer
of that society and opened her house for
them to meet in. She afterwards gave a
small house to the society which they met
in for twenty years. As age and infirmities
came on her she was unable to leave her
home for a number of years. She was of a
peaceful disposition, a good neighbor and
endeavored to live her religion and keep
the golden rule. She died in full faith and
left a large posterity, of whom nearly all
are in the Church.
			MARY ROCKWOOD.
Centerville Relief Society.
KATE LAUGHAN LEISHMAN.
	We have been called upon to record the
death of one of our beloved sisters, Kate
Maughan Leishman, who departed this life at
her home in Wellsville, Cache Co., Utah,
Jan 15, 1899.
	Sister Leishman has been a faithful work-
er in our Relief Society, and has endeared
herself to the little band of sisters in this
branch by her sweet and gentle disposition,
and we her sisters offer these resolutions of
respect as a slight token of our love for her
who has been called to a higher sphere of
action.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Resolved, That as God in His infinite
wisdom has seen fit to call home our dear
sister, Kate Maughan Leishman, we the
officers and members of the Baker City
branch of the Relief Society, express our
heartfelt sympathy for the bereaved hus-
band and family in their loss; also that they
have been greatly blessed with her sweet
and gentle influence in her home; and be it
further
	Resolved, That these resolutions be
placed upon our society records, one sent to
the bereaved family and one sent to the
Woman’s Exponent for publication.
			REBECCA NIBLEY, Pres.
			NELLIE W. ROUNDY, Sec.
A SAD BEREAVEMENT.
	Sister Elizabeth Pehrson, beloved daugh-
ter of Eric John and Anna S. Pehrson,
died January 15, 1899, after a lingering
weakness for some time, but was not con-
fined to her bed till one week prior to her
death, when she was attacked with an in-
termittent fever. Her weakness became
greater, though she took nourishment till
the last. She passed away without a strug-
gle Sunday morning between five and six
o’clock, in the arms of her beloved mother
and sister.
	Sister Pehrson was born and raised in
the Gospel and died a faithful Latter day
Saint, placing her religion above every
thing else. She was honest, virtuous, kind
and patient, never refusing any call that
was made upon her, therefore holding
many responsible positions at her death.

[Column 2]
	She was a teacher in the Sabbath School,
secretary in the Relief Society, second
counselor in the Y. L. M. I. A., and secre-
tary in the Y. M. and Y. L. Improvement
Association; also held the position of school
trustee and postmistress. She was born
April 28, 1873, at Vernon, Tooele Co., Utah.
	On Friday 20, her angel like form was
borne in a beautiful white casket decorated
with wreaths and blooming roses, to the
meeting house where the funeral services
were held. The stand was draped in white
and decorated with flowers, giving the
whole a beautiful appearance. The choir
sang “Guide Me to Thee.” Prayer was
offered by Brother David Bennion. Sing-
ing, “Wanted on the Other Side.” Consol-
ing remarks were made by Stake President
H. S. Gowan and our beloved Bishop J. C.
Sharp. The choir sang “Sister Thou
wert Mild and Lovely.” Benediction by
Neils Fredrickson.
	The remains were conveyed to the ceme-
tery in a light vehicle drawn by white
horses. The dedicatory prayer was offered
by President H. S. Gowan. The choir
sang “There is Sweet Rest in Heaven.”
The body was then carefully sheltered by
mother earth to rest in peace.
							C.
FRISTENCE MAGDALENE ERIKSEN.
	It is our painful duty to announce that
on December 4, 1898, occurred the death of
Sister Fristence Magdalene Eriksen from
heart failure.
	Deceased was born in Otostrup, Wolstrup
Sogn, Denmark, on the 11th of March 1857.
She was baptized into the Church of Jesus
Christ of Latter-day Saints in 1880 and
emigrated to Utah in 1881. She was a
member of the Newton ward, Cache Stake
of Zion. She died in full faith of the Gos-
pel and with the hope of a glorious resur-
rection. She was a member of the Relief
Society and always took an active part in it.
	The following resolutions were adopted
by the society in her memory:
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Whereas in the providence of God it
has pleased Him to call away this our be-
loved sister to a higher sphere, and we
reverence Him who holds the destinies of
men and who permits nothing to come to
pass but for a wise and glorious purpose; and
	Whereas at the time of her death she
was an active member of the Relief Society
and was held in high esteem by all who
knew her, for her noble qualities, therefore
be it
	Resolved, That we the members of the
Relief Society of Newton ward tender to
the bereaved family our heartfelt sympathy
and condolence for the sad loss they have
sustained of a faithful wife and a loving
sister; and be it further
	Resolved, That a copy of this preamble
and resolutions be recorded in the minutes
of the Relief Society, and a copy printed in
the Woman’s Exponent.
		ELIZABETH FUNK,
		MARY JENKINS,
		MAGDALENE PETERSON,
						Committee.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT AND 
CONDOLENCE. 
	In loving respect to Sister Anna Lang-
hurst Layne, one of our pioneer women
and a faithful teacher in the Cardston Re-
lief Society, Canada.

[Column 3]
	She was born February 22, 1849, at
Stow on the Woods, Glostershire, England.
Died December 23, 1898, in Cardston after
an illness of two days, leaving a husband
and ten children to mourn her departure.
	Sister Layne came to Cardston with her
husband and family in the year 1887. She
was true to her religion and her covenants,
having embraced the higher order of mar-
riage for eternity she has now a claim to a
higher celestial glory. She was always an
energetic and faithful worker in the Relief
Society, a sympathetic and faithful nurse,
ever ready to assist the sick and the poor
and to cheer up the downcast; a loving
wife, a devoted mother, looking after her
children with a love unceasing. We feel
that her wise counsel will be heeded by her
children, (the youngest of whom is eight
years of age) that they will profit by her
worthy example.
	Whereas our Heavenly Father in His
infinite wisdom has seen fit to call home
our beloved sister, Anna Layne,
	Resolved, That in the death of Sister
Layne we are deprived of the society of a
loving sister and a dear friend, one who
was always alive to her duties and will be
greatly missed; so willing in the hour of
need, many sad hearts have been brightened
by her cheerful words of encouragement;
therefore, be it
	Resolved, That we sincerely sympathize
with those who are bereft and are bound by
the nearest and dearest ties, and we share
with them the hope of a happy reunion in
the bright hereafter. We pray that heaven’s
choicest blessings may attend the bereaved
family, and the spirit of the Lord be with
them to comfort and cheer them.
	Resolved, That a copy of these resolutions
be given to the bereaved family, one placed
on record and one sent to the Woman’s
Exponent for publication.
			SARAH B. DAINES, Pres.
			C. ELNORA WIGHT, Sec.
			LOTTIE BARTON, Rec. Sec.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol35 | 1906 | July | Issue: 2 | Title: In Memoriam | Author: WOMAN’S EXPONENT]

IN MEMORIAM.
	I send you a brief sketch of the life of Sister
Elizabeth Brook Fox, who passed away April 9,
1906:
	Sister Fox was born at Leeds, Yorkshire, Eng-
land, January 5, 1825; was baptized into the
Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints in
March, 1851. On April 23, 1861, she sailed for
America in the ship Underwriter; she arrived at
Florence June 2nd, and at Salt Lake City on Sep-
tember 12, 1861; she then moved to Lehi, where
she married Elder Charles W. Fox, February,
1862. In November she with her husband moved
to Franklin.
	June 2nd, 1874, Sister Fox was set apart as pres-
ident of the Franklin Relief Society and served
in that capacity for ten years.
	When the Oneida Stake was organizsd she was
set apart as Stake President of the Relief Society;
she labored faithfully in that office for 16 years,
when failing health compelled her resignation.
	During the time she was president she and her
counselors, Sisters Mary Hawks nnd Anne Hale,

[Column 2]
traveled many miles visiting the branches in the
stake which at that time was a very large one.
	She was of a mild and loving disposition; her life
was full of good works and her many virtues en-
deared her to all who knew her.
	She was a noble woman and and the fruits of
her labors can be seen throughout the Oneida
Stake.
	Sister Fox was the mother of t[-]o children, one
son and one daughter, 17 grandchildren and 10
great grandchi dr[-]n.
	The funeral services were held Wednesday,
April 11 under the direction of Counselor Thomas
Durant. The remains were conveyed in a hearse
from the residence of her daughter, Mrs. L. L.
Hatch, to the me[-]ting-house, followed by the
family and members of the Relief Society, under
the direction of the president, Sister Annie
Challis.
	Services opened by the choir singing, "O my
Father;" prayer by I. B. Nash singing, "O grave,
where is thy victory?" Speakers were L. H. Hatch
of Logan and H. E. Hatch; solo by I. B. Nash,
"Shall we meet beyond the river?" speakers were
C. Go sland, President Geo. C. Parkinson, S R,
Parkinson and Thomas Durrant, all addressed
words of consolation to the bereaved family. Ben-
ediction by E. Buckley,
	The floral offerings were numerous and beauti-
ful. The grave was dedicated by L. H. Hatch,
ANNIE CHALLIS,
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Therefore it is resolved by the sisters of the
Franklin Relief Society,
	That we mourn the loss of our honored presi-
dent; but that we appreciate the goodness of her
life and the worthy example she has set us to fol-
low.
	Resolved that we extend to the bereaved our
sympathy, and pray God to comfort them, and
lead them to know what is their loss, is the gain of
those that were taken from them.
	Be it further resolved that a copy of these reso-
lutions be inscribed upon the records of the Soci-
ety and also published in the WOMAN'S EXPO-
NENT.
PRESIDENT ANNIE CHALLIS.
Franklin, Idaho.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	In memory of Mary I. Loveland of Deweyville,
Utah, who departed this life January 12th, 1906.
Deceased was born March 27th, 1861, she was the
daughter of Miles V. and Margaret M. Gardener,
and wife of Orson C Loveland. The deceased
was the mother of 10 children 9 girls and one boy.
	The officers of the Deweyville R. S. offer the
following resolutions:
	Whereas, our much esteemed sister and colabor-
er has by the hand of divine providence been re-
moved fr m our midst, and has, we feel confident-
ly assured gone to a better world, we her sisters,
who know of her loving deeds and appreciate her
many noble qualities, desire to express our tender
sympathy for the bereaved family and to acknowl-
edge her labors and ministrations of kindness as a
member of the R. S., and also as a friend and
neighbor in the best sense of the word. There-
fore be it
	Resolved we do humbly testify that charity and
humility were leading traits of her character, and
that she was a loving wife and mother; and be
it,
	Resolved that we, the sisters of the society of
which she was a member so long, may follow her
noble example of diligence and faithfulness,
	Resolved, that these resolutions be recorded in
our ward record, also to be printed in the Woman's
Exponent, and copies sent to the family.
	Hannah E. Loveland, President; Martha M.
Lish, Sec.; Bashua A. Chapman, Asst. Sec.
	In behalf of the Deweyville R. S.
SISTER ANNA M FOLKMAN.
	Sister Folkman passed away from this sphere of
action, March 26, 1906, after six weeks illne[-]s.
She was 84 years old the 5th of last February She
died as she had lived a faithful Latter-day Saint;
beloved by all who knew her, surrounded by her
husband and family and kind and loving friends,
her last words being "Be united." Until her last
sickness she was able to do her own work and take
care of her husband.
	Sister Anna Maria Peterson, was the daughter
of Jence C. and Anna C. Peterson, was born Feb.
5, 1852, in the city of Rands, Jydlund, Denmark;
her mother died when she was five years old and
when she was 12 her father died, she had one
brother younger than herself; they were then left
to themselves. She passed through many hard
trials, and much severe sickness. She was mar-
ried to J. G. Folkman, in the spring of April 26,

[Column 3]
1848, was converted to the Gospel after having
heard it preached three times. She with her hus-
band was baptized Dec. 5, 1851. Emigrated in
1853, came to New Orleans in a sailing vessel, then
up the Mississippi to St. Louis She had cholera
twice and was healed each time by the power of
the priesthood. Came across the plains with ox
teams and walked the entire way, arrived in Salt
Lake City Oct. [-], 1854. Brother and Sister [-]olk-
man first settled in Lehi, from there went to Plain
City as pioneers, from there they were among the
first to sett[-]e in Soda Springs, Idaho, and were
among the first Saints to settle in Gentile Valley,
Idaho, in each of these places she held one or
more offices in the R. S. She was teacher and
treasurer in the R. S. when it was first organized
in the latter place, which off[-]e she held as long as
she was able. Not having any children of her
own she took a great interest in helping to raise
her husband's children. This dear sister passed
through much sickness, and all the horrors of the
crusade, which was very trying on her, had small
pox as late as 1900, by which she almost lost her
eyesight. Her faith was very great in the power
of healing by the priesthood, and she was ever
ready to acknowledge the hand of the Lord in all
things. After much suffering she passed peace-
fully away. Her complaint for the last few years
was chronic bronchitis, followed by dropsy.
	The funeral services of Sister Anna M. Folk-
man were held in the new meeting house at
Thatcher Ward, Idaho, March 29, 1906, Bishop
J T. Pond presiding. Singing by the choir,
"When first the glorious light of truth. prayer by
Brother Nelce. Graham. Singing, "Sister, tho[-]
wast mild and lovely." The speakers were Coun-
se[-]ors Geo. C. Peck, Patriarch W. Sterrit, John
B. Thatcher and Christopher Folkman, her Broth-
er-in-law, of Plain City, who gave a sketch of her
early life; they all bore testimony of her excellent
qualities and firm faith in the principles of the
Gospel of Christ, said her doors were ever open
and her table spread for the Saints and Elders and
she was ever on hand to assist the poor and her
motto was to "return good for evil." Spoke of the
glorious resurrection that awaits the faithful,
	Stake President L S. Pond heartily endorsed all
that had been said, spoke of her many good
qualities, said no one had ever done Sister Folk-
man a favor without getting threefold in return.
Spoke of what a glorious meeting it would be when
her husband would be permitted to meet her in the
mansion prepared for the faithful. Bishop J. T.
Pond made some very consoling remarks. Sing-
ing, "Farewell, all earthly honors." Benediction,
Counselor Geo. D. Anderson.
COM.
RENDINA OLSEN.
	Resolutions of respect to Sister Rendina Olsen
who departed this life June 4, 1906.
	Sister Olsen was born in New Hedemarken, Nor-
way, September, 1848; embraced the Gospel in
her childhood and came to Utah in 1866. Sister
Olsen was an active member of the Relief So[-]i[-]ty.
having acted as one of the counselors to the presi-
dent of that association for a great many years,
and we realize that we have lost a dear friend and
efficient co-laborer, and while we mourn the loss
of her society here, we also realize that our loss
is her gain, and she has gone to meet her reward,
far from the storms of mortal life.
	The funeral was held in the Santaquin Ward
meetinghouse, which had been beautifully decor-
ated.
	The services were conducted by Bishop J. M.
Holladay. The speakers paid a beautiful tribute
to her life and character in their remarks, and the
choir sang appropriate hymns.
	The beautiful casket that held the mortal remains
of Sister Olsen was loaded with floral gifts. A
broken wheel was presented by the members of
the society. Her remains were followed to the
cemetery by a large number of carriages. May
she rest in peace.
	Whereas, It has pleased the Great Creator to
take unto Himself our beloved sister who has been
a faithful worker in the Relief Society, never tir-
ing in well doing, a devoted friend to the poor and
a kind and loving wife and mother, therefore
be it
	Resolved, by the sisters of the Santaquin Re-
lief Society that we tender our sincere sympathy
to the bereaved family and relatives, and while
we mourn not as those who have no hope, yet we
realize that we have lost a faithful counselor who
died as she had lived, in the hope of a glorious res-
urrection.
	Resolved, That these resolutions be put upon the
records of the society and be sent to the EXPO-
NENT for publication.
ALICE ELLSWORTH, Sec’y.
Santaquin, July 13, 1906.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

OBITUARY.
SISTER SARAH S. HARDER.
 	Sister Sarah S. Harder died at her residence in
Kamas, Summit Co., October 28, 1900, after a long
and painful illness and severe suffering for five
months.
	She was born in Portsmouth, England, March 3,
1840, and was the daughter of Stephen and Eliza-
beth Biddlecome Spicer, and the wife of [-]illet S.
Harder. She leaves a husband, two married
daughters, seven grandchildren and many friends
to mourn her loss. Two sons preceded her to the
great beyond. She was a faithful member of the
Kamas Relief Society for many years.
	Sister Harder was set apart as assistant secretary
on March 7, 1881, and appointed secretary of the
same society on June 10, 1895, which position she
held until her death. Her Relief Society work
was well and faithfully done and stands to her
honor. She came to Utah in 1863, and crossed the
plains in Rosel Hyde’s company.
		M. L. WOOLSTENHULME, Stake Sec.
SKETCH AND RESOLUTIONS.
	Resolutions adopted by the officers and mem-
bers of the Georgetown Relief Society in honor of,
their beloved sister, Hetta Emeline Welker Dunn,
who departed this life Nov. 17, 1900, at her home
after an illness of many years of lung trouble. 
	Sister Dunn was born in the State of Iowa,


[Column 1]
November 1, 1848. She was a faithful Latter-day
Saint, and as such we mourn her departure. She
was president of the Young Ladies’ Association in
Bloomington (her former home) for ten years, and
second counselor in the Georgetown Relief Society,
which position she filled with honor until her health
failed and she resigned her position as counselor.
	She was of a quiet, retiring disposition, yet
always willing to do her duty. She was the
mother of eight children, three sons and five
daughters, all of whom attended her funeral ex-
cepting one son. who resides in Canada and did
not arrive in time to see his beloved mother, and
drop one tear of sorrow on her marble brow.
	Resolved, That as our Heavenly Father has
taken our beloved sister to her heavenly home,
that we desire to emulate her noble example, and
that we herewith express our heartfelt sympathy
for her bereaved husband and children. Be it
fnrther
	Resolved, That these resolutions be placed on
the record of the society, a copy be sent to the
family and one to the Woman’s Exponent for
publication. 
			SUSAN E. THOMPSON, Pres.
MARY CRAIGG PETERSON.
	Sister Mary Craigg Peterson, who, after an ill-
ness of several months, of dropsy, departed this
life November 11, 1900.
	Sister Peterson was born in Denmark, May 20,
1863. After embracing the Gospel she came to
Zion to mingle with the chosen people of God. She
leaves a husband and four children, three sons and
one daughter the youngest child being a boy six
months old.
	Sister Mary was a faithful worker as a teacher
in the Pleasant Green Ward Relief Society, of Salt
Lake Stake of Zion. She never shrank beneath
the responsibilities of her duties in the cause of
trnth.
	“Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from
henceforth, yea. they rest from their labors and
their works do follow them.”
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Resolved. That as God in His in[--]ite wisdom
saw fit to call our beloved sister to the Great
Beyond, we all share in the grief of her departure
from us, yet joy when we think of her being in
happiness in her heavenly home.
	That we will miss her as a faithful worker and a
dear associate.
	That we present a copy of these resolutions to
her bereaved husband and family, to her mother,
one to the WOMAN’S EXPONENT for publication and
one be placed on file in the Relief Society record
book.
					ALVIRA S. HIRST, Pres.
ANNIE LAZENBY.
	Sister Annie Lazenby died at her home in Loa,
Utah, October 29, 1900. She was the daughter of
[-]ohn Tether and Phebe Oads Tether. She was
born at Thorn, near Doncaster, England, June
30, 1845, joined the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints. June 1866, was baptized by
Elder Fulmer and came to Utah in 1868 on the
good ship Constitution. She crossed the plains
from the Platte River, walking most of the way.
There were sixty wagons drawn by oxen. Cap-
tain John Gallispie’s company arrived in Salt Lake
City in September, where she took a room near
Brother Betson Eardley in the seventh Ward.
	She was married to John T. Lazenby by D. H.
Wells in the Endowment House, March 17, 1869,
and went with her husband to the railroad at the
mouth of Weber canyon on J. W, Young’s con-
tract. They moved to Pleasant Grove June 1869,
and to Minersville Nov. 1869, and came to Fre-
mont Valley Nov. 1878, and located at Loa, which
was then a part of Grass Valley Ward, the in-
habitants at that time being mostly Indians.
	She was a faithful Latter-day Saint, was the
mother of three sons and six daughters. One son
and one daughter preceded her to the better land.
She leaves a husband and two sons (one of whom
is now on a mission to South Carolina) and five
daughters to revere her memory.
	She was a teacher in the Loa Relief Society ever
since its organization until her health was so poor
she could not go as a teacher any longer, but was
always a faithful, energetic woman. She was an
invalid for about five years before her death.
					M. J. TAYLOR, Pres.
ANNA CHRISTINE MARIA PALLISEN JANSEN.
	Sister Anna Christine Maria Pallisen Jansen,
widow of the late A. A. Jansen, of the Third Ward
of Brigham City, died Monday, September 17, at
9:15 p. m., of dropsy, after a lingering illness.

[Column 2]
	She was born October 27, 1838, in Norie, Konges-
lov, Niborg, Amt, Denmark, and joined the Latter-
day Saints when eighteen years of age. She came
to Utah in 1858, settled in Brigham City in 1859.
	She was the mother of four children, two
daughters surviving her.
	She was called and acted as teacher in the Re-
lief Society in 1876 and on March 13, 1878, was
called as second counselor to Mary Stark, which
position she held until she was called to preside
over the Relief Society of the Third Ward, Feb.
25, 1896, which position she held with honor until
a few months before her death, when her failing
health caused her to request and obtain honorable
release.
	She died as she had lived, a true and faithful
Latter-day Saint. Her many friends will miss her,
still to know that her sufferings are passed became
a comfort to those who loved her, and that she is
now enjoying sweet rest.
	The following resolutions were unanimously
adopted at a recent meeting of the Relief Society
of the Third Ward of Brigham City.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Whereas, Our Heavenly Father has seen fit in
His infinite love and mercy to call home our be
loved president. Sister Anna Christine Maria
Pallisen Jansen, therefore be it
	Resolved, That in her death we have lost an
earnest and faithful president and worker of the
Relief Society, one who was ever ready to help the
needy and give words of sympathy to those in
trouble.
	That we, her co-laborers, have lost a true, loving
sister, and that we will miss her words of wis-
dom and advice. But we know that God doeth all
things we[-]l, and while we miss her here, she is
happy with her husband and friends in the
eternal world.
	That we express our love and gratitude for
her unceasing labors, present a copy of these
resolutions to her bereaved family, and also that a
copy be sent to the WOMAN’S EXPONENT for publi-
cation and a copy be placed on file in the ward
record book.
']

["[WomansExponent | Vol24 | 1895 | August | Issue: 6 | Title: CORFERENCE OF THE NATIONAL AMERICAN | Author: Unknown]

CORFERENCE OF THE NATIONAL AMERICAN
WOMAN’S SUFFRAGE ASSOCIATION; HELD
AT SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MAY 13, AND
14, 1895.

	THE Conference was called to order by
Mrs. Emmeline B. Wells, president of the
National Woman’s Suffrage Association of
Utah. Prayer was offered by Rev. Anna
Shaw.
	Mrs. Wells introduced the Governor of
Utah, Hon. Caleb W. West, who wel-
comed the guests.
ADDRESS By GOVERNOR WEST.
Ladies And Gentlemen:
	I am informed that this is an assemblage
under the auspices of the National Ameri-
can Woman’s Suffrage Association. It is
one of the four Conferences that has been
provided for to be held in the United States
the present year in the interest of Woman’s
Suffrage. I am very glad to know that our
City and our Territory has been selected for
the holding of this Conference. I think
that it was wisely chosen because in the
Convention that assembled here during the
past month to form the organic law which
will make Utah one of the States of the
Union, moved by the spirit of the people
behind them they wisely, justly and
judiciously provided that there should be
no discrimination of sex in this new State.
Therefore it is meet and proper that those
who agreed with our people upon this sub-
ject should meet here in Council and in Con-
ference to devise ways and means to fur-
ther the cause that is so dear to the hearts
of many noble and self-sacrificing women.
	Now upon this occasion I don’t deem it
necessary or proper that I should attempt
to discuss the question—I have not as I un-
derstand it been invited here for that pur-
pose. The distinguished honor which was
done me in asking me to come here this
morning is to present to this audience a
woman who is known not only over our
whole land but over the civilized world,
one who has devoted her noble self-sacrific-
ing life to the cause of her sex, and in so
doing of course has devoted it in the high-
est sense to humanity. Now a word of in-
troduction here is entirely unnecessary I am
sure: in fact Susan B. Anthony was known
to the people of Utah long before it ever
knew your humble servant and speaker.
Twenty-five years ago as I understand Miss
Anthony paid a visit to this Territory. I
believe upon that occasion she was pre-
sented to its people by the distinguished
citizen who was then Mayor, who is now
dead, Mayor Daniel H. Wells. Now then
I trust and believe that Miss Anthony has
come with very pleasant recollections of
her visit; that she has returned to us now
is a matter of congratulation and pleasure,
and I trust that she upon this occasion will
find her visit both pleasant and profitable.
I can assure those ladies who have come
to visit us, that there is but little doubt that
the Constitution which has been made in
this hall and which will be submitted to
the people, our people at the November
election, will be carried by an overwhelm-
ing majority as it ought to be. (applause.)

[Column 2]
	Now permit me to introduce to you Miss
Susan B. Anthony. (applause)
REMARKS BY MISS ANTHONY.
	My good Friends:—This is the second
time on which I have been honored by the
Governor of a state to open our meeting,
to make the introductory speech. The
other night in Cheyenne Gov. Richards
made the opening address and presented
the speakers. Now to-day Governor West
of this Territory has made the opening ad-
dress of this Conference and I want you
my dear women to ask how it happened
that it should be those two Inter-mountain
States in which women hold the right of
suffrage, in which women are absolutely
clothed with that power in one state, and
have borne to the ballot box the vote not
only at City and State elections but at the
last general Presidential election absolutely
went to the ballot box and voted for the
President—how it happened that woman’s
enfranchisement is fully recognized—that
this Territory in its organic law to be pre-
sented to Congress has engrafted in it this
primal idea of perfect equality of rights to
women, civil and polttical, how it happened
that the highest officials of these two moun-
tain States should come to the front and in-
troduce at the meeting, or present the speak-
ers at a meeting of this sort. I don’t be-
lieve you could have gotten the Governor
of the State of New York or Pennsylvania
or the Governors of any of the older states;
I don’t believe you could have impressed it
upon the minds of any one of those Gover-
nors that it would be an honor to him as
well as an honor to the audience or people
to come forward and occupy this position.
It is because the women of this Territory
when it becomes a State are to be the
equals of the men, that it is best for the
man holding the highest position in the
State to take this position. I shall never
forget an incident which occurred in Wash-
ington last year after a majority of the men
of the State of Colorado had gone to the
ballot box and voted to place the ballot in
the hands of the women of the Centennial
State, when invited to come to our meeting
every representative of Wyoming and every
representative of the new State of Colorado
was present to bear his testimony to the
g[-]eat principle of equality of rights, and I
presented those men to the large audience
assembled in the City of Washington and I
made the remark that while representatives
and others of the entire state of New York
had been going to Washington for the last
quarter of a century making appeals and ad-
dresses before Congressional Committees as
well as other large audiences we never yet
had such a delegation of members of Con-
gress and the Senators in the United States
senate come to the hotel to pay their re-
spects to the New York delegate, to our
Convention—nor had I ever known of the
representatives of the whole delegation,
Congressional delegation from any other
State in the Union to have come to the con-
vention to make their assertion that they
believed in equality of rights for women, it
is not until women actually hold the power
of the ballot in their hands that the mass of
government officials in each of the States
will ever think of it, will even know that
women are a part of the people. So I con-
gratulate all of you upon this fact that in
this great Conference that is assembled here
that three states out of the forty-five, [for
I consider your State virtually in the Union]
three states ont of the forty-five have

[Column 3]
recognized this fundamental principle of the
right of women to an equal voice in the
government with men. Is it not remark-
able that for over a hundred years since our
Revolutionary Fathers made the declara-
tion that all men are created equal, that
governments derive their just power from
the consent of the governed, that taxation
without representation is tyranny, is it not
remarkable that it has taken one hundred
years to convince the men that women
were a part of all men who are created
equal and that women are a part of the
governed whose consent should be given or
with-held from the government, that wo-
men as tax payers have a right to represen-
tation, that to tax women without confer-
ring the right of representation was equally
tyrannous to women as to men, is it not re-
markable that it has taken one hundred
years and more to convince and educate the
men of this nation that women are a part
of the men, a part of the people, a part of
those whose consent should be given or
witheld in every government organization
of the City, State and nation. But now
this point has been obtained, this justice
has been accorded in three of the States
wholly, and in one of the states partially,
they have municipal suffrage in the state of
Kansas and in twenty-three of the states
they enjoy the privilege of voting on
school matters and in five more the question
of equal suffrage is pending. This ques-
tion has come to be discussed and agitated
in every possible, public assembly.
	You can scarcely enter an educational con-
vention or a convention for the good of the
different religious denominations, a scientific
convention, a political convention of any
sort that the question of granting equal
rights to women is not uppermost. Dur-
ing the last two years there is scarcely a
legislature that has not had this question
of the extension of suffrage to women un-
discussion and has had a bill presented and
in many cases voted upon it. In most of
the cases where the bill passed one house
it failed to pass in the other. In several of
the states a bill for the submission of the
question has been passed by both houses so
that in five of the states in the Union to-
day the question of woman’s enfranchise-
ment is now pending. The legislatures of
Oregon, Idaho, Nevada, California and
New York have during the past year passed
this resolution so that the whole question,
or the question of giving the right of suf-
frage to women is being seriously consider-
ed. So now you may say it is only a ques-
tion of time as some of our friends have
been wont to answer us in the days gone by
and now the question has become one of a
very short time when every possible gather-
ing of men and women under the stars and
stripes and will discuss the question.
	When such a splendid majority in your
Constitutional Convention gave their voice
in favor of this perfect equal recognition
for the women of this country. I stand
here to congratulate the men of the Ter-
ritory of Utah for if the rank and file, if the
vast mass of the men who go to the ballot
box in the Territory of Utah had been op-
posed to the enfranchisement of women,
then the representatives in this convention
held during the last month would never
have felt justified in having incorporated
that splendid suffrage clause in the Con-
stitution, it is because the rank and file of
the men and women of this Territory have
been educated into the idea of perfect


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


equality of rights for women that the con-
vention was able to pass that resolution
and incorporate the suffrage clause giving
equal rights to women. So it is not only
the men of the convention that have to be
congratulated but the men all over the Ter-
ritory who are the constituents of these
representative men in convention assembled.
Representative men cannot do much beyond
what the rank and file of their constituents
shall demand, and the women of Utah
must remember when they hold the ballot
in their hands that their representatives in
the legislature will be able to do but very
little beyond the majority of the rank and
file, the representatives are always to con-
form to the fountain, and the fountain can
rise no higher than the source that feeds it.
So legislation can do but little more than
what is demanded by the vast majority of
the rank and file of the voters. Therefore
I congratulate not only the representatives
who sat in this hall of the great and
glorious Constitution which is to be pre-
sented to the voters of this Territory for
ratification, I congratulate the rank and
rfile of the voters all over the state for hav-
ing made demands in their respective
localities to produce this splendid results.
	But I want to say to you that as President
of the National Woman’s Suffrage Associa-
tion I am here for the purpose of looking
into this new constituency of the Woman’s
Suffrage Association and I am here to take
the chair in this convention by virtue of
having been re-elected by the delegates
representing the thirty-six different states
and societies of the Union composing the
National. Ever since we were fairly or-
ganized Utah has always been with us and
among us to help to make and to establish
and to maintain the National Woman Suf-
frage Association. We have always had
with us the delegates from Utah. Nearly
always have we had with us the lady who
is now the President of our Territorial As-
sociation Mrs. Emmeline B. Wells who
represented the women of this Territory at
the Convention in Washington. As I am
President by the choice of the delegates
sent from those thirty-six states I feel
highly honored with my position and I am
delighted to be here and I congratulate you
in their name and on their behalf in the
grand step that has been taken by your Con-
stitutional Convention in contributing to
the admission of Utah as a State giving
the equality of Suffrage to women. (Ap-
plause.)


", '[WomansExponent | Vol24 | 1895 | October | Issue: 10 | Title: CONFERENCE N. A. W. S. A. | Author: Unknown]

		CONFERENCE N. A. W. S. A.
	CONFERENCE OF THE NATIONAL AMERICAN
		WOMAN SUFFRAGE ASSOCIATION; HELD
		AT SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MAY, 13, AND
		14, 1895.
				[Continued.]
	MISS ANTHONY, upon introducing Mrs.
Stansbury said:
	"We have with us a representative of
your neighboring State that is a little in ad-
vance of this last action of Utah in enfran-
chising women, and that is a representative
from Colorado where the women voted at
the last election of '94 and as you remem-
ber were enfranchised by a vote of seven
thousand majority at the election of '93,
Mrs. Stansbury, and I want to say to you
that she is no small part of the Editorial
staff of the Rocky Mountain News, and if
you behave yourselves you will probably
see yourselves reflected in the columns of
that paper. (applause.)
	REMARKS BY MRS. L. M. STANSBURY.
	Miss President, Ladies and Gentlemen:—
I am very glad that Aunt Susan has told
you that it is my business to write rather
than to talk. I am afraid in our state we
would think it was cruel to ask me to speak
after the speakers that you have listened to
here this morning, and yet it seems that we
from Colorado ought to thank you for the
help that Utah has given us. You have
heard a great deal about what we have to
go through in politics, you have heard
how utterly disreputable it is to go to the
primaries, caucuses and conventions. I
think from my experience that we have had
a great deal more disreputable time in secur-
ing the ballot than in using it, in the State
of Colorado. In the State of Colorado the
struggle has been going on ever since
General McCook suggested it. In the first
legislature I believe in 1876 when the Con-
stitutional Convention was held there, there
was a very hard struggle to get it put into
our Constitution, but our men were not so
well posted in regard to suffrage as they are
today. In 1882 there was a bill introduced
which met an untimely death as bills are
wont to do. There was a suffrage associa-
tion formed for the purpose of raising
money to help to carry their campaign. I
think there were only 8 charter members,
ours has about 180; we kept on working by
ourselves, I do not think there were more
than 20 or 30 of us in regular attendance
until the first legislature convened. After
that, my friends, we introduced a bill, we
were all very young and very green, we
did not know anything about a bill. They 
told us a constitutional amendment was
necessary but it took the lawyers forty-five
years to find that out. We found a young
man who had a constitutional amendment
which provided that no foreigner should
vote on his first papers. He said he was
willing to strike out the word "male"

[Column 2]
which would carry with it equal suffrage to
all women. So in spite of all their ridicule
if we did not know enough to get our bill
in we knew enough to get a rider, and horse
and rider went down together. When the
next legislature met we had a bill. There
were three more bills besides ours to give
suffrage to woman, and it was that more
than anything else that made us feel the
sentiment was general. I think that was
what gave us courage to go to the National
Woman's Association and ask for help. In
1893 the women of Utah held a silver meeting
a few weeks after. That was the first of
our suffrage campaign- We did not desig-
nate it a suffrage meeting, some women
talked silver and some talked suffrage.
Miss Shaw said that she did not know what
she would vote for. Now we are very un-
certain, we may be sorry which ever way
we vote but we do know that we will
vote for silver just as early, and just as fast
as the law will allow us. You have heard
a great deal about the silent influence of
women. Now in our state we have come
to the conclusion that the silent influence
is to talk and talk to a man until he will
give you anything on earth to make you
shut up. I really believe our men have
concluded that they would rather let us go
and vote as we pleased, than to talk and
labor and ask them to do this and that and
the other thing, because they are all willing
to please the ladies.
	I did not expect to speak at all here this
morning; if I had, I would have put on
some bigger sleeves. You understand that
Mr. Worth invented this style himself as he
thought that large sleeves would be neces-
sary to accommodate the muscles.
	Not having expected to speak to you at
all I will make way for some one who is
better capable of doing so. (Applause.)
	Miss Anthony—This was one of the
political candidates for State Superintendent.
She did not get quite votes enough to elect
her but nevertheless she was one of the
nominees of the democratic party, Mrs. C.
C. Bradford of Colorado. 
REMARKS BY MRS. C. C. BRADFORD
	Miss President, Ladies and Gentlemen:
Just fancy coming as a representative of a
state where women vote, to a Territory
where women don't yet quite vote and be
presented as a defeated candidate. It is
perfectly dreadful. Nevertheless I was the
first woman ever nominated for a state of-
ficer in Colorado, and though I was beaten
they cannot take that honor from me.
	It seems to me that it is almost like call-
ing for a rush light when the full blaze of
the sun has been permitted to enter a room
to ask me to talk after Miss Anthony and
Miss Shaw have thrilled you as they have
done this morning, it is unkind to you, but
it is a very great kindness to me because
Miss Anthony, Miss Shaw and the women
who have worked so long for Suffrage have
helped to put me on the throne and I am
here; (applause) and I am supremely
thankful to them for putting me there. I
like it a great deal better just as Miss Shaw
said than when I was behind the throne.
Another thing, so many people seem to
think that women are going to become so
terribly masculine and do so many terrible
things when they get into politics. I have
been in politics up to my eyes, and up to
my head, and I stumped the state of Col-
orado twice, once as an organizer of a politi-
cal party, and once as a candidate who did

[Column 3]
not get elected, and I have never met in all
the thousand miles I have traveled with
more reverence or respect than I did then,
when I was upon the throne. I never met
anything more cordial or more delightful
than the good fellowship of the men and
women who were glad to support the wo-
man who had helped to win suffrage for
them, and who chose to cast her lot with a
political party who absolutely hadn't the
ghost of a show. I do not believe in wo-
men seeking offices but I do believe in edu-
cational offices in every way, I believe that
the love and education of women are going
to show through some offices in the state
just as much as will that of the men.
	In regard to the differences of sex, that has
been so ably touched upon that I need not
refer to it except that being in politics will
make you more prominent than you ever
were before, because every woman who has
a child knows absolutely that she must take
an interest in the state—and I am so glad
as Miss Shaw says that you are coming in
as a state—I know you are and I believe
every hope will be confirmed, and I know
the faith and the hope of the Utah Women
and the faith and hope of the Colorado wo-
men for their sisters when this great and
glorious Territory becomes a State; and let
me thank God for the recognition of equal
suffrage here in Utah, and it will yet pre-
vail from clime to clime, from shore to
shore and from Canada to Mexico, wher-
ever the flag of the United States waves it
will be dear to our hearts for it is also a
Suffrage flag. (Applause.)
REMARKS BY MRS. C. E. ALLEN, PRESIDENT
	OE UTAH FEDERATION OF CLUBS.
	Ladies and Gentlemen—I am very glad
to be asked to assist in extending the wel-
come of Utah to our guests Miss Anthony
and Miss Shaw. It is most fitting that at
this time the women of Utah should confer
with these distinguished leaders in all that
tends to the elevation of women. This is a
time when very many women here in Utah
are hesitating about what is best to be done.
There are many women who are influenced
by the old conservative feeling which for a
long time influenced me, that political life
was not best for women. It is but a few
years since I became associated with this
movement but for a long time I recognized
this great truth. In this respect my little
daughter far excels me. When she was
eight years old we were walking up Main
street and she said to me, "Mother, I don't
see why the women can't take some part in
politics," and this opinion she has adhered
to up to this time. I finally became con-
vinced that moral reform was impossible
until the ballot was granted to women.
	I used to feel that it would interfere with
home life, but I now see that the safety of
the home depends upon woman suffrage. I
read somewhere in an old law book that the
first of all Roman laws was the preserva-
tion of the home. What a revolution it
would work in our American politics if this
beautiful idea could be carried into effect
from the mother's standpoint. The life of
our very nation is threatened by the evil in-
fluences which threaten our homes, and
who shall speak concerning the young men
if not the home-makers. I believe if this
idea is carried into our politics it will make
of woman the power she should be. I
honor the women who are laboring for the
cause and all who are in sympathy with
them and I believe their noble deeds will be
recorded on the pages of history, that the


[Column 1]
reformation was far deeper than it appeared
on the outside, that their true work was in
restoring our political principle to its true
basis. (Applause.)
	Miss Anthony:—Last February we held
our first National Conference south of the
Mason and Dixon line and when the wo-
men from Alabama, Florida and Georgia
were introduced to the audience I was com-
pelled to say that the southern women were
truly born orators and speakers and I begin
to think the women of Utah are born ora-
tors and speakers as well as the southern
women.
	Mrs. Joannah Melton President of the
G. A. R. Relief Society was introduced by
Miss Anthony.
	REMARKS BY MRS. MELTON.
At the last moment your chairman has
invited me to say a word of welcome to our
guests Miss Anthony and Miss Shaw which
I feel very much pleased to do. Twenty-
six years ago; I am not going to say as long
as I live for I don’t want to tell you that,
but 26 years ago in the City of Lafayette.
Indiana, there were posters all over the city
that Miss Susan B. Anthony and Mrs.
Elizabeth Cady Stanton would appear at the
Opera House. Of course the women of the
city were very much interested and we all
asked what kind of women they were.
Some of the gentlemen said “Now don’t
you go to that Opera House to hear those
women,’’ “Why, what is the reason?
Why should’nt we go and hear those wo-
men, are they not women?’’ “Yes, but
they are men women” (laughter) and I was
a little bit doubtful about going but I said
to myself: “Well, I won’t believe anything
until I see it and I am going to see what
kind of a woman a man woman is”
(laughter) So I went to the Opera House
and I saw women that I only wished that I
could be just like them, they were so
modest and motherly and kind and charm-
ing in their speeches and their conversation
that I was converted then and there before
I left the Opera House and I thought that
Woman’s Suffrage was just right. About
ten years ago I received a letter from Miss
Anthony telling me that she had heard of
me through some friends asking me to take
up the work in the Dakotas’. It seemed a
very hard thing to do in those two states
then because they were divided, but we
succeeded during the little while we worked.
	We were encouraged in our work but it
was just the same as it is here a great many
of the women had a great many arguments
against Woman Suffrage. Since coming
here six years ago I have watched the wo-
men here very closely and I see you have
some very able women and especially this
little woman (alluding to Mrs. Wells) seems
to be a bundle of executive power and she
has a good corps behind her and I knew the
suffrage question was safe in their hands.
I have done all the work I could but you
know the opposition which women are re-
ceiving at this present time and mostly
from the men. It was my privilege to be
invited to a home to meet some of our
Scotch delegates that were here at the Con-
vention, and there were five or six present
and several ladies, some with their wives.
One woman came in and said, “Well, I
suppose you gentlemen voted for us?” One
gentleman replied “You hit me hard!” and
I knew from that that he must have done it
and voted the other way. The lady re-
plied: “Oh, the women have shown to the

[Column 2]
world what they can do, they have shown
they have brains, and now I think it is time
that we have the ballot.” An old gentle-
man remarked to me: “that is my daughter,
she graduated at college, she is a mother
and now she says ‘I will show the people
what I can do.” That is just the argu-
ment that she used that now we have at-
tained this power we have got to the top
and we must not go backwards again. But
ladies, I think you have a grand future be-
fore you and with all the opposition to com-
bat you will succeed in this Territory.
	As Miss Shaw said, I have no patience with
women who do not think that the women
will do right in this Territory when given
the ballot, I have faith to know that the
women have heads and hearts and are just
as true and noble and kindly towards their
own sex as the men, and I don’t see why
the women of this Territory should not
vote just as intelligently for themselves as
a man. One gentleman in the Convention
said his wife was a Republican and he was
a Democrat. He said they would not want
to fight on the question. If I want to do
anything that my husband is opposed to I
don’t say anything I just keep quiet but I
just do my own way. (Laughter) Now I
tell you ladies and gentlemen that we are
just going to get there. Just look at that
ship of State as it comes in. Do you see
those Eastern States? Do you see the wo-
men bending down and looking up? They
are just as eager as the ones marching in
side by side. See the twin States of Dakota
they don’t come in equal they are just half.
Now we have the State of Colorado march-
in, men and women look alike. Sometimes
when we see them together we don’t know
which is the man and which is the woman.
See the State of Wyoming, the men and
women look just alike, and as Miss Shaw
said last night “We live together so many
years that we grow to look alike” and here
comes this beautiful young State of Utah.
Why it is dressed beautifully in white, com-
ing in with purity and glory and every-
thing behind it with men and women
equal. (Applause.)

WOMAN’S DAY AT SALTAIR.

	THE Relief Society of Salt Lake Stake
and the Women of the State arranged for an
excursion to Saltair on Tuesday, August
20. The affair was in the hands of a com-
mittee of women represented by Mrs. M. I.
Horne, President of the Relief Society of
Salt Lake Stake, and Mrs. E. B. Wells Pre-
sident of the State Suffrage Association. The
day was not very satisfactory even though the
Carnival of Madrid was well advertised,
and consequently many who had intended
to patronize the ladies did not attend; how-
ever there was quite a good-sized gathering
and a rather pleasing program of exercises,
though one of the distinguished gentlemen
who was expected to speak disappointed
the audience by his absence, Judge W. H.
King. The exercises commenced at 5 p.
m. General John Q. Cannon acting as
Master of Ceremonies, called the assembly
to order and announced prayer by Sister
Zina D. H. Young, who then offered an
eloquent prayer, and next came the Pream-
ble and Sentiments by ladies in different
parts of the state, a part of which were
read by General Cannon in a clear distinct
voice, followed by the Governor whose re-
marks to the women of the state are given
here.
 PREAMBLE.
	IN all the years past since the pioneers enter-
ed this then barren, desert valley, years full
of startling episodes and thrilling incidents,
woman has fulfilled her part of the pro-
gramme equally with man, and has emulat-
ed the noble example of the heroic pilgrim
mothers of New England, in bearing the
burdens and sharing the toil of the men
who have made the “desert blossom as the
rose,” and after many years of patient
struggle and endurance have at last suc-
ceeded in establishing a great common-
wealth, ranking equal with other states in
this free Republic, aud in one respect
surpassing all, except Wyoming and Colo-
rado, in according to women equal suffrage
with men. Thus has a new era opened up
for woman. She has now entered into her
land of promise; the eyes of the world are turn-
ed hither to see what the women of Utah will
do with this boasted right, the ballot. We
must not disappoint our friends or gratify
our opponents by our indifference or neglect,
but show our true patriotism in this hour
of our country’s greatest need. We should
prove worthy the sublime confidence re-
posed in us by those valiant men, who
championed our cause in the memorable
Constitutional Convention of 1895.
	We cannot too strongly urge upon wo-
men voters the grave responsibility
devolving upon them in the present condi-
tion of affairs in our state and nation, and
entreat them to come proudly forward and
take part in the struggle for right now be-
ing agitated, by casting their votes and us-
ing their utmost influence to maintain the
principles of justice, freedom and liberty,
our noble inheritance from our revered fore-


[Column 1]
fathers, according to their honest convic-
tions of right and wrong.
SENTIMENTS
	THE sacred right, but at the same .time
privilege, of the franchise has been obtained
by the women of Utah.
	We are watched by three classes of men
and women. By the cynic who expects
that we will make but a poor use or, per-
chance, no use at all of it, by the timid
women who fear for “the consequences”,
and by earnest women of many states who
look to us to vindicate their hopes and as-
pirations. And deeper down in the heart
of things there is the great need for the
good, pure resolute women of these United
States to rise in their might and help right
the deplorable condition of our country!
Let every woman voter in Utah feel her
responsibility and study the questions at
issue, and then vote her convictions.

	WOMAN’S domestic qualities will in no
sense deteriorate because of her political
equalities; she will lose none of her woman-
ly wifely attributes; her influence will be
exerted to purify the political atmosphere
and secure a rigorous enforcement of all
just laws, and an equally prompt repeal of
obnoxious ones.
	The female pioneer element, which is
largely represented in this state, now as in
days of old, developes the noblest womanly
characteristics, and already is observed a
marked independence of action and charact-
er which is seldom found in older civiliza-
tions where fashion and social distinction
rule with autocratic sway.

	The franchise is one of the highest privi-
leges granted to the individual under a free
government. If to vote is one of the great-
est privileges, is it not then a sacred duty
which no woman who desires the sanctity
of her home protected, the right to life,
liberty and the pursuit of happiness, sus-
tained and preserved inviolate, both of her
loved ones and herself, will neglect to per-
form? It is now an imperative obligation
devolving upon the women of Utah, to en-
courage, teach and sustain each other in
voting intelligently for honest efficient
officers, who will labor unswervingly for
the best interest of the people trusting
them with the affairs of state, county or
city. 
	Whether women purify the ballot or not
remains to be seen; but most certainly they
will add an element tending to greater per-
fection, the same as exemplified in the
family, which is the unit of government.
	A one sided organization only half per-
forms the work; when the other half is
added it rounds out into a perfect whole.
	If the study of politics is the main work of
women’s clubs from now until November,
a more determining vote for the good
of the whole country will be polled than
ever before in Utah. Women all rise to
the hight of your privileges!

	A victory gained for a great principle if
barren of results, is more to be deplored
than defeat; for defeat leads to renewed
effort, while victory followed by apathy
and indifference, is like Dead Sea Fruit and
the Apples of Sodom. If the women of
Utah who one year ago were earnestly de-
manding and striving for the right of suf-
frage, now sit down supinely and fold their
hands and say, “We have the ballot, we

[Column 2]
will vote but beyond that will take no fur-
ther interest in politics”, they are not
worthy of the rights of citizenship in a
growing state or great nation.  
	The acceptance of the position and privi-
leges of a citizen carries with it the obliga-
tions and responsibilities of citizenship, and
in my opinion, it is the duty of every wo-
man, not only to vote, but inform herself
on the vital issues of the day, and acquaint
herself thoroughly with the great principles
that uplift and benefit humanity, and un-
derlie all correct legislation, good govern-
ment and true prosperity. Having done
this for herself, I believe she is then bound
by her duty to the state and nation to dis-
seminate those principles, and to see, as far
as she can assist them, that all other women
within her reach, vote and understand what
they are voting for. It is only through the
efforts and influence of women alive to
their duties and responsibilities that the
state or nation will be benefited by wo-
man’s enfranchisememt.

	I think we have had a great privilege
granted us in having the franchise at such
an important time, when great political
questions are confronting us as at the pre-
sent. When we as citizens of this great
Republic, will feel in duty bound to take
part in the great issues that lie before us;
and may we do so conscientiously according
to our convictions of right, and strive with
all diligence to understand our duties and
the great responsibilities that rest upon us
as citizens of this great Republic, as well as
of our own fair state of Utah.
	We know we are on the eve of a great
financial crisis; its importance we can only
realize in part; how it will end the Lord
only knows. We must use the wisdom of
Solomon, aided by the Spirit of the Lord to
direct us that we may be on His side, then
we need not fear what the result would be.
	I often think of the reply that President
Joseph Smith made to Senator Douglas
when he asked him how he managed to
govern the people. He said he taught
them correct principles and let them govern
themselves.
	That is what we want today, to under-
stand the correct principles that underlie
our Government. It is this lack of know-
ledge with us as a people that I fear at this
time when there is so much at stake. We
have never had such a responsibility resting
upon us before, as the privilege of voting
for the highest official of this great Com-
monwealth; and may we give this question
and every other, that due deliberation, that
we may vote intelligently upon all ques-
tions that come before us, even holding it
as a sacred trust that can be neither bought
nor sold for any consideration. And may
we always have reason to be grateful to
those who worked so faithfully that we
might enjoy political freedom.
GOVERNOR HEBER M. WELLS.
	We are making history rapidly What-
ever future centuries may bring forth, they
must concede to the Nineteenth, that it
revolutionized the world industrially, polti-
ically and religiously.
	Freedom of thought and action was
never so great as now, the yoke of religious
creed or despotic government never so far
removed or successfully repelled, the main-
tainance of human liberties never so well
sustained.
	What wonder then, that this progressive

[Column 3]
age of human enlightenment should witness
the breaking forth of women from the
chrysalis of exclusive domesticity into ac-
tive participation in Government affairs,
and that man’s assumption of the right to
vote and govern, without consultation with
his equal and natural partner and compan-
ion, should be relegated to the past.
	The women of Utah are to be congratu-
lated upon their accession to the rights of
American citizens. To attain these rights
Herculean work has been performed; wo-
men, themselves, as well as men have had
to be converted to the cause, strong antipa-
thies and prejudices have had to be over-
come in both, and only the most persistent
and determined effort could have accom-
plished so much in comparatively so short
a time.
	The credit of this great work belongs to
the women of Utah, for whom no words
of commendation can be misplaced. Their
reward is great for they now enjoy many
privileges that are vouchsafed to but com-
paratively few women of our country.
They will not be content with this, how-
ever, as their cause is too great to permit of
their resting satisfied with merely local
advantages—it is the enfranchisement of
half the Nation, the better half—they are
contending for, and no doubt, the influence
of the women of Utah will be felt in behalf
of “Woman Suffrage” until the last woman
in this broad land shall have been enfran-
chised.
	History will record you among the pion-
eers of this great work, and the memory of
your good deeds will live after you.
	After the Governor’s address W. C. Dun-
bar delighted the audience with selections
upon his Highland bagpipes, and was
warmly applauded. Senator Frank J.
Cannon made a thrilling speech in which
he dwelt strongly upon the present condi-
tion of the masses of the people here and
in the United States, and paid a glowing
tribute to the women of Utah, their cour-
age, integrity and patient endurance of
toil and hardship, and cited incidents in
the life and experience of the General
President of the Relief Society, making a
pathetic picture and extoling her virtues
and excellence of character. No greater or
more eloquent compliment could be paid
to a mother in Israel. We expected his
remarks in brief to publish, but hope now
to have them for the next issue of the
paper.
	Miss Webster’s skipping-rope dance
came next and was very pretty and grace-
ful and in perfect time. Miss Webster is
quite a favorite wherever she appears.
The bagpipes were introduced again and
were as welcome as at first. Then followed
a further reading of sentiments and resolu-
tions.

	THIS is our sentiment: Woman, the
equal of man, and the crowning glory of
the celestial kingdom.

	I think that as women are amenable to
the laws of our state and bear equally the
responsibilities with men, woman should
also share in all the privileges accorded to
men, not only in voting, but in holding
offices of honor, emolument and trust suit-
able to their sex.

	IN my judgment the women of Utah,
having been given the elective franchise,
would do well to read the editorial and


[Column 1]
political portions of our own state press,
which not only has the welfare of the peo-
ple of the state in view, but is as patriotic
as any portion of our national press; and
they should endeavor to post themselves
upon the questions that are to be settled by
the ballot, and in voting should be guided
more by judgment and principle than by
partisanship.

	FOR a long time I have been in favor of
suffrage. I believe it will be the means of
elevating us. Woman can accomplish
more than man if she is prepared for the
work. She can have statesmen born and
raised under her influence so that they can
become a power in the land. I believe
there is a need for such men, and it can
never be accomplished while women are
considered inferior to men. I don’t see
why we cannot understand the political
situation of our country. I am convinced
that if we are united we can do a great
deal of good towards elevating our race.

	HAVING, after a long struggle, gained
the right of suffrage, I think it becomes
our duty as citizens of the new state of
Utah, to arouse from our lethargy and use
all our energy and the intelligence God has
given us to study the best way to use this right
not only in this state but also in the nation,
and also use our influence in getting them
to see the necessity of the same.
	Statistics show that a large percent of the
women in the United States are self-sup-
porting.
	They are expected to pay taxes and help
support the Government. This being the
case should they not take an interest in
national affairs? “Taxation without repre-
sentation is tyranny.”
	I would like to ask you gentlemen
and ladies, if the franchise rightly used,
will not tend to morally revolutionize our
government and place it on that grand un-
selfish, unintriguing political platform that
was presented to us by our forefathers.

	WE ought to be alive to the best interest
of the country. We want silver restored to
its full value (sixteen to one) that we can
pay our debts from the products of our
farm's without selling real estate. We
ought to let all other considerations be as
minor ones until that is accomplished, then
see that our laws are carried out, or some
of them mended, and not leave the law in
the hands of men that will not carry them
out, there are thousands of cases where
justice sleeps, and I for one am tired of it.
Can we as women sit by and see such out-
rages commited and not say anything about
it? I say out with such men!
	Let us have honorable men and women
for our officers, and see to it that we get
them. Don’t let us shirk our duties. See
that all women are registered and vote, and
also attend our Primaries. Keep abreast of
the times; stand for our rights and do not
be daunted. These are some of my views
also the most of the women that I am ac-
quainted with. They seem to be waking
up—the women of Utah—to their rights.

	EVERY person of observation who has
noticed the great strides every nation is
making in more humane and liberal govern-
ment. has also noticed the rapid advance-
ment of women throughout the world.
Co-eval with all greatness among nations,
comes the God-given liberty of women.

[Column 2]
Just as the misty clouds of superstition and
fogyism about woman’s sphere gives way
to the scearch-light of truth, will she take
her place politically, and will not only take
her place, but man will willingly recognize
it, learning at last that all their interests are
one. Now that equal suffrage has been
granted to the women of Utah, the obsta-
cles raised by our opponents have all melted
away. Everything works in harmony, and
all seem perfectly satisfied. Ladies who
declared they would never exercise the
franchise, are today enthusiastic over the
great questions that are holding the atten-
tion of the nation, and are ready to cast
their ballots. I see nothing discouraging,
but believe the little stone of Woman Suf-
frage set rolling here in the West will yet fill
the whole earth.

	I think the status of the women in the
new state is very good and is destined to
place woman upon the high elevation des-
tined by her Creator; nor will the true
woman of the state fill less her domestic
relations as a wife and mother because she
has a right estimate of her position and new
responsibilities. She will preserve her
self respect and dignity in her new position
as in her home life, and seek with true
nobility to refine and purify the political
and moral atmosphere.
	The influence of woman is the same
everywhere. Her condition influences the
morals and character of the people in the
community of which she is a member. The
granting of the franchise to women shows
the political status of the people. The wo-
men of the new state asked many times for
this great boon ere it was again bestowed;
and having received it again, I have no
fears that she will abuse this great blessing,
nor transcend the proper limits of womanly
dignity, but by study and thought prove
herself the intellectual peer of her brother
man. Her education is extended; her
knowledge is increased; she is proving her
capabilities to grasp and understand, and
discuss the leading questions of the day as
intelligently as the majority of the other
sex. In many cases her conceptions are
clearer, while her opportunities are increas-
ing and her field of labor widening, I feel
assured she will prove herself equal to the
occasion. I think the women of the new
state will prove to our opponents and the
world at large, that we do appreciate the
franchise, by studying the political issues
of the day and voting for men and measures
that are best calculated to advance the in-
terests of all mankind. Therefore, in my
humble opinion, the granting of the fran-
chise or equal suffrage, has raised the status
of the women In the new state materially
and will eventually prove a great blessing
not only to herself, but to future genera-
tions. Let the good work go on until all
women shall receive the same blessing that
the women of Utah now enjoy, by using
and not abusing this great boon.
RESOLUTIONS.
	WHEREAS, The constitution of the state
provides that the rights of citizens of the
state of Utah to vote and hold office shall
not be denied or abridged on account of
sex; and
	WHEREAS, Experience has taught us
that by woman’s entering into the profes-
sions of law, medicine and teaching, those
professions have been raised to a higher
place; that by her admission to the colleges
and universities of our country the moral

[Column 3]
tone of our institutions of learning has been
elevated and the standard of scholarship ad-
vanced; that by the product of her brain
and pen the progress of the whole human 
race has been promoted; that in the limited
opportunities offered her she has demonstrat-
ed her capacity to grasp governmental
questions and her ability to solve them; to
be equal to that of her male contemporaries;
and
	WHEREAS, Woman possesses in a very
high degree the power to reform, ennoble
and purify every organization of a religious,
social or political nature; now, therefore, be
it
	RESOLVED, That it is the duty of every
woman of the state possessing the requisite
qualifications, to enter into active political
work, to the end that our new privileges
may be exercised in behalf of pure govern-
ment.
	RESOLVED, That we agree to identify
ourselves with the political party that is the
best exponent of our political ideas, and
that we will participate in the Primaries and
Conventions of our respective parties as well
as vote upon election day that our voice
may be heard in formulating our party’s
policy, and that candidates may be nomi-
nated worthy of our support.
	RESOLVED, That we extend our hearty
greetings to our sister voters of the neigh-
boring states of Wyoming and Colorado and
we express the desire that ere long the
people of our whole nation may recognize 
the inestimable blessings conferred upon the 
body politic by the admission of man and
his helpmate, woman, to equal political
privileges.
	(Signed.)
ELIZABETH J. MCFARLANE.
MRS. J. FEWSON SMITH,
ETIZABETH M. PRICE,
				Committee.
FINAL RESOLUTION.
	RESOLVED, That the women of Utah
take the heartiest interest in all organiza-
tions tending to ameliorate the condition of 
women throughout the world.
	That they recognize the great education-
al work of the Federation of Clubs, and will
as far as they are able, co-operate with
them.
	That they sympathize with the persecut-
ed inhabitants of Armenia, and call upon
the nation to show their interest by gener-
ous contributions to a starving nation.
	Mrs. Elizabeth J. McFarlane was intro
duced and recited a very appropriate
selection in a most pleasing style. The
address was entitled “To Our Brothers.’’


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


"]
