["[WomansExponent | Vol33 | 1904 | February | Issue: 8 | Title: Mothers’ Work | Author: MYRTHA TONKS,]

MOTHERS’ WORK.
		SUMMIT STAKE.
Lec, 1st. Woman, Her Mission:
	(a) How to attain the highest type of
womanhood.
	(b) Learn the will of God concerning
them.
	(c) Preparation for further usefulness.
	(d) Laws of health, how observed.
	(e) Word of wisdom a fundamental prin-
ciple.
	(f) Full development of physical and
mental powers.
	(g) Tell of special ambition of women of
ancient Israel.
	(h) How do women of latter-day Israel
compare with those of former-day Israel in
fulfilling the most important function of
woman’s life?
Lec. 2nd.— Wifehood.
	(a) Woman’s duty to husband and home.
	(b) Love of God, desire to fulfill His
commandments.
	(c) How to be companionable.
	(d) Use influence for good, religiously
and intellectually.
	(e) What greater privileges do women of
today enjoy than women of ancient days
enjoyed?
	(f) God’s commandment to Adam and
Eve. (Gen 1: 28-36.)
Lec. 3rd.—Parents:
	(a) Highest calling of man and woman.
	(b) Responsible to God for their ministry.
(Ref. Maeser's “School and Fireside.”)
	(c) Child’s best heritage, honorable birth.
	(d) Purposes bent to prepare heart and
mind for embryonic moulding.
	(e) Cultivation of spirituality.
	(f) Prenatal influence: example, child of
faith.
	(g) Beginning and duration of parent’s
responsibility.
Lec. 4th.—Infancy.
	(a) The child’s welcome as a heaven-sent
gift.
	(b) Cleanliness a necessity.
	(c) Fresh air and warmth essential to
well-being.


[Column 1]
	(d) Nature’s food best, how often given.
	(e) Hygienic clothing,and plentiful mas-
sage with olive oil.
Lec. 5th.—Childhood.
	(a) Bright looks and gentle tones the
child’s birthright.
	(b) The character of the child a special
study.
	(c) Prayer taught at the mother’s knee,
and early imprsssions of faith in God.
	(d) First principles taught, obedience,
truthfulness, honesty and respect for the
priesthood and those in authority over
them.
	(e) Faith in ordination taught by pre-
cept and example.
Lec. 6th.
	(a) Co-operation of parents necessary for
future welfare of child.
	(b) Special teachings necessary when
child leaves home to attend district school.
	(c) Example set by others not always
worthy.
	(d) Care taken to guide children in choice
of companions.
	(e) Bad effects of continual fault-finding.
	(f) Parents should control themselves
before correcting child.
	(g) Cultivate spirituality and develop by
constant care. (Ref. “Fireside Talks,”
Carl G. Maeser.)
	Committee on Mothers’ Work, Summit
Stake.
			ONEIDA STAKE.
	Lesson 1st. Marriage,
		Ye kindred spirits filled with mutual love,
		Pure as the dews descending from above,
		All hail! for you the sacred keys are given,
		To make you one on earth and one in heaven,
		Be fruitful then, and let your race extend;
		Fill Earth, the stars and worlds that never end.
							Key to Theology.
	1. Marriage is ordained of God unto
man, Doc. & Cov. Sec. 49-15. 2. The
great purpose for which marriage was or-
dained is to fulfill that first divine law given
in the beginning— “Multiply and replen-
ish the earth,” Gen. 1: 28. Heb. 13: 4. 3.
Those contemplating marriage should pos-
sess a proper knowledge of the laws of life
and have a reverence for God’s laws con-
cerning the marriage relation. Key to The-
ology, page 165 166. 4.Warn our girls
against the delusive hope of reforming after
marriage a man whose habits before were
bad. 5. Justify advice by giving example
of lives which have been wrecked in this
way. 6. Kind of marriage approved, Doc.
& Cov. Sec. 132-15-18-19. Key to Theo-
logy, page 162.
	Lesson 2nd. Personal appearance and
cleanliness.
	“Cleanliness is indeed next to Godliness.’’
	1. Importance of cleanliness in person
and dress. Doc. &. Cov. Sec. 42-40-41.
2. Effect of personal appearance in society,
in the home, and in business. 3. Clothing
of expectant mothers should be both
healthful and becoming. 4. Good effects
of early training in cleanliness and personal
appearance. 5. Fashions and frivolities
of Babylon should be discouraged. (Brig-
ham Young) Journal Discourses, Vol. 16
& 17 page 27. Doc. & Cov. Sec. 42-40-41.

[Column 2]
	Lesson 3rd. Economy and Industry.
	1. Economy and Industry are unfailing
sources of temporal prosperity. 2. Econ-
omy of strength, of time and of money is
absolutely necessary to success. 3. Thou
shall not be idle: For he that is idle shall
not eat the bread nor wear the garments
of the laborer. Doc. & Cov. Sec. 42-42.
Doc. & Cov. Sec. 72-29. Proverbs 19: 15.
II Thess. 3-10-11-12. 4. Begin early in life
to teach a child to love manual labor: the
educational value of work.
	Lesson 4th. Tithing and Fast Offerings.
	1. By the law of Tithing the land of Zion
may be sanctified unto the Lord. Doc. &
Cov. Sec. 119: 6. 2. Origin of the law of
Tithing, Lev. 27-30 to 34. Gen. 14 18-19
20. 3. Meaning of the term Tithing. Doc.
& Cov. Sec. 119, Mal, 3: 10-11, Prov. 3: 9-
10. 4. When and to whom Tithing should
be paid. 5. Promise to those who are
tithed. Doc. & Cov. Sec. 64-23. 6. Potency
of the law of Tithing. 7. Fast offerings. In
as much as ye impart of your substance to
the poor ye will do it unto me. Doc. &
Cov. Sec. 42. 30-31.
	Lesson 5th. Word of Wisdom and prepar-
ation of Food.
	1. Health, wisdom and knowledge are
promised to all those who observe the word
of wisdom and the destroying angel shall
pass them by. Doc. & Cov. Sec. 89.
Dan. 1: 6-21 inclusive. 2. The word of
Wisdom was given first as a principle with
a promise, but since that time it has been
given as a commandment. 3. Show the
injury and disadvantage resulting from non-
observance of this commandment. 4. Food
should be clean, wholesome, and well
cooked. 5. Avoid the use of fermented
liquors and strong spices in seasoning lest
an appetite be created in the youth for
strong drink.
	Lesson 6th. Review Entire Lessons.
	LOUISA B. BENSON, President, HANNAH
MICKELSEN, LUCY D PARKINSON Coun-
selors. ELSIE B. ALDER, Sec. and Treas-
urer. PHEBE A. M. MCNEIL, Asst. Secre-
tary-
			MORGAN STAKE.
	Lecture I. The Human Body.
	(a) The human body the home of
thought. (b) Woman’s aim a noble one
in life. (c) The human heart the pulse
beat of life. (d) A perfect being the
noblest work of God. (e) We hear no
sound of hammer but the building never
ceases.
		We are building every day,
		In a good or evil way,
		And the building as it grows
		Must our inmost thought disclose;
		Build it well whate’er you do,
		Build it straight, and strong, and true;
		Build it high, add deep, and broad
		Build it for the eye of God.
	Lecture II.
	“The world was sad, the garden was a wild,
and man, the hermit sighed ‘til woman smiled.”
	(a) Nature has no secrets. (b) How
to avoid transgressing the laws of being.
(c) Know thyself. (d) What beauty de-
pends upon. (e) Result of ill health. (f)
The mother’s influence. (g) How to

[Column 3]
avoid base thoughts. (h) The mother as
companion to the daughter. (i) The dan-
ger that besets girls. (j) Motherhood a
woman’s sweetest joy.
	“Men are more eloquent than women made
	But women are more powerful to pursuade.”
	Lecture III. Our Boys and Girls. The
Fathers and Mothers of the next Generation.
“Ah happy years who would not be a child again.”
	(a) Childhood, its joys and trials. (b)
Good seed brings forth good fruit. “As
we sow we reap’. (c) Every organ per-
forms a certain work. (d) Duties of moth-
ers in instructing sons. (e) Importance
of cleanliness. (f) Foundation of success.
(g) Earnest and living truths. (h) Plenty
of pure air and sleep the best medicine for
children. (i) Choice of profession or trade.
(j) Self sacrifice.
		“When you meet a ragged urchin,
		Standing wistful in the street
		Without hat and kneeless trousers
		Dirty face and bare red feet;
		Pass not by the child unheeded,
		Smile upon him. Mark me when
		He’s grown, he,11 not forget it
		For remember boys make men.”
	Lecture IV. Love, courtship and mar-
riage.
	“Man’s love is of man’s life a thing apart
	’Tis woman’s whole existence.”
	(a) Love the source of happiness or
misery. (b) Courtship the mere alphabet
of love. (c) Love and marriage duly ap-
pointed. (d) Woman’s love stronger than
death. (e) Life’s companionship. (f)
Desirable traits of character in opposite sex.
(g) Causes of unhappy marriages. (h)
Preparation for married life — Domestic
duties—Industry and thrift. (i) Sharing
common joys and sorrows. (j) Self cult-
ure and discipline. (k) Gentleness greatly
loved. (1) The art of adaptation.
“How much the wife is dearer than the bride.”
	Lecture V. Advice to the unmarried.
		‘‘Home is not merely four square walls
		Though with pictures hung and gilded
		Home is where affection calls;
		Filled with shrines, the heart hath builded.”
	“A home should be of one’s building—
Man erects the building, woman enshrines
it. Home sweet home.”
	(a) Marrying out of one’s natural sphere.
(b) Domestic happiness. (c) How to
make one’s self worthy of a good husband.
(d) Marriage means parentage. (e) The
enlargement of affections. (f) The little
one in the home. (g) Sweeter as a moth-
er than a wife. (h) Indelible impressions.
“The children like olive plants round about thy
	table.”
Lecture 6. Marriage relations.
	(a) Is marriage a lottery? (b) Expect
not too much from each other. (c) Cold
indifferences. (d) Remedy for discord. (e)
Duties and pleasures of married life. (f) A
woman’s greatest privilege. (g) Home the
kingdom of woman. (h) Self-improvement.
(i) Consecration each to the other.
Lecture 7. Heredity and how overcome. .
	(a) Shall we transmit health or disease?
(b) The universe tends to health rather
than disease. (c) Parental responsibilty.


(d) Home surroundings. (e) Healthful
food. (f) Cheerful conversation. (g) The
physical helped by the mental. (h) Do
children inherit a tende[-]cy to lie and steal?
	i) Improvement for the race.
Lecture 8. Infants, and the care of them,
	A babe in the home, a wellspring of pleasure—
	A child the precious gift of God,
		(a) Culture before birth. (b) Influence
of mother on unborn. (c) Bathing of the
infant. (d) How clothes should be fastened.
(e) Cleanlinese and health. (f) Warmth a
requisite. (g) Diet of an infant. (h) Mis-
cellaneous suggestions.
Lecture 9. Children and their diseases.
		(a) A happy nursery. (b) Terrifying sto-
ries. (c) Nurtured in love. (d) Be a child
among your children. (e) How to spoil a
good temper. (f) Teach by example, (g)
Bathing of children. (h) Dress. (i) Evil
effects of garters. (j) Nourishing food.
(k) Vegetables rather than meat. (1) Sim-
ple remedy for croup, sore throat, measles,
mumps, worms and boils.
Lecture 10. Accidents and their treatment.
		(a) Presence of mind in a fire or other
accidents. (b) Treatment for a scald. (c)
What to do in case of an accident to the
eye. (d) Choking. (e) Sting of a bee.
(f)To remove foreign bodies from the
stomach. (g) Treatment of a bruise, (h)
Treatment of a stunned child. (i) Poisons
and their antidotes. (j) To make a bread
poultice. (k) Taking count of the pulse.
Lecture 11. The sick room.
		(a) Perfect ventilation. (b) Cut flowers
for the sick room. (c) Calmness and self-
possession. (d) Sunny exposure. (e) Avoid
draughts. (f) Clean bed linen. (g) De-
sirable qualities in a nurse. (h) Sunshiny
disposition. (i) When to engage a nurse.
(j) How to sweep the room of the sick.
Lecture 12. Personal beauty.
		(a) Public appearance. (b) How to over-
come imperfections of the body. (c) Influ-
ence of beauty and happiness. (d) Beauti-
ful dress. (e) Expression and brilliancy of
the eye. (f) Care of the teeth. (g) Care
of the hair. (h) How to take care of the
hands. (i) The body’s greatest need.
			MARYETTE R. WALDRON,
						Chairman.
			EMMA S. CLARK,
			ANNA S. DICKSON,
						Committee.
LYDIA RICH, President.
MYRTHA TONKS,
MARY J. WELCH,
			Counselors.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

", "[WomansExponent | Vol32 | 1903 | April | Issue: 11 | Title: MOTHERS' WORK. | Author: Unknown]

MOTHERS' WORK.
PAROWAN STAKE.
Lecture I.—Pre-existence of spirits.
	Sing Hymn, “O My Father.” Give a
talk explaining the doctrine expressed in
the hymn. Speaker read the hymn, as proof
that we have a Father in Heaven. Prove
that all existed with God and His only be-
gotten Son before we came to this earth.
What was our condition in our pre-exis-
tant state?
	References.—John, 1 14. John 16, 28.
John 17th, 5. Heb. 12, 9. Matt. 6. 9. Job
38, 4 7. Num. 27, 16. Ecc 12, 7. John 3,
2. Jer 1, 4-5. Rev. 12, 7-9. As set forth in
“Woman who art thou, What is thy Mis-
sion, By Pres. John Taylor. Added upon.
Jude 6.
	Questions.—1. What proofs have we
that we have a Father and Mother in
Heaven? Read explanation on marriage in
Compendium, also Gen. 1, 7. 2. Had we
developed as thoroughly as we could in the
spirit world? 3. Did we all keep our first
estate?
Lecture 2.—Object of existence.
	(a) Whence we came, (b) Who we are,
(c) Where are we going? (d) Object is to
benefit humanity and to gain eternal life.
	References.—(a) Pearl of Great Price,
page 32. Alma 13, 3-7. Eph. 1, 3-4-5.
Tit. 1, 2. (b) John 3, 2. Heb. 12, 9. (c)
I Nephi, 28, 8. Doc. and Cov. 43, 44-45.
Ecc. 12, 7. Pearl of Great Price, page 22.
Rev. 20, 6. Doc. and Cov. 29, II. (d)
Multiply and replenish the earth. Gen. 1,
28.
	Questions.—Will all get back to our
Father’s home? Doc. and Cov. 29, 9-14-21.
What was the first commandment given to
Adam and Eve? When was it given? How


[Column 1]
can we benefit humanity? How may we
gain eternal life.

Lecture 3.—Divinity of Parents Mission.

	Love and attributes as God planted them
in the human heart for a holy purpose. Mar-
riage a divine covenant for the propagation
of the race. Woman as a helpmate.
	References.—Read articles on marriage
(Com) John 5, 42. Tit. 3, 4. 1 Cor. 11, 11.
Doc. and Cov. Sec. 132. Sec. 49, 15-18.
1 Cor. 11, 3-12. Gen. 2, 24. Doc. and Cov.
Sec. 23. Gen. 2, 18.
	Questions.—Why is love implanted in
the human heart? 2. Is the mutual attrac-
tion of the sexes proper? 3 Enumerate the
ways in which women can be truly help-
mates. 4. When was woman divinely
chosen to be a mother? 5. What is truly
woman’s mission?

[-]ecture 4.

	(a) As mothers in Israel what manner
of women should we be? (b) Very prayer-
ful. (c) A sound mind in a sound body.
(d) Deportment to husband and children.
(e) Seek knowledge from all good sources,
truth, wisdom and the Holy Spirit. (f)
Motherhood a gift from God.
	References.—Doc and Cov. The prayer-
ful life by A. O. Woodruff, Young Wo-
man’s Journal Jan. 1[-]04. Eph. 5 chapter
22-25 vs.
	Questions.—1. What are some of the qual-
ifications necessary to be a true mother in
Israel? 2. Was the 25th Sec of the Doc.
and Cov. given for Emma Smith only? 3.
Should mothers devote their time entirely
to their home? 4. Why is it necessary to
keep our bodies sound and healthy? 5.
Why should we be careful in our deport-
ment around our home? 6. Is Motherhood
a gift from God?

Lecture 5—Prospective Mother.

	What shall we do to have our babies
healthy and happy? A life in harmony
with the gospel is the perfect example.
Discuss the physical needs of the body.
Freedom of dress, out door exercise, clean-
liness, food, fresh air and sunshine. Power
to guide and control oneself. Study well
one’s character before undertaking the grave
responsibility of Motherhood.
	Questions.—1. Give experiences what ef-
fect certain tendencies of the mother have
upon the child. 2. Does our temporal con-
dition effect our children? 3. To what ex-
tent should women exercise?

Lecture VI.

	Mental development, choice of literature.
The social life of the mother Spiritual de-
velopment, following the steps of the Sav-
ior. Parents may have the kind of spirits
they are worthy of.
	Questions.—1. What course should a
mother take in order that her child may
have the proper development? 2. What do
you think of mothers going into society
during this period? 3. Did the Roman and
Grecian nations honor prospective mothers?
4. Were children welcome by the Jewish
nation?

Lecture VII Care of the Infant.

	The baby’s wardrobe. How to care for
the infant. Discuss ventilation and sleep-
ing apartments. The baby’s food, when to
feed and quantity. Baby’s bath, baby’s ills
and how to treat them.
	Questions.—1. How should baby be

[Column 2]
dressed? 2. Why have flannel next to the
skin? Should the clothes be long or short?
3. Do you think a child should be fed at
regular intervals? 4. Why should a child
be bathed and how often? 5. Should a
baby learn to creep before it walks? 6.
Should a child be given water often? 7.
When the mother’s milk is insufficient,
what artificial food do you think best?

Lecture VIII.—First Years of the Child.

	Obedience is heavens first law. As the
twig is bent so the tree inclines. “Too
much indulgence has spoiled many a child,
too much love not one. Love brought the
child into the world and the more love we
give them the more we have for ourselves.”
“Love begets love.” Home is the child’s
world. Keep it uppermost in our
minds. One of the first steps is to gain
the child’s confidence. Habits are formed
in infancy.
	Questions.—What lessons of obedience
should a child learn in the first five years of
its life? 2 Illustrate love and indulgence.
3. In what way would we have more love
if we gave more? 4. Should we gratify the
child’s wishes about pleasure and outings?
5. How can we gain a child’s confidence?
6.What habits are formed in infancy?

Lecture IX.—Physical Development.

	Teach the child proper attitudes. Observ-
ance of the Word of Wisdom is essential to
physical development. The rights of chil-
dren in the home. Pay strict attention to
questions and give suitable answers. To
put children off or to send them away, what
effect does it have on the child’s character
	Questions.—1. What are proper attitudes.
2. How shall we entertain our children in
the home? 3. Should each child have some
part in keeping up the home? 4. What
effect has responsibility on character? 5.
How far should the freedom of a child ex-
tend in the home? 5. Why are idle mo-
ments dangerous to the child?

Lesson X.—Mental, Moral and Social
	Development.

	Overcome evil with good. Encourage
virtue and vice will disappear. Selection of
good books and companions should be
taught early in life. A mother’s influence.
The part a mother takes in the forming of
character cannot be over-estimated. It is a
social duty to be cleanly of speech, person
and dress.

	True politeness is to do and say,
	The kindest thing in the kindest way.

	Questions.—1. How can we overcome
evil with good? 2. Illustrate how we can
encourage virtue? 3. Name some good
books suitable for children of all ages. 4.
llustrate how a mother helps to form a
child’s character. 5. Why should we be
clean of speech, person and dress?

Lecture XI.—Spiritual Development.

	Each day should properly begin with
prayer. Peace, love and contentment come
from within. Christ the true ideal. One
life which embodies all the virtues, should
be made the example in the life of all. Give
special religious training in order that hon-
or for the priesthood may be fostered. Why
should the lesson of obedience be one of the
foundation stones of character building.
How is obedience connected with our spir-
tual progress?
	Questions.—1. Why should each day begin
with prayer? 2. Do favorable temporal sur-

[Column 3]
roundings make us contented and happy?
3. How do parents constantly teach morals
good and bad to their children? 4. How
soon does a child have a moral sense? 5.
What special religious training should be
given in order that children may obey or
honor the Priesthood?

Lecture XII.— The Development of the
	Child to Man and Womanhood.

	Teach the child the sacred laws of life be-
fore he gains the knowledge from evil
minds. Be sure you have his confidence
and then pay strict attention to every need.
Assist the children in choosing suitable
companions and books. During the devel-
opment period the boy and girl should be
tenderly and wisely guarded.
	Questions—Do you think children should
be taught the sacred laws of life, and how
soon? How can we gain their confidence,
and what are their needs? How can we as-
sist our children in choosing their compan-
ions and books? To what extent should
our boys and girls be allowed to participate
in dancing and other exciting pastime?

			DAVIS STAKE.
Lecture I.—Motherhood aud Maternity:—

“O wondrous power! how little understood—
	Entrusted to the mother’s mind alone,
To fashion genius, form the soul for good,
	Inspire a West or train a Washington.”
		(a) How can we best prepare for
	motherhood?
		(b) Observing the Word of Wisdom
	as essential to physical development.
		(c) The Gospel as essential to spirit-
	ual development.
		(d) The development of the mother
	as effecting the unborn child. How
	can we overcome hereditary weak-
	nesses?
		(e) What effect will obedience to the
	moral law have upon our offspring?
	(Refer to St. Matthew i: 24; also Heb. xii: 9;
Rev. 12: 7-17.)
		(f) How does child-bearing effect
	the Latter-day work?
		(g) How does it effect our exalta-
	tion?

Lecture II.—Pre-natal Influences:

	“A pebble in the streamlet scant.
		Has turned the course of many a river,
	A dewdrop on the infant plant.
		Has dwarfed the giant oak forever.”

		(a) How does the physical, moral
	and spiritual development of the moth-
	er effect the unborn child?
		(b) How should we dress during
	gestation?
		(c) Why is plain, nourishing food
	and regular, light exercise essential to
	mother and child?
		(d) What responsibility has the fath-
	er during this period?
		(e) How can he assist in the one
	great mission to be performed—that of
	bringing forth a perfectly developed
	and well-balanced child?
		(f) Compare the amount of character
	building the mother may do during
	this period to that that may be done
	later.

Lecture III—Proper Care of Mother and In-
	fant:

	“Something to live for has come to our place,
		Something to die for, may be;
	Something to give even sorrow a grace,
		A dear little innocent baby.”

		(a) What nourishment is best adap-

[Column 3]
	ted to the mother’s condition?
		(b) Why should she be kept quiet?
		(c) Why is proper care a vital ques-
	tion at this time? 
		(d) Discuss the proper way to give
	baby’s first bath, its clothing, bed and.
	food.
		(e) How does cleanliness promote
	health?
		(f) Why should a baby have its
	wants anticipated?
		(g) Why is it best to teach it to go
	to sleep naturally?
		(h) Give practical illustrations of the
	effect of habit upon the new born babe.
		(i) Discuss the nursing period and
	how it effects the child’s future life.
		(j) How should we care for the
	child during teething?
		(k) How and when shall we wean
	our babies?

Lecture IV.— The Infant Child and its Early
	Training:

“A little bit of patience often makes the sunshine
	come,
And a little bit of love makes a very happy home.”

		(a) In what order are the senses
	developed?
		(b) In what order are the emotions
	developed?
		(c) How can these developments be
	used in the early training of the child?
		(d) Of what value are play moments
	in a child’s life?
		(e) Discuss the motto, “learn by
	doing.”
		(f) How soon does a child have a
	moral sense?
		(g) Why is firmness so essential in
	the training of the child?
		(g) When and how may obedience
	be cultivated—unselfishness, gentle-
	ness, kindness and truthfulness?
		(i) What effect has the parent’s self-
	government upon the government of
	the child?
		(j) Of what value are the first three
	years of the child’s life in the building
	of his character.

Lecture V.—Obedience—Necessity of its
	Early Enforcement:

“O’er wayward childhood, wouldst thou hold firm
		rule,
	And sun thee in the light of happy faces,
Love, Hope and Patience—these must be thy
		graces.
	And in thine own heart let them first keep
		school,”

		(a) Why should the lesson of obed-
	ience be one of the foundation stones
	of character building?
		(b) How will this principle effect
	the life of a Latter-day Saint through
	time and all eternity?
		(c) How is it connected with our
	spiritual progress?
		(d) How should obedience be
	taught?
	(Refer to Eph. vi: 1-9; I Saml. xv: 22; Juvenile
Instructor June 15, 1903, page 371.)

Lecture VI.—Childhood and its Proper Direc-
	tion:

	“He who checks a child with terror,
		Stops its play and stills its song,
	Not alone commits an error;
		But a grievous moral wrong.”

		(a) How do both paren[-]s constantly
	teach morals good and bad to their
	children?


[Column 1]	
	(b) How does a child get his first
ideas of God’s love, truth and justice?
	(c) Can extravagance at home teach
dishonesty?
	(d) Should discourtesy to children
be the rule at home?
	(e) Why should children have set
tasks and light responsibilities within
the home?
	(f) When should a child be permitted
social life outside the family?
	(g( Does the judgment of a child
develop early? Should he rule in the
home?
(Refer to Doc. Cov. Sec. 68, 25 verse; Juvenile
Instructor July 1, 1903.)
Lecture VII.—Child's Place in the Home:
“Gather them close to your loving heart,
	Cradle them on your breast,
They will soon enough leave your brooding care,
Soon enough mount youth’s topmost stair,
	Little ones in the nest.”
	(a) Should each child have some
part in keeping up the home?
	(b) What effect has responsibility on
character?
	(c) What is the effect of not feeling
one’s self a necessary element in the
home?
	(d) Why should mother and child be
united, and their interests be one?
	(e) How far should the freedom of
the child extend within the home?
	(f) Why should a child’s rights be
determined by obedience to law?
(Refer to Doc. and Cov. Sec. 83, verse 4.)
Lecture VIII.—Necessity of Retaining the
	Child’s Confidence:
	“Oh, what a tangled web we weave,
	When first we practice to deceive.’’
	(a) Why should fear be avoided in
the government of the young?
	(b) Define faith in God (Doc. and
Cov.)
	(c) Define faith or confidence in
father and mother.
	(d) Why should a mother listen to
the child’s experiences of the day.
	(e) Explain the danger of practicing
deception in the governing of children.
	(f) How does a parent’s self-gov-
ernment strengthen the confidence of
the child?
Lecture IX.—Employment and Occupation of
	Our Children:
	“Work for some good, be it ever so slowly
	Labor! all labor is noble and holy.”
	(a) Why is activity so essential in
the young?
	(b) How and by whom should it be
directed?
	(c) Why is reading to and story tell-
ing beneficial in the training of the
child?
	(d) Why is the study of music bene-
ficial, and at what age should it begin?
	(e) When should the child be given
tasks to perform?
	(f) When should he share responsi-
bility?
	(g) Why are the idle moments of
danger to the child?
(Refer to Doc. and Cov., Sec. 42, verse 42.)
Lecture X.—Our Girls and Boys—the Fath-
ers and Mothers of the Next Generation:

[Column 2]
“Oh ! thou child of many prayers.
Life hath quicksands—life hath snares,
Care and age come unawares.”
	(a) What special teaching should
boy receive and when?
	(b) How should we teach our daugh-
ters?
	(c) Dwell upon the mission of both.
	(d) Should boys and girls be allowed
to associate together?
	(e) Show the evil effect of an excess
of social life to our boys and girls.
	(f) Also show the evils of late hours
to their physical; and moral develop-
ment.
	(g) In what way do our hopes cen-
ter upon our boys and girls?
Lecture XI.—The Law of Chastity:
“A mother’s holiest task is to keep
	Her darlings pure and true,
Her constant care, her watchful prayer.
	Alone can guide them through.'
	(a) Give the true definition of chas-
tity.
	(b) How and where can personal
purity and chastity best be taught?
	(c) How can we as mothers best
avoid impure thoughts within our chil-
dren?
	(d) How can the home influence
and conversation promote purity of
heart?
	(e) What are the effects of breaking
these laws among us as a people to-
day?
(Refer to Juvenile Instructor, November 15,
1902, page 688; Matt. v: 8)
Lecture XII.—Marriage— Object and Sacred-
	ness of:
	“And again I say unto you that who so forbid-
deth to marry is not ordained of God, for marriage
is ordained of God unto man.” (Doc. and Cov.
Sec. 49, verse 15.)
	(a) What is the object of marriage?
(Gen. i: 28.)
	(b) How should we prepare for mar-
riage? (Doc. and Cov. Sec. 132, 15-66
verses.)
	(c) What are the existing evils re-
garding this covenant today?
	(d) How can we as Latter-days best
overcome these evils?

Nellie C. Taylor, Wealthy R. Clark,
Athalia M. Stead, Maria Clark, Committee.
Susan Grant, President Relief Society Davis
Stake.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


[Column 1]
"]

["[WomansExponent | Vol25 | 1896 | December | Issue: 11 | Title: LADIES’ SEMI-MOMTHLY MEETING. | Author: singing “Come, come ye Saints;”]

LADIES’ SEMI-MOMTHLY MEETING.

	MINUTES of Ladies’ Meeting held in the
14th ward Assembly hall Oct. 31, 1896,
President M. I. Horne presiding. Opened
by singing “Come, come ye Saints;”
prayer by Counselor E. Webb; continued
by singing “O, ye mountains high.”
President M. I. Horne thanked the sisters
for their faith and prayers, felt they had
weight with her Heavenly Father in restor-
ing her to health, gave good counsel to the
sisters to rise and bear their testimonies, for
all would be strengthened thereby. Spoke
at some length on the discernment of
spirits and urged the sisters to be watchful
and prayerful. “Find fault with none but
ourselves; we have no right to judge
another. Attach our faith to no person or
we might be overcome; trust to the Lord
and all will be well with us.”
	Sister Margaret Brown made a few happy
and encouraging remarks concerning the
Gospel and the lives of the Saints.
	Counselor E. Webb referred to Sister
Kimball speaking on the subject of Faith,
and spoke at some length in that respect,
and also of prayer and the work for the
dead, and urged the sisters to attend to this
part of our religion.
	Sister Frances Smith spoke of the work
in the Temple aad how she enjoyed herself
therein, and prayed for Sister Horne and all
faithful sisters.
	Sister Elizabeth Paul spoke of Temple
work and wished she was better so that she
could work still more in that holy house;
also spoke at some length on the subject of
speaking ill of each other.
	Sister Louisa Stevenson spoke also on
Temple work for the dead, also alluded to
being healed by the administration of her
young son.
	Sister Julia C. Howe said as regards
Saints, “By their works ye should know
them.” Urged the sisters to cultivate
Faith and spoke of the revelation in the
Doctrine and Covenants on this subject.
Felt that she could prophesy that Sister
Horne would live for many, many years yet.
Made other encouraging remarks.
	Sister Davis spoke of the faith of her
brother who is on a mission, and how well
he had been received where he was.
	Sister Jane James felt to bear her testi-

[Column 2]
mony and rejoiced that she had beheld the
Prophet and Patriarch Joseph and Hyrum
Smith, and wished she could go into the
Temple; but she felt to wait in patience on
her Heavenly Father. Prayed to be faith-
ful unto the end and alluded to the time she
embraced the Gospel, and how she rejoiced
even until today in the same.
	Sister Julia Samson spoke of the privileges
of the Saints both in Sunday school and
meetings. Felt thankful for all the bless-
ing she had received at her Heavenly
Father’s hands, and bore her testimony to
the truth of the Gospel.
	Sister Jones bore her testimony to the
truth of the Gospel. Wondered where the
sisters were who used to come to these
meetings. “Surely they have not stepped
aside! I pray for all our sisters in the
name of Jesus.”
	Sister Waterfall spoke of the pleasure it
gave her to be able to come to meeting
again, and told of a vision she had when
she was ill. One whom she saw said “We
are watching you;” also bore her testimony
to the truth of the Gospel.
	Sister Mary Silver encouraged the sisters
to be faithful and true.
	Sister Sarah Goss spoke of the healing of
her little daughter by the faith of herself
and of others, and also of the healing of
her little son and Sister Jones’ mother.
Said she had many blessings by being here
and thanked her Heavenly Father that she
had heard and received the Gospel.
	President M. I. Horne made closing re-
marks; thought she would like us to con-
tinue the subject of Faith next meeting.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks.
Closed by singing “ ’Mid scenes of con-
fusion.” Benediction by L. D. Alder.
	LYDIA D. ALDER, Sec. pro tem.

	MINUTES of Ladies’ Semi-Monthly
Meeting held in the 14th ward Assembly
Hall, Saturday, November 14th 1896, Pres.
M. I. Horne presiding. Singing “For the
strength of the hills;” prayer by Coun-
selor Elizabeth Webb; singing “Welcome
best of all good meetings.” The minutes of
the previous meeting were read and approv-
ed. President Horne felt truly thankful
to meet her sisters today, and wished the
sisters to continue speaking on the subject
of Faith for it was Faith that prompted us
to do everything that is good; faith that
brought us the desires of our hearts. “The
humble and the faithful ones are the ones
that will reap the blessings. We have no
time to criticise the actions of the author-
ties.” If they did wrong or were led
astray the Lord would remove them; urged
the sisters to be guarded in their speeches
and prayed God to bless us.
	Counselor E. J. Stevenson spoke of her
gratitude in having the privilege of meeting
with the sisters and said it was twelve
weeks since she had enjoyed this treat; re-
ferred to her husband’s illness and said that
through faith and the administration of the
Elders he had recovered. Felt to rejoice in
this. Spoke very earnestly on faith and
prayed we might have a time of rejoicing
here together.
	Sister Waterfall made some excellent re-
marks on Faith, and told how the Lord
had opened up her way before her in a very
remarkable manner. Prayed the Lord to
bless us all. 
	Sister Lydia D. Alder though not feeling
well, felt to bear her testimony; urged
the sisters to cultivate the Spirit of God


[Column 1]
and guard against losing it.; felt that fault
finding was the first thing towards it.
Prayed that our faith might increase and
that we might always be in possession of
the Spirit of God.
	Dr. E. S. Barney said it was many years
since she had spoken in this building, and
spoke on faith, bringing up passages of
scripture to explain the meaning and told
of the inspiration she enjoyed one morning
on arising, and which she was compelled
to write. Made some very excellent and
encouraging remarks, and bore a faithful
testimony. Testified that the Spirit of
God was in this room, and prayed the
Lord to bless us all and increase our faith.
	Sister Julia Samson felt she must bear
her testimony. Said her heart was ready
to burst with joy to think we were number-
ed with the Church of the “First Born,”
and felt she could shout. Urged the sisters
to be united and have faith; for we must
cultivate it. Bore a strong testimony.
	Counselor Annie T. Hyde was pleased
to meet with her sisters, said she had been
deprived of this privilege for some time;
also spoke upon faith. Felt it was faith
that sustained us in our trials; in our
prayers we should always say “Not my
will O, God be done, but Thine.” Her
remarks on faith were beautiful. Prayed
that we might have faith that we could
know there was a God who watched over
us and heard us at all times.
	Sister Sarah M. Kimball made some ex-
cellent remarks on faith, and spoke of the
WOMAN'S EXPONENT and felt that it was
not sustained as it should be. It was a
very excellent little paper and was a wo-
man’s paper and should be sustained by the
women of Utah at least. Urged the sisters
to do better in the future, and refered to the
remarks of Sister Alder on the resurrection,
she also had heard Joseph Smith say that
when the keys of the resurrection were
given it would be so simple that we would
wonder we had not seen it before. Prayed
the Lord to bless us all.
	Sister Jane James bore a faithful testi-
mony and said she had been terribly afflict-
ed in her head, and she took her consecrated
oil and anointed herself and she was heal-
ed. Felt that that was faith, and praised
the Lord for her blessings.
	Sister Barrow told how she had anointed
her children with oil and blessed them, and
they recovered; also spoke of the gift of
wisdom and discernment; said she had
always been blest with faith. Pra[-]ed
the Lord to help us to overcome evil and
have our faith increased. Testified God
heard and answered prayers when He was
sought in faith. The speaker finished by
speaking in tongues, which was interpreted
by Dr. E. S. Barney.
	Sister Elizabeth Paul felt there had never
been a greater flow of the Spirit in this
building than now. Told how she had
been blest by going to the Temple. Spoke
on faith, and felt there was a great day at
hand. Prayed we might be able to endure.
	President Horne made closing remarks.
Felt we had had a feast of fat things. Sis-
ter Horne spoke of the EXPONENT; said
there was no paper that had done the good
and allayed the prejudice that that little
paper had done. The price of it was very
low, and in the reach of all. Urged the
sisters to do all they could in this direction,
and speak of it in their Relief Society meet-
ings; also spoke of the Woman’s Store
and the competent help in there now, and

[Column 2]
hoped the sisters would patronize them.
Felt these were temporal things to speak
of, but we had to mix the temporal with
the spiritual. Urged the sisters to induce
and persuade more of the sisters to come to
these meetings; she felt they were becom-
ing better all the time.
	Adjourned for two weeks. Singing Dox-
ology, benediction by Sister J. C. Howe.
", '[WomansExponent | Vol25 | 1896 | Unknown | Issue: 14 | Title: LADIES’ SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING. | Author: Unknown]

LADIES’ SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	HELD in the 14th ward Assembly Hall,
Jan. 8 ’97, Counselor Elizabeth Webb pre-
siding until President Horne arrived.
Singing “Once more we come before our
Lord;” prayer by Sister Clara C. Cannon;
continued by singing “All hail the new
born year;” minutes of last meeting read
and accepted. Pres’t. M. Isabella Horne
wished all the sisters a Happy New Year.
Last year we lost four of our good, true,
faithful saints, Sister Ellen C. Clawson,
Sister Helen Mar Whitney, Sister Hannah
Rawlins Pres’t. Draper and Sister Mary A.
Hazen Pres’t. of Brighton Society. “Let
us put our trust in our Father in heaven,
for we are all weak and have to wage war
against sin.” Spoke of our late conference,
said we had an excellent conference.
“Satan gathers his forces here where the
priesthood is; he has great power, places
stumbling blocks in our way and evil sug-
gestions, but everything good comes from
our Father. We are in the midst of trials
today, but God has given a key-word to be
faithful and humble in prayer. Let us
look over our actions each night and thus
strengthen ourselves day by day. We
must serve Him and keep His command-
ments; if we fail in this life we will fail in
the next; He scourges every son and every
daughter whom He receives. I can testify
that Joseph was a prophet of God, and his
blood was shed as a testimony to the world
of the truth of his mission. I desire to be
faithful to the calling where unto we have
been called.”
	Pres’t. Zina D. H. Young said “It is
over twenty-seven years since Pres’t. Brig-
ham Young organized these meetings and I
am pleased to see you, my sisters; we come
here and learn to love each other, and feast
on the good words of peace and salvation.

[Column 2]
I received a testimony of the truth of this
work before I was baptized and I have
never doubted its truth.” Spoke of the
falling away in the days of Kirtland, when
six of the twelve forsook Joseph. “O, my
sisters I have seen the sorrow of those who
have fallen by the way; the results of deny-
ing the faith are so terrible. I feel to bear
my testimony to the truth of this work, and
the truth of celestial marriage. I thank
my heavenly Father that the light has been
given; it is nothing to die if we have been
faithful. Keep free from finding fault and
you shall be blessed. May we meet here
for years and years to come, and be faithful
in all things.”
	Sister Laura Miner said, “I can say I
have never doubted the truth of this Gos-
pel.” Spoke of the Relief Society work in
Logan and made other encouraging re-
marks.
	Another sister bore an excellent testi-
mony to the truth of the Gospel. Sister
Margaret Brown spoke of her labors in the
Temple and desired to go on in such glori-
ous principles. Sister Julia Samson spoke
of the many things we had to make us re-
joice, and hoped we would be stronger in
another year. Sister Caroline C. R. Wells,
bore her testimony that the Gospel was true
and she had never doubted. A sister in
the congregation also bore a strong testi-
mony. Sister Nunn bore her testimony to
the truth of this work and the power of the
priesthood, and also the power of dreams.
	The sisters sang “Guide us O, thou
Great Jehovah.”
	Sister E. B. Wells thought it beautiful to
hear the sisters bear their testimonies,
spoke of the WOMEN’S EXPONENT; Pres’t.
Young was anxious that the sisters should
sustain their own paper. The women of
of this church should not let this paper fail,
spoke earnestly and impressively; spoke
about the silk, said the Commission was
appointed and twenty-five cents a pound
bounty is sure to you on every pound; also
spoke, of the kindergarten, also that we
should pray that the right one
should be sent down to Washington as
Senator. This matter concerns us; let us
pray about this matter that the Lord will
choose him. “I pray the Lord will bless
us.”
	Pres’t. Horne made closing remarks.
Urged the sisters to sustain the EXPONENT,
read from it at their meetings and sustain
it. Meeting adjourned for two weeks.
Singing “Come let us anew;” benediction
L. D. Alder.
		L. D. ALDER, Sec. pro tem.

	Minutes of Ladies’ Semi-Monthly Meet-
ing held in the 14th ward Hall, Jan. 23,
1897, Sister Lydia D. Alder presiding.
Opened by singing “Come, come ye
Saints;" prayer by Sister Julia C. Howe
singing “Welcome best of all good meet
ings.” Sister Alder made some remarks,
urging the sisters to faithfulness, if we only
proved humble and prayerful. She hoped
the sisters that were wont to preside would
find that we had enjoyed a goodly portion
of the Holy Spirit.
	A sister in the congregation bore a faith-
ful testimony, hoped we would be careful
in what we had to say. Sister Morris sang
“Redeemer of Israel, ” also bore her testi-
mony; she had never seen the day she did
not rejoice in this work.
	Dr. E. S. Barney also made some re-
marks, said she knew this was the work of


God and the Spirit of the Lord was with
us, we should do better every day, if we
could not hear we could read and think,
thereby obtaining the Spirit of God.
Read some remarks made by Henry Ward
Beecher.
	Sister Mary P. Silver bore her testimony,
said she had always known this was the
work of God. A Sister in the congrega-
tion bore a testimony to the truth of what
Sister Barney had said, also spoke of bless-
ngs we derived from prayer.
	Sister Nunn spoke of her labors in the
Temple, of seeing her aunt whom she was
working for, and the many blessings she
was the recipient of day by day, also the
blessings derived by fasting and prayer.
	A sister in the congregation bore a very
faithful testimony, said she felt to rejoice in
the Gospel.
	Sister Young felt to rejoice in the Gos-
pel, had been in the Church forty-nine
years, desired us to pray for her sister who
was sick, prayed the Lord to bless us all.
	Sister Sarah Phelps also spoke of the
truth of the Gospel in this our day, she felt
we ought to pray to the Lord more ferver-
ently than we had ever done before.
	Sister Silver sang “How firm a founda-
tion.” Sister Wilkins felt pleased that she
had the privilege of meeting with us.
	Sister Mina Hardy bore a faithful testi-
mony. Another sister felt to rejoice but
felt she had a testimony to bear, and knew
we could call down the blessings of heaven
if we would only ask for them.
	Sister Mary A. Hardy also bore a faithful
testimony.
	Sister Held knew if she did not say a few
words she would feel condemned. Singing
“We thank Thee O, God.” Sister B. W.
Smith knew the Prophet Joseph was a true
prophet; she asked the Lord to bless our
young sisters who had spoken in our midst,
prayed for our aged prophet Brother Wood-
ruff.
	Pres’t Horne having arrived made some
remarks, was pleased to see so many
present, thought there was signs of a re-
vival, thought we should have to retire to
the Assembly Hall; she had often thought
of the Lord’s bringing forth this work
through his servants and handmaidens,
that the Lord knew whom he was choosing.
“Let us set an example before our young
sisters.” Sister Horne said she had been
attending a meeting ever since eleven
o’clock this morning. She hoped the sis-
ters would continue to come to these meet-
ings. The first patriarchal blessing she re-
ceived she was told she would have a great
deal of sickness which she had passed
through; she also had a letter read, after
which the meeting adjourned for two
weeks. Singing Doxology; benediction by
Sister E. S. Barney.
	MARTHA BARROW, Sec. pro. tem.

', '[WomansExponent | Vol26 | 1897 | February | Issue: 17 | Title: LADIES SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING. | Author: Unknown]

LADIES SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	MINUTES of the Ladies Semi-monthly
meeting held in the 14th ward Assembly
hall Saturday Feb. 19, 1898, President M.
I. Horne presiding. Singing “O say what
is truth.” Prayer by Counselor E. Webb.
Singing “How will the Saints rejoice to
tell.” Minutes of the previous meeting
read and accepted.
	President Horne quoted the saying of the
Savior that “If ye are not one ye are not
mine.” She thought the Saints should all
be united in everything they attempt to do
and said we all had a portion of the Spirit
of God, that we brought it with us when
we left the Lord’s presence: it was this that
prompted us to do any good thing and it
remained with us whether we used it to do
good or not. Said it took bravery to be a
Latter-day Saint for we had much to over-
come, but when we kept His command-
ments no good thing either in heaven or on
earth would be witheld from us. She felt
we were indeed a blessed people. In a little
company recently where President Woodruff
was present she heard him say that we
should strive to do the works of Latter-day
Saints, that we would not fall by the way-
side, Urged those present to do those
works and talked very plainly to the Saints.
	Sister Annie O. Brown said she felt it
was necessary to do the works of God, and
knew we should feel like brothers and sis-
ters to one another. The Gospel was every-
thing to her, bore a strong testimony and
felt that this was all she lived for.
	Sister Lydia D. Alder referred to the ter-
rible things happening in the world of
which we read, also of the signs of the times.
She felt we could not help asking ourselves
the question “Will we be able to stand the
test?” We could only trust in God; when
we were in trouble it was then He was the
nearest to us. Spoke of the condition of the
world today, said that people scarcely had
any faith in one another and very little in
God; they feared greatly something but did
not know what; knew this was one of the
signs of the times.
	Sister Carlhurst bore her testimony to
the truth of tithing, and told how by pay-
ing her tithing she was greatly blest.
	Sister Margaret P. Young said she en-
joyed these meetings and the sweet spirit

[Column 2]
that always attended the meetings of the
Saints She realized that God had been
good to her and had ever had a watch-care
over her. She had never felt like drawing
away from Him. Spoke of the life and
works of Elder Erastus Snow. He lived at
her father’s home for some time, had en-
joyed the Gospel then very much as also
now. Said she did not care to be the one
to say she had never had a trial.
	Sister Cottrell said she thought we should
not speak of trials when we were permitted
to come here and attend these meetings and
enjoy the sweet spirit which was here.
She had been reading about some of the
ancient martyrs who had been so cruelly
persecuted for their religion, and could not
help thinking what a blessed people we
were that we could enjoy our religion to
the extent we did; bore a strong testimony.
	Sister Barrow said there was qot a day
when she did not thank God for the bless-
ings she enjoyed and wondered how any
one could doubt, especially when we could
see the prophecies of the prophets of God
being so rapidly fulfilled. Prayed the Lord
to bless our aged sisters, that they might be
spared to us for many years yet to instruct
and encourage us.
	Sister Rawlings said she had been very
ill but was healed by the power of faith, for
which she was very grateful. A sister in
the congregation also bore her testimony to
the power of healing.
	Sister Barnes felt thankful for our dear
aged sisters, Sister Horne and others, who
had been such a blessing and comfort to us.
Spoke of her native country Scotland, said
she loved it, yet she was glad to leave it to
come to Zion. Singing “The Spirit of God
like a fire is burning.”
	Sister Annie T. Hyde felt we had had a
treat in listening to the beautiful testi-
monies of the sisters, and knew we were
strengthened by this. She thought with
Sister Horne that the Spirit of the Lord was
being poured out upon the sisters, and al-
luded to the signs of the times, felt there
was a very eventful time before us. The
mothers should be careful what they said
before their children, that their faith
might not be weakened but strengthened.
Prayed that we might not make much of
our trials but put our trust in God.
	Dr. E. S. Barney said she thought we
should learn to sift ourselves and throw out
the chaff. Spoke on humility and the
signs of the times. Said we must not for-
get our God or He would forget us. When
we get discouraged just throw off the humor
and rest and then with the help of the Lord
we would go on again refreshed.
	Sister Mary Silver said she felt glad that
she was a Latter-day Saint, and she was also
glad and proud that we had a sister like
Sister Horne to help and advise us. Said
we never knew her to tell us anything that
was not true, and spoke on the word of
wisdom, knew there was peace for those
who practiced it.
	Sister Hannah Wright thought it was
good to devote a little of our time to meet-
ings, said the more time we gave to the
Lord the better it was for us, there was no
enjoyment like serving the Lord. She had
heard Brother Woodruff say, “How very
few of the millions that are in the world
have embraced the Gospel and how few of
the Saints are really living their religion.”
She felt pleased and grateful to see so many
here. Said sometimes she wished the Gos-
pel had come to her earlier in life, but was

[Column 3]
consoled by having a brother say to her
that it came in just the right time.
	Singing “Be it my only wisdom here.”
President Horne had been reminded to-
day of a saying of Sister Eliza R. Snow that
“a good meeting was like a picnic where
each one brought something different that
was good.” It was so with the testimonies
this afternoon. Said when this building
would not hold all who came, we would
gladly adjourn to the Assembly hall.
	Adjourned for two weeks. Singing
“Satan’s empire long has flourished.”
Benediction by Dr. E. S. Barney.

	Minutes of the Ladies’ Semi-monthly
meeting held in the 14th ward Assembly
hall Saturday, March 5, 1898, President
M. I. Horne presiding. Singing “Jesus the
Giver.” Prayer by Sister Maria W. Wil-
cox. Singing “Where the voice of friend-
ship’s heard.” Minutes of the previous
meeting were read and accepted.
	President Horne requested the sisters to
be more united in their votes, she would like
to see every hand raised when a vote was
taken. Spoke strongly on fault finding,
especially with our brethren and the author-
ities, said the Lord was not pleased with us
when this was the case and urged the sis-
ters to unite their forces to quell this. Said
that there was nothing would give us the
joy that knowing we had served the Lord
would, and that there would be a place in
our Father’s kingdom for us. Fault find-
ing was not serving the Lord, neither does
it bring us peace or pleasure; it was the
humble and the meek who had the Spirit
of the Lord. She regretted that some of
our young people were married outside the
Temple. Knew this was a day of choosing
as well as warning, spoke strongly on the
signs of the times.
	Sister Holling knew by the minutes that
we must have had a beautiful meeting last
time, and felt the older she grew the strong-
er was her desire to serve the Lord.
	Dr. E. S. Barney said she had been fifty-
four years in learning obedience, that if we
lived right we were entitled to the prompt-
ings of the Holy Spirit. Asked how many
had had their Patriarchial blessings and
urged those who did not have them to get
them; said that they would be a source of
comfort. Spoke on the signs of the times,
also on young people being married right.
	A sister from Cache Valley said she felt
impressed to bear her testimony. She was
glad to see Sister Zina D. H. Young here,
and felt to say God bless her. Said many
times had she put her hands upon her head
and blessed her, also had heard her speak in
tongues many times.
	Singing “You are called to labor.” Sis-
ter Margaret P. Young said she never re-
membered of doubting the truth of the Gos-
pel, she had always been a teacher in the
Relief Society and enjoyed her labors.
Sister Mary A. Hardy felt thankful for the
good women whom we had in our midst,
	Sisters Young and Horne, to comfort and
advise us. We knew the Lord was not
pleased with us when we indulged in fault-
finding and evil speaking against the Lord’s
anointed.
	President Zina D. H. Young said that
while looking around on the faces of her
sisters she was reminded of the Savior’s
words, that those who feared the Lord met
often together and spoke one to another,
“And they will be mine when I come to
make up my jewels.” “Just think of it


my sisters, by coming here and bearing our
testimonies and speaking one to another we
can be His. It is a simple thing to do but
means much.” Said she felt sorry for any
woman who spoke against the revelations
of God. Singing “Our God we raise to
Thee.”
	Sister Elizabeth Eangton said she knew
the Spirit of the Lord was in this meeting
in great abundance, she had been greatly
blessed by the Lord, had been enabled to
send money to her husband who was on a
mission and also to gather a great amount
of genealogy. Said she had been forty
years in the Church, and felt greatly blest
in being able to say this.
	Sister Emma Nunn said she and her little
girl had both been very sick but had been
healed by the power of God. Had prom-
ised the Lord she would speak of His good-
ness to her this afternoon.
	Sister McMaster bore her testimony, felt
if she went away without doing this she
would be condemned. Spoke of her Relief
Society work and how she enjoyed it. She
knew it was the work of God.
	Sister Sarah Phelps made some beautiful
remarks, also spoke in tongues.
	Sister Derrick spoke of her recent be-
reavement in the death of her husband, and
alluded to his many virtues. Told how
suddenly he was taken, that he was almost
changed in the twinkling of an eye bore a
strong testimony. 
	A sister in the congregation said she was
thankful that we had a place like
this where we could meet together and sing
and pray and comfort one another.
	President Horne felt the meeting had
been very enjoyable. Gave out the notice
of Stake conference two weeks from today.
	Adjourned for four weeks. Singing
“This God is the God we adore.” Bene-
diction by Counselor E. Webb.
']
