WATCHMAN, WHAT OF THE NIGHT?
	During the long cycles of time, that are
buried in the past, this question has been
asked, by those, who as the prophet Malachi
states it, “Seek the Lord.”
	By him we are also told, that “He will
send his messenger before his face, even the
messenger of the covenant.” Also “that he
will suddenly come to his temple.” Then
the question is asked “But who shall abide
the day of his coming.”
	That this could not have referred to his
first coming is clear; for then he did not ap-
pear suddenly, but as all other infants, he
lay on his mother’s bosom, encircled by her
loving arms, while the wise men worshipped
him, bestowing precious gifts upon him.
	He grew to boyhood and manhood as
other children do. This prophecy therefore,
did not refer, to Christ’s coming in the me-
ridian of time.

[Column 2]
	In Matt. XXIV we read that the minds of
the Apostles were troubled about the future,
and when he sat upon the Mount of Olives,
they came to him privately, asking him to
explain the things he had been teaching
them, saying “Tell us when shall these
things be? What shall be the sign of thy
coming, and the end of the world?”
	4) And Jesus answered and said unto
them, Take heed that no man deceive you.
	5) For many shall come in my name,
saying I am Christ, and shall deceive many.
	6) And ye shall hear of wars and rumors
of wars; see that ye be not troubled, for all
these things must come to pass, but the end
is not yet.
	7) For nation shall rise against nation,
and kingdom against kingdom, and there
shall be famines and pestilence; earthquakes
in divers places.
	13) But he that shall endure unto the
end, the same shall be saved.
	14) And this gospel of the kingdom, shall
be preached in all the world as a witness
unto all nations, then shall the end come.
	That the world would not be looking for
his coming, he plainly foreshadows, in the
parable of the Ten Virgins, with which he
illustrates his previous teachings. He ever
tried to familiarize their minds with the
fact that he should go away, but that he
would come again.
	His ignominious death follows. Suspended
between heaven and earth, on the dreadful
cross, he cries “It is finished,” or his mortal
career ended, and gave up the ghost.
	This is followed, by the unmistakable
signs, of the anger of an offended God, that
his Only Begotten Son, had been cruelly put
to death. His anguish is shown by earth-
quakes and darkness.
	The veil of the Temple is rent in twain,
bearing record, that with all its glories, it is
rejected as the house of God; that from
henceforth he no longer will visit them in
the holy of holies, nor heed their petitions.
Of what avail their boast of being the chil-
dren of Abraham, or possessing the Book of
the Mosaic Law.”
	An overwhelming fear falls on all those,
who took part in the tragic scene, blanching
their faces and chilling their hearts.
	Behold! Death is victor, the grave hath
won; the powers of hell hath triumphed.
		Watchman, what of the night?
	Darkness on the earth, darkness on her
children. Comes there no light?
	Lo! the risen Lord, appears unto the af-
frighted Mary, who becomes the first am-
bassador, of the Resurrection.
	From the door of the sepulchre, angels
have rolled the stone away, and the Prince
of Life, again stands upon the earth.
	The clouds of inky darkness flee, before
the illuminating rays, of a resurrection
morn. The graves of the righteous are un-
sealed, and walking into the Holy City, they
are seen of many. The risen Lord and his
Saints are ransomed from the power of
death.
	Watchman, what of the night? Seeest
thou aught? A glorious ascension! In the
sight of his chosen Apostles, the Mediator
between God and man, is taken up to
heaven. Missionary blessings and instruc-
tions he has left with them, with strict in-
junction, that they shall tarry in Jerusalem,
until the Holy Ghost shall be come upon
them, and they shall be endued with power
from on high; then he becomes invisible to
their sight.
	Still looking steadfastly unto heaven, they
see two men standing clothed in white ap-


[Column 1]
parel, who said “Why stand ye gazing up
into heaven, this same Jesus, which is taken
up from you, into heaven, shall come in
like maimer, as ye have seen him go into
heaven.”
		Watchman, what of the night?
	The Lord of life ascended; the promised
Holy Ghost not come. Now shall the dis-
ciples of the Bridegroom fast and pray, for
he has departed from them, and the cold
dark clouds of absence, fall over them.
Now shall the Bride mourn, watching and
praying for the lost one’s return.
	But over the city of David, dawns fair, a
Pentecostal Day. The believers gather with
one accord in one place. When, lo! a sound
from heaven, as of rushing wind, fills the
house.
	And there sat on each one of them, as it
were, cloven tongues of fire, and they were
filled with the Holy Ghost.
	The Comforter is come! The Witness of
the Father and the Son, to be companion of
those, who are loved of the Lord, until,
clothed in the majesty of heaven, he shall
come to earth again.
	The work rolls on apace. The Apostles
teach and exhort. Traveling far and near,
they call upon all men to repent, ever de-
claring that Jesus is the Christ.
	They make a history of Christ’s earthly
mission, leaving the record to be a guide to
the footsteps of men, unto the latest genera-
tion.
	One by one, they seal their testimony
with their blood. Other faithful disciples
follow them to the death, rather than yield
up the testimony of the Lord Jesus. Save
only one Apostle, whose chosen mission it
is to win souls to Christ, till he shall eome.
	Again darkness falls upon the earth!
	The authority to act in the name of the
Lord Jesus, has returned to the heavens,
through those who held it having suffered
martyrdom and death, in every imaginable
form, by the hand of violence.
	The believers struggle on, but their hearts
often cry out,
		“Watchman, what of the night?”
	The scriptures give forth an uncertain
light, in this day of gloom, or we but dimly
understand. Still the Holy Ghost forsakes
not those, entirely, who grope in the dark-
ness for God. Yet the shadows of night
have fallen on all the world, causing the
earth to groan for long ages.
	The Council of Nice has met. The brigh-
test lights of the existing Church, have de-
clared, that God is a non-entity; is incom-
prehensible, yet indestructible. All knowl-
edge of God is lost! Many go to and fro in
the earth, seeking the true God, and the old
way of approaching him, but in despair they
cry, in vain they call, O, Watchman, what
of the night?
	After a time a Luther arises, a Wycliffe,
a Knox, and a few shades of light, pene-
trates, the all peryading gloom.
	These Reformers with others, accomplish
much for mankind. The scriptures are now
within the reach of God’s children.
	In other paths, believers seek for Christ
earnestly desiring to discern the footsteps of
the Lord, but die, confessing they have not
found the way the ancients took.
	Then John Wesley stands forth, declaring
“All the world is my parish.” Earnestly and
prayerfully, he sought the way marked ont
by Christ, and his Apostles, but alas! in vain.
	As a great Reformer, he stands before the
world, and much light is given unto him.
A church is established, much good accom-
plished, yet with sorrow in the evening of

[Column 2]
his life he laments the sad truth, that he
has acted without authority, in the name of
the Lord Jesus.
	So the world continues on; one after an-
other arises, eagerly scanning the pages of
Holy Writ, feeling the certainty of their in-
sufficient light, asking again and again the
question, “Watchman, what of the night?”
until the early dawn of the 19th century.
	Then one was born into the world, with a
a destiny it could not read. He for a few
years walked in the path, that his fathers
had trod. But when the great revival time
came, and He beheld the spectacle, of the
ministers dividing the spoil among their re-
spective churches; some of the members, of
even his own family, joining one sect and
some another, the natural question, of which
one was right, and which one he should
connect himself with, enters his mind.
	Sufficient of the light of the Holy Ghost
burns within him, that he reads the script-
ures with an understanding heart. So he
decides to ask his heavenly Father, who has
said, “If any man lacks wisdom, let him
ask of God, who giveth to all men liberally,
and upbraideth not, and it shall be given
him.”
	The answer came, in an overwhelming
burst of light! The heavens were opened,
and two glorious personages appeared to the
boy’s startled vision; and one pointing to
the other said, “This is my beloved Son,
hear ye him.”
	An angel was sent from the courts of
glory, who announced that his name was
Moroni. That he it was, who had sealed up
the records of the Everlasting Gospel, and
hid them in the hill Cumorah; there to
await God’s command to, come forth. That
the time was now fulfilled; the set time had
come, quoting the first part of the third
chapter of Malachi, and all of the fourth,
differing but slightly, from that given in
King James translation; also some othr
scripture. The youthful prophet was in-
formed that these things were about to take
place.
	Behold the glorious rays of light, that
now envelope the sleeping world! Behold,
another Dispensation has been ushered in;
God’s voice had again been heard, speaking
to man!
	In the few succeeding years, the Church
of Jesus Christ is established on the earth,
with a promise, that it shall never more be
thrown down, or given to another people.
Prophets and Apostles again among men;
the authority of heaven restored again!
	People, few at first rally around the stand-
ard of truth, set up in the new century.
Men again act by the authority delegated to
them, by Christ himself.
	The world all in wonder, may well ask
“Watchman, what of the night?”
		O, the shadows are fleeing away,
		To usher in the perfect day.
	But the powers of darkness, which ever
assail the work of God, are brought to bear
on the infant Church. God has said, “in
the mouth of two or three witnesses, every
word shall be established,” and it came to
pass.
	The Prophet, as all his brother prophets
have done before him, walks the thorny
road of persecution.
	Dark clouds again lower; of his closest
friends, some turn to foes. He is assailed
on the right hand, and on the left. But
when the great need came, thanks be to
God, friends fearless and true, are raised up,
and the Gospel ship, steadily sails over the
turbulent, stormy sea.

[Column 3]
	Persecution is rife, yet the work of God,
goes on apace, ever guided by his unerring
hand. At length every blessing and key
pertaining to the New and Everlasting Cove-
nant has been bestowed on man.
	The powers of darkness, now make a fear-
ful onslaught; to destroy Jehovah’s work;
seeking at one fell blow, to accomplish its
overthrow; aiming at nothing less than the
murder of his chosen leaders.
		“O, Watchman, what of the night?”
	The pall of death has fallen; the Prophet
and Patriarch no more! Over lands far be-
yond the sea, falls a cloud of dread fore-
bodings. O, surely a terrible thing has hap-
pened! With crushing force, the awful truth
falls on Zion. O, ye who would give your
lives for his, be comforted! He hath not
left you comfortless; he loved you unto the
end.
	Now are dark days come; dismal and dis-
tressing thoughts fill fearful hearts. Amid
prayers and tears, hear the cry, “Watch-
man, what of the night?”
	Steady, be not moved. God’s work rolls
on apace.
	The blow that deprives the Church, of be-
loved Prophet and Patriarch, places on their
heads a martyr’s crown. Gives them entrance
over the portal, where are those who gave
their lives for the testimony of Jesus, im-
ages past. Now, two of the noblest of the
sons of earth, are ushered into their pres-
ence; where under the Great White Throne,
they join with them, in the cry, of “How
long, O, Lord, how long.” A white robe is
given to them also, and to them God says
“Yet a little while longer, until all shall be
gathered here, whose blood the world will
shed, for the testimony like unto yours.”
	O, stricken Church! O, weeping Saints!
What of the night, whose shadows so thick-
ly fall?
	Lift your heads, dry your tears, for God,
your defender, will deliver you; will lead
you safely through the troubled waters, and
plant your feet forever, on the glorious prom-
sed land. As he lead his children of old,
extending deliverance unto them, so will he
lead you. They were trodden down by op-
pressors; forced to make bricks without
straw, yet by his arm, were they delivered.
	“One like unto Moses, will raise up, him
shall you follow. By my hand he is direct-
ed, him shall you follow. Obedience and
humility are required of you, safety and de-
liverance your sure reward. Get ye out over
raging rivers, over burning plains, to a land
that I will show thee.”
	The stopping place is unknown; enemies
behind, who threaten dire destruction, the
broad Mississippi before, frozen over as by
the finger of God, that Latter Day Israel
too, shall pass over dry shod.
	The broad rolling plains, where roams the
wild Indian, stretch for hundreds of miles
trackless as a wilderness they seem before
them. What wonder that in anguish of soul,
they often cry “Watchman, what of the
night?”
	The great leader makes answer, in tones
clear and unmistakable, “Onward, we go
onward.”
	Highways are cast up, rivers are forded.
Some stop to sow and reap, while others
press forward, to find the resting place.
	And as of old, God went before, and was
in their rearward. The tender and weak,
grew tired and weary, and lay them down
to rest on the prairies lone and still: and
like them of other days, their mortal eyes
never behold Canaan’s fertile land.
		(Concluded on page 46.)

', "[WomansExponent | Vol25 | 1896 | Unknown | Issue: 16 | Title: ANSWER TO W. W. PHELPS. | Author: JOSEPH SMITH]

ANSWER TO W. W. PHELPS.
(Go With Me.)

I WILL go, I will go, to the home of the Saints,
	Where the virtue’s the value, and life the
		reward;
But before I return to my former estate
	I must fulfill the mission I had from the Lord.

Wherefore, hear, O ye heavens, and give ear O
		ye earth;
	And rejoice ye inhabitants truly again;
For the Lord he is God, and his life never ends,
	And besides him there ne’er was a Savior
		of men.

His ways are a wonder; his wisdom is great;
	The extent of his doings there’s none can un-
		veil;
His purposes fail not; from age unto age
	He still is the same, and his years never fail.

His throne is the heavens, his lifetime is all
	Of eternity now and eternity then;
His union is power, and none stays his hand,—
	The Alpha, Omega, for ever: Amen.

For thus saith the Lord, in the spirit of truth,
	I am merciful, gracious, and good unto those
That fear me, and live for the life that’s to come;
	My delight is to honor the saints with repose;

That serve me in righteousness true to the end;
	Eternal’s their glory and great their reward;
I’ll surely reveal all my mysteries to them,—
	The great hidden mysteries in my kingdom
		stored—

From the council in Kolob, to time on the earth
	And for ages to come unto them I will show
My pleasure and will, what my kingdom will do;
	Eternity’s wonders they truly shall know.

Great things of the future I’ll show unto them,
	Yea, things of the vast generations to rise;
For their wisdom and glory shall be very great,
	And their pure understanding extend to the
		skies:

And before them the wisdom of wise men shall
		cease,
	And the nice understanding of prudent ones
		fail!
For the light of my spirit shall light mine elect,
	And the truth is so mighty’t will every prevail.

And the secrets and plans of my will I’ll reveal;
	The sanctified pleasures when earth is renewed,
What the eye hath not seen, nor the ear hath yet
		heard;
	Nor the heart of the natural man ever hath
		viewed.

[Column 2]
I, Joseph, the prophet, in spirit beheld,
	And the eye of the inner man truly did see
Eternity sketched in a vision from God,
	Of what was, and now is, and yet is to be.

Those things which the Father ordained of old,
	Before the world was, or a system had run,—
Through Jesus the Maker and Savior of all;
The only begotten, (Messiah) His Son.

Of whom I bear record, as all prophets have,
	And the record I bear is the fulness,—yea even
The truth of the Gospel of Jesus—the Christ,
	With whom I conversed in the vision of heaven

For while in the act of translating his word,
	Which the Lord in his grace had appointed to
		me,
I came to the gospel recorded by John,
	Chapter fifth and the twenty-ninth verse which
		you’ll see.
	Which was given as follows:

“Speaking of the resurrection of the dead,—
“Concerning those who shall hear the voice of
“the son of man—
“And shall come forth:
“They who have done good in the resurrection
“of the just.
“And they who have done evil in the resurrection
“of the unjust.”

I marveled at these resurrections, indeed!
	For it came unto me by the spirit direct:—
And while 1 did meditate what it all meant,
	The Lord touched the eye of my own inte-
		lect:—

Hosanna forever! they opened anon,
	And the glory of God shone around where I
		was;
And there was the Son, at the Father’s right
		hand,
	In fulness of glory, and holy applause.

I beheld round the throne holy angels and hosts,
	And sanctified beings from worlds that have
		been,
In holiness worshipping God and the Lamb,
	For ever and ever, amen and amen !

And now after all of the proofs made of him,
	By witnesses truly, by whom he was known,
This is mine, last of all, that he lives; yes he
		lives!
	And sits at the right hand of God on his throne.

And I heard a great voice bearing record from
		heaven,
	He’s the Savior, and only begotten of God—
By him, of him, and through him, the worlds
		were all made,
	Even all that career in the heavens so broad.

Whose inhabitants, too, from the first to the last,
	Are saved by the very same Savior of ours;
And, of course, are begotten God’s daughters
		and sons,
	By the very same truths, and the very same
		powers.

And I saw and bear record of warfare in heaven;
	For an angel of light in authority great,
Rebelled against Jesus, and sought for his power
	But was thrust down to woe from his Godified
		state. 

And the heavens all wept, and the tears dropped
		like dew,
	That Lucifer, son of the morning had fell!

[Column 3]
Yea, is fallen! is fallen, and become, Oh, alas!
	The son of perdition; the devil of hell!
And while I was yet in the spirit of truth,
	The commandment was: write ye the vision all
		out;
For Satan, old serpent, the devil’s for war,—
	And yet will encompass the saints round about.

And I saw. too, the suffering and misery of those,
	(Overcome by the devil, in warfare and fight,)
In hell-fire, and vengeance; the doom of the
		damned;
	For the Lord said, the vision is further: so
		write.

For thus saith the Lord, now concerning all those
	Who know of my power and partake of the
		same;
And suffer themselves, that they be overcome
	By the power of Satan; despising my name:—

Defying my power, and denying the truth;—
	They are they—of the world, or of men most
		forlorn,
The sons of Perdition, of whom, ah! I say,
	’T were better for them had they never been
		born!

They’re vessels of wrath, and dishonor to God,
	Doomed to suffer his wrath in the regions of
		woe,
Through the terrific night of eternity’s round;
	With the devil and all of his angels below:

Of whom it is said, no forgiveness is given, 
	In this world, alas! nor the world that’s to
		come;
For they have denied the spirit of God,
	After having received it: and misery’s their
		doom.

And denying the only begotten of God,—
	And crucify him to themselves, as they do,
And openly put him to shame in their flesh.
	By gospel they cannot repentance renew.

They are they, who must go to the great lake of
		fire,
	Which burneth with brimstone, yet never
		consumes,
And dwell with the devil, and angels of his, 
	While eternity goes and eternity comes.

They are they, who must groan through the great
		second death,
	And are not redeemed in the time of the Lord;
While all the rest are, through the triumph of
		Christ,
	Made partakers of grace by the power of his
		word.

The mystery of Godliness truly is great;—
	The past, and the present, and what is to be;
And this is the gospel!—glad tidings to all,
	Which the voice from the heavens bore record
		to me.

That he came to the world in the middle of time
	To lay down his life for his friends and his foes,
And bear away sin as a mission of love:
	And sanctify earth for a blessed repose.

’Tis decreed that he’ll save all the work of his
		hands,
	And sanctify them by his own precious blood;
And purify earth for the Sabbath of rest,
	By the agent of fire, as it was by the flood.


[Column 1]
The Savior will save all his Father did give, 
	Even all that he gave in the regions abroad,
Save the sons of perdition: They,re lost; ever
		lost,
	And can never return to the presence of God.
They are they, who must reign with the devil in 
		hell,
	In eternity now, and eternity then,
Where the worm dieth not, and the fire is not
		quenched;—
	And the punishment still is eternal. Amen.
And which is the torment apostates receive,
	But the end, or the place where the torment
		began,
Save to them who are made to partake of the 
		same,
	Was never, nor will be, revealed unto man. 
Yet God shows by vision a glimpse of their fate,
	And straightway he closes the scene that was
		shown:
So the width, or the depth, or the misery thereof,
	Save to those that partake, is forever unknown
And while I was pondering, the vision was closed.
	And the voice said to me, write the vision for
		lo!
’Tis the end of the scene of the sufferings of 
		those,
	Who remain filthy still in their anguish and woe.
And again I bear record of heavenly things,
	Where virtue’s the value above all’s that’s
		priced—
Of the truth of the gospel concerning the just,
	That rise in the first resurrection of Christ.
Who received and believed, and repented Iike- 
		wise,
	And then were baptized, as a man always was,
Who asked and received a remission of sin,
	And honored the kingdom by keeping its laws.
Being buried in water as Jesus had been,
	And keeping the whole of his holy commands,
They received the gift of the spirit of truth,
	By the ordinance truly of laying on hands. 
For these evermore, by their faith and their 
		works,
	Being tried in their life-time, as purified gold, 
And sealed by the spirit of promise, to life,
	By men called of God, as was Aaron of old.
They are they, of the church of the first born of
		God,—
	And unto whose hands he commiteth all things;
For they hold the keys of the kingdom of heaven,
	And reign with the Savior, as priests and as 
		kings.
They’re priests of the order of Melchisedek,
	Like Jesus (from whom is this highest reward,)
Receiving a fullness of glory and light;
	As written: they’re Gods; even sons of the 
		Lord.
So all things are theirs; yes, of life, or of death;
	Yes, whether things now, or to come, all are
		theirs,
And they are the Savior’s, and he is the Lord’s,
	Having overcome all, as eternity’s heirs.
’Tis wisdom that man never glory in man, 
	But give God the glory for all that he hath;
For the righteous will walk in the presence of 
		God,
	While the wicked are trod under foot in his 
		wrath.
Yea, the righteous shall dwell in the presence of
		God,
	And Jesus, forever, from earth’s second birth— 
For when he comes down in the splendor of 
		heaven,
	All these he’ll bring with him, to reign on the
		earth.

[Column 2]
These are they that arise in their bodies of flesh,
	When the trump of the first resurrection shall 
		sound;
These are they that come up to Mount Zion in 
		life,
	Where the blessings and gifts of the spirit
		abound.
These are they that have come to the heavenly
		place;
	To the numberless courses of angels above:
To the city of God; e’en the holiest of all,
	And the home of the blessed, the fountain of 
		love:
To the church of old Enoch, and of the first born;
	And general assembly of ancient renowned,
Whose names are all kept in the archives of
		heaven,
	As chosen and faithful, and fit to be crowned.
These are they that are perfect throngh Jesus’
		own blood, 
	Whose bodies celestial are mentioned by Paul,
Where the sun is the typical glory thereof,
	And God and his Christ, are the true judge of
		all.
Again I beheld the terrestrial world,
	In the order and glory of Jesus, go on;
’T was not as the church of the first born of God,
	But shone in its place, as the moon to the sun. 
Behold, these are they that have died without 
		law;
	The heathen of ages that never had hope,
And those of the region and shadow of death,
	The spirits in prison, that light has brought up.
To the spirits in prison the Savior once preached,
	And taught them the gospel, with powers
		afresh;
And then were the living baptized for the dead,
	That they might be judged as if men in the 
		flesh.
These are they that are honorable men of the 
		earth;
	Who were blinded and duped by the cunning
		of men:
They received not the truth of the Savior at first;
	But did when they heard it in prison again.
Not valiant for truth they obtained not the crown,
	But are of that glory that’s typed by the moon:
They are they, that come into the presence of 
		Christ,
	But not to the fulness of God, on his throne. 
Again I beheld the telestial as third, 
	The lesser, or starry world, next in its place.
For the leaven must leaven three measures of meal
	And every knee bow that is subject to grace. 
These are they that received not the gospel of
		Christ,
	Or evidence, either, that he ever was;
As the stars are all different in glory and light,
	So differs the glory of these by the laws. 
These are they that deny not the spirit of God, 
	But are thrust down to hell, with the devil, for
		sins,
As hypocrites, liars, whoremongers and thieves,
	And stay ’til the last resurrection begins.
’Til the Lamb shall have finished the work he 
		begun;
	Shall have trodden the wine press in fury
		alone,
And overcome all by the power of his might:
	He conquers to conquer and save all his own.
These are they that receive not a fulness of light, 
	From Christ, in eternity's world where they are,
The terrestrial sends them the Comforter, though;
	And ministering angels to happify there.

[Column 3]
And so the telestial is ministered to,
	By ministers from the terrestrial one,
As terrestrial is, from the celestial throne;
	And the great, greater, greatest seem stars,
		moon, and sun. 
And thus I beheld in the vision of heaven, 
	The telestial glory, dominion and bliss, 
Surpassing the great understanding of men,—
	Unknown, save revealed, in a world vain as
		this.
And lo, I beheld the terrestrial too, 
	Which excels the telestial in glory and light, 
In splendor, and knowledge, and wisdom, and
		joy,
	In blessings, and graces, dominion and might. 
I beheld the celestial, in glory sublime:
	Which is the most excellent kingdom that is,—
Where God, e’en the Father, in harmony reigns;
	Almighty, supreme, and eternal, in bliss. 
Where the church of the first born in union reside, 
	And they see as they’re seen, and they know 
		as they’re known;
Being equals in power, dominion and might,
	With a fulness of glory and grace, round his 
		throne.
The glory celestial is one like the sun;
	The glory terrestrial is one like the moon;
The glory telestial is one like the stars,
	And all harmonize like the parts of a tune. 
As the stars are all different in lustre and size, 
	So the telestial region is mingled in bliss;
From least unto greatest, and greatest to least,
	The reward is exactly as promised in this. 
These are those that come out for Apollos and 
		Paul;
	For Cephas and Jesus, in all kinds of hope;
Enoch and Moses, and Peter and John;
	For Luther and Calvin, and even the Pope. 
For they never received the gospel of Christ. 
	Nor the prophetic spirit that came from the 
		Lord;
Nor the covenant either, which Jacob once had;
	They went their own way, and they have their
		reward.
By the order of God, last of all, these are they, 
	That will not be gathered with saints here
		below,
To be caught up to Jesus, and meet in the
		cloud:—
	In darkness they worshipped; to darkness they
		go.
These are they that are sinful, the wicked at large,
	That glutted their passion by meanness or 
		worth;
All liars, adulterers, sorcerers, and proud;
And suffer, as promised, God’s wrath on the
		earth.
These are they that must suffer the vengeance of
		hell,
	’Til Christ shall have trodden all enemies
		down,
And perfected his work in the fulness of times:
	And is crowned on his throne with his glorious
		crown. 
The vast multitude of the telestial world—
	As the stars of the skies, or the sands of the
		sea;—
The voice of Jehovah echoed far and wide, 
	Every tongue shall confess, and they all bow 
		the knee. 
Every man shall be judged by the works of his
		life,
	And receive a reward in the mansions pre-
		pared;
For his judgments are just, and his works never
		end,
	As his prophets and servants have always de-
		clared.


But the great things of God, which he showed
		unto me,
	Unlawful to utter, I dare not declare;
They surpass all the wisdom and greatness of 
		men,
	And only are seen, as has Paul, where they 
		are,
I will go, I will go, while the secret of life,
	Is blooming in heaven, and blasting in hell;
Is leaving on earth, and a budding in space;—
	I will go, I will go, with you, brother, fare-
		well!
					JOSEPH SMITH.
Nauvoo Feb. 1843

['[WomansExponent | Vol29 | 1900 | June | Issue: 1 | Title: REPORT OF THE NATIONAL COUNCIL. | Author: invitation of the Local Council of that]

   REPORT OF THE NATIONAL COUNCIL.
    The National Council of Women of the
United States held its annual business ses-
sion in Minneapolis Minnesota, this year,
by invitation of the Local Council of that
city. These meetings were set for Novem-
ber 13, 14, and 15, 1900, but from the
multiplicity of business to be transacted, it
was not possible to close the executive
meetings until Friday, November 16, at
one p. m., and even then some matters
were left over to be finished by the
General officers.
    These were indeed most interesting
meetings of this great Council, and the
Minneapolis ladies proved their generous
hospitality and welcome, as well as sym-
pathy with the cause of woman’s work in
many ways, that gave the Council good
and lasting impressions of their warm-
heartedness and nobility; also of the Mayor
and citizens of that enterprising and rapidly
growing city of the far-famed Minnehaha.
    We regret that the weather was so in-
clement when the meetings closed that we
could not venture out to see those beautiful
falls mentioned by one of our greatest
American poets in the favorite poem
“Hiawatha.”
    On Monday, November 12, a luncheon
was given by the ladies of the Relief Corps
of that Post at half past one, at the Nicolet
Hotel, in honor of their General-President,
Mrs. Mary L. Carr, of Denver, and the
officers of the Council. Only a few ladies
had yet arrived, but we who had come in
early were royally welcomed. The parlors
were gaily decorated and the banquet pre-
pared was on a magnificent scale. Hand-

[Column 2]
some menu cards and a beautiful rose were
provided for each guest, and the tables ar-
ranged in the form of the letter T were set
for fifty persons. Mrs. Morrison, chaplain
of the St Paul Lodge, pronounced the blessing
and the most social geniality prevailed
among the guests. The president of the
Minnesota Post gave a tribute of welcome
to her superior officer, the General-Presi-
dent, Mrs. Carr, to which the lady replied
in fitting words of goodfellowship and love
for the cause for which they unitedly stood.
One or two others of the local Relief Corps
spoke, and officers of the Council, including
the writer, complied briefly with the polite
request. Mrs. Higbee and the other Past
presidents of the Local Council of Minne-
apolis each added a few choice words of
greeting and welcome, making the affair “a
feast of reason and a flow of soul.”
    The same evening a reception was given
by these ladies in the parlors of the Nicolet
Hotel, quite a number of gentlemen also
being present, Grand Army men and other
citizens mingling in the brilliant affair.
Many handsome costumes were worn and
decorations of the order and flags and ban-
ners were quite conspicuous. Patriotic
musical selections by the band; and fruit
punch was served by young girls in soft,
dainty silk and organdy dresses of the most
approved styles, with ribbons and laces to
match. It was a festive occasion and one
full of pleasant memories of the grand
women who are interested in the home and
the state of Minnesota, and the welfare of
the world. Such women are growing in
numbers throughout this great and free
Republic. Mrs. McCune arrived in time
for the evening reception, she having to
travel from Omaha on another route from
the writer.
    On Tuesday from four to six, another
reception was tendered by Mrs. Walker, of
Minneapolis, and other local ladies, to the
Council, at the handsome residence of Mrs.
Walker, who is famous for her philanthropic
work and for her personal help in the way
of culture to the public. Every day, we
were informed, her gallery of choice paint-
ings is thrown open so that the students
and people desiring to avail themselves of
this privilege of cultivating the eye and
heart by gazing upon the workmanship of
great artists, have a wonderful advantage
in this private collection, many of them
gems of great value.
    Here, too, the ladies were entertained
uniquely by a band of girl musicians, who
discoursed sweet strains of music during
the entire time, and the music room and
dining room were beautifully decorated
with a variety of lovely flowers, daintily
scattered about here and there. Tea, coffee
and cocoa, with wafers, candies and cake,
were served to all guests who could leave
the picture gallery long enough to partake
of the dainty refreshments. At the table
we had the pleasure of meeting Mrs. Has-
kell, whose father and husband are news-
paper people of the Boston Herald and
Minneapolis Tribune; Mrs. Holman,
daughter of the hostess, and widow of the
Reverend Dr. Holman, who took a tour
through Utah for his health a few years
ago. Mrs. Holman speaks with much
feeling of the kindness received from our
people in the south, and we very much en-
joyed a few minutes conversation with one
who had sojourned for a time among our
people and knew something of their cor-
diality to strangers. She mentioned


[Column 1]
especially the Woolleys of Kanab, Can-
nons and Brooks of St. George.
	The Local Council of Minneapolis also
gave a reception to the officers, delegates
and members of the National Council on
Thursday afternoon at half past three, in
the parlors of the West Hotel, the Coun-
cil’s headquarters. Mrs. Higbee, the
president of the Council, was hostess. Mrs.
May Wright Sewall, the president
of the International Council, was
present at this reception, but was obliged
to leave early for home. She, as well as
Mrs. Fannie Humphreys Gaffney, always
shine in brilliant assemblies of this kind.
Both have great charms and dress with ex-
cellent taste. The writer imagines that one
would know at a glance that Mrs. Gaffney
lived in the City of New York, both by her
dress and manners. Dr. Kate Waller
Barrett, although always in the black garb
of her calling, is a woman of the South, one
must know it by her Southern polish and
attraction. She carries the unmistakable
stamp both in manner and speech. Bril-
liant, witty and emotional and a wonderful
speaker. Rev. Annie Garlin Spencer is
another type with all the collegiate graces of
manner and correctness of language. Her
gray costume might make one suspect her
a Quaker, and she comes from Providence,
Rhodelsland; but instead of Puritianism she
holds most broad and liberal views on
religious questions and all others. Then
we have Mrs.Solomon, a pronounced Jewish
type, but with the grand and lofty aspira-
tions of the tribe of Judah concerning her
people, interested in all that makes for their
advancement, but especially in religion and
the Bible, a woman of strong character.
	Though, as our readers know, we are not
given to describing costumes, yet while
mentioning others Mrs. McCune, of our
own city, might with all due propriety be
spoken of, because she wore an elegant Paris
gown of black lace which suited her style
remarkably well, and was sufficiently con-
spicuous for the writer to observe, even
among so many elegant dresses.
	Mrs. Peck, the Vice-President, was not
able to be present, but there were presidents
and delegates from many of the National
organizations belonging to the Council,
some of whom we knew well and others we
had not known heretofore. Mrs. Mary
Newbury Adams, of Iowa; Mrs. I. C.
Manchester, of Rhode Island; Mrs. [-]iske,
of Minneapolis; Mrs. Lillian E. Hollister,
of Michigan; Mrs. Emma R. Wallace, of
Chicago, and Mrs. Flo Jameson Miller, of
llinois. Miss Bower, of Detroit, and Mrs.
Terrell of Washington, D. C., were women
we had known previously,some of them for
many years. The new acquaintances we
met more familiarly were Mrs. Olds, of
Elyria, Ohio; Mrs. Woods, Quincy, Ill.;
Mrs. Glazier, of Ft. Madison, Iowa; Mrs.
Burns, of Michigan; Miss Mannering, of
Chicago; Mrs. Parry, who gave us a copy of
her poems, and Dr. Cora Smith Eaton, of
Minneapolis, a schoolfellow and fellow-
graduate of Miss Babcock’s, who was de-
lighted to see us because we knew her at
home in Salt Lake, and sent loving mes-
sages to her by us.
	There is not space to tell all the good
things about the women of the Council, but
we must mention one or two facts. First,
that Mrs. Gaffney is a fine presiding officer,
just and courteous to all, with unfailing
patience and always punctual and knows
how to economize time. Her opening ad-

[Column 2]
dress was a masterpiece and a great credit
to the Council, as was the welcome extended
by Mrs. Leach and Mrs. Tuttle, of Min-
neapolis, and also that of Mayor James
Gray of that city. The Mayor dwelt
much npon women influencing public senti-
ment and their work in education, and on
the school boards, etc. Certainly one of the
ablest speeches made during any of the open
meetings, was on Tuesday evening, by
Professor Maria Sanford, of the University
of Minnesota. She elaborated upon the
larger life with broader purposes, if men
and women would work together upon
practical matters in the home, the city, the
state, the country and the world. Gave
practical illustrations of what cnildren had
been induced to do in cleaning and beautify-
ing that city, etc, when sufficiently aroused
by means of prizes offered and such like in-
ducements.
	At the gathering extended by the Local
Council, as at all the others, we met many
beautiful women, one cannot even remem-
ber the names, but it was an exchange of
greetings between women of a great variety
of faiths and from all points of the compass.
Music of the choicest kind; and flags and
flowers in available places gladdened the eye
and enlivened the scene. In a gathering of
this sort one notes the many highly intel-
lectual faces that make the ocoasion more
attractive than aught else. Unexpectedly
one meets with kindred spirits, and here
we met one of the Wells family of Wethers-
field and Hartford, also one of the Whitneys
of New England.
	Very little time can be given to social
gatherings when the Council meets for
morning, afternoon and evening sessions.
There are meetings to be attended and work
to be accomplished as well as speeches and
reports to be listened to, if one keeps in
touch with the spirit of the Council. News-
paper women seemed to enter most heartily
into the real merits of the Council, and to
comprehend its highest ideals.
	The objects of the Council have been
furthered at this meeting and we may look
forward to hopeful suggestions from these
able women. A telegram received from
Miss Anthony said: “I send greetings to
my daughters of the most comprehensive
organization of women, and the one in
which I have the greatest hope.”


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

ANNUAL MEETING D. R.
	The annual meeting of the Utah State
Society daughters of the Revolution was
held on Friday, June 10, 1904, at 10:30 in
room 509 Templeton Building, Salt Lake
City, Regent Isabel M. Whitney Sears pre-
siding, a majority of members present.
After prayer, the secretary, Mrs. Lucy
Woodruff Smith, read the minutes of the
May meeting and the last annual meeting,
June 10, 1903. The minutes of both meet-
ings were approved.
	A letter was read from the General Soci-
ety D. R. acknowledging the ten dollars
given by the Utah Society towards the tes-
timonial tendered to Miss Adeline W. Ster-
ling, the retiring Pre[-]ident General. Letter
was read from Mrs. Julia P.M. Farnsworth,
Chaplain, who was absent in Beaver at the
time, expressing her good wishes, and re-
gret at being away on so important an oc-
casion.
	Reports were then given by the officers
who had served during the past year. The
Regent, Mrs. I. M. W. Sears, referred to
the report made by her to the General So-
ciety, and expressed briefly her great pleas-
ure in the year’s work, thanking the mem-
bers for their support. Reports were given
by First Vice-Regent, Mrs. Phebe Young
Beatie, and S cond Vice-Regent, Mrs. Myra
Young Rossiter, Rec Sec’y, Mrs. Lucy W.
Smith, Cor. Sec’y, Mrs. Florence S. Sears,
Treasurer, Mrs. Elizabeth R. Wright, Reg-
istrar, Mrs. Maria Young Dougall, and
Historian, Mrs. E. B. Wells.
	It was moved by Mrs. Annie L. King,
seconded by Mrs. Ella W. Hyde, that all
the reports be accepted with thanks—car-
ried. It was moved by Mrs. Beatie and
seconded by several that a full report of the
meeting, including the Regent’s report,
to the General Society, be published in the
WOMAN'S EXPONENT—carried.
	Regent announced that the next business
would be the election of officers for the en-
suing year. The following members were
elected, nominations from the floor:
	Regent, Mrs. Phebe Young Beatie; First
Vice-Regent, Mrs Mary E. R. Webber;
Second Vice-Regent, Mrs. Annie Louise L.
King; Rec. Sec’y, Mrs. Clarissa Y. Spencer;
Asst. Sec’y, Mrs. Carol Y. Cannon; Cor.
Sec’y, Mrs. I. M. W. Sears; Treas., Mrs. Ella
W. Hyde; Registrar, Mrs. Maria Y. Dougall;
Historian, Mrs. E. B. Wells; Chaplain,
Mrs. Lucy W. Smith.
	Vacancies in the Council will be filled by
appointment of the Regent later. Annual
meeting closed with prayer and adjourned
to June 10, 1904.	E. B- W.


The Duke of Norfolk’s carelessness of
rank and homely manner make it almost
impossible to think of him as a duke.
There is probably some truth in the stories
which have their origin in his disregard of
dress. The Duke was going to Stonyhurst
College for Speech day, and a carriage met
him at the railway station, Two tall and

THE FLOATING TEA PARTY.
A MEMORY OF BERLIN.
From Wansu's pier we sail away,
	Adown the Havel fair;
By verdant banks and castles gray,
	Bright sunshine in the air.
How clear the water shimm’ring flows,
	As swift we speed along!
The small craft ever by us goes, 
With greeting and with song.
The band plays in the steamer’s bow,
	Songs famed in ev’ry land,
No foreign thought these guests allow,
	Where warm is proffered friendship’s hand.
O, Potsdam rising there in view,
	Where palaces abound;
Of thee come mem’ries not a few,
	Thy grandeur all around.
And there dwelt lovely Queen Louise,
	So famed in history;
The palace almost hid by trees,
	Her refuge proved to be.
Though many years have passed away.
	She is beloved still;
Her virtues living e’en today,—
	And they pure thoughts instil
Romantic, green is yonder isle,
	The steamer circles round;
While strains of music e’er beguile
The throng, with restful sound.
All hearts are beating full of praise,
	For honor freely shown.
The dainty viands—German ways,
	Politeness all their own.
All hail Berlin, most generous host!
	All hail: Berlin the fair;
‘‘Yet greater be” this is our toast.
	Thou city of “The Bear.”
					LYDIA D. ALDER.

		     NEW YORK CITY.

	The Annual Meetings of the General
Society of the Daughters of the Revolution,
held in New York City, Monday and
Tuesday, April 23rd and 24th, 1900, were
of an interesting character and well attended
by members from almost all parts of the
country, and the usual routine business of
such gatherings was transacted.
	A preliminary meeting was held jn the
Society’s headquarters, and a nominating
committee elected, to prepare a ticket, etc.
The public meetings convened in the same
building, situated on Fifth Avenue and
corner of 20th Street, a large and commo-
dious hall, handsomely decorated for the
occasion, with the stars and stripes taste-
fully draped and pictures of heroes and
heroines, etc. Each state was designated
by a banner with the name, and all dele-
gates were supplied with badges.
	The President General, Mrs. Henry
Sanger Snow occupied the chair. She is a
very charming woman and has completely
won the hearts of all the Daughters who
have been associated with her during her
term of office. Her address was very bril-
lian and concise, dealing with facts in a
very gentle and persuasive manner, and
was specially significant of the work in
hand.
	The society moves as smoothly as the
sun and his satellites toward one common
object Loyalty and patriotic effort are al-
ways the general aim. Everywhere in
local chapters and State societies the gener-
al work has gone on with unity, strength
and enthusiasm of purpose. We have come
together to transact the most important
business in our societies life—to elect the
officers who are to carry on the work of the
organization for the two coming years. In
the fulfillment of this duty we are to per-
mit nothing of local or personal prejudice
and jealousy to influence us, and we are to
accept with the fullest loyalty the expres-
sion of the general will, whatever our in-
dividual disappointment may be.
	The Recording Secretary General in a
very gracious and appropriate speech
made the presentation of a “Loving Cup,”
the gift of the Society at large, which Mrs.
Snow responded to in a very brief but affec-
tionate and particularly happy manner.
	Reports from other General Officers fol-
lowed this part of the exercises, most of
them very brief, relating to each one’s own
department of work. We noted the His- 
torian General mentioned her report from
the Utah Historian, who looked after her
domestic affairs, managed her home, was a
member of the Legislature and had a baby
four months old; this elicited considerable
applause.
	These reports extended into the afternoon
and after they were finished the election of
officers began. There was considerable dis-
cussion upon the floor, meantime tellers
were appointed. Several nominations
were made, but finally the president Gener-
al was elected, Miss Adeline W. Sterling,
First Vice-President of the recent adminis-
tration.
	Tuesday morning the delegates were,
early in their seats and the election began.
There were several nominees for Vice-
President, but when it came to the Record-
ing Secretary General, eighteen candidates
were nominated before it was possible , to
get one to accept the office, as it is well


[Column 1]
known it is one of the most arduous posi-
tions in any organization. The Registrar
was re-elected by acclamation. She had
been a very efficient officer and was appar- 
ently a great favorite.
	The officers elected were: President
General, Miss Adeline W. Sterling, N. J.;
First Vice-President General, Mrs. Nathan-
iel S. Heap, Penn.; Second Vice-President-
General, Mrs. Mary A. Chapman, Mass;
Recording Secretary-General, Mrs. Carlton
M. Moody, Penn.; Corresponding Secretary-
General, Mrs. George B. Wallis, Jr., N. Y.,
Treasurer-General, Mrs. Joseph J. Casey,
N. Y.; Historian-General, Mrs. J. R.
Ditmars, N. J.; Librarian-General, Mrs.
Alexander M. Ferris, Mass.
BOARD OF MANAGERS.
	Mrs. James B. Grant, Colorado; Mrs. Henry
Sanger Snow, L. I.; Mrs George F. Dan-
els, Mass.; Mrs. David C. Carr, N. Y.;
Mrs. George Hodges, N. J.; Miss Tar-
quinia L. Voss, Indiana; Mrs. Andrew W.
Bray, N. J.
	The time was so fully occupied with the
election that the reports from the several
states had to be read during the intervals
when the tellers were out counting votes.
Massachusetts came in with the largest
number of votes, being over seventy, and
naturally the report was excellent as re-
garded numbers and work, for besides a
State Society, Massachusetts has thirty-two
chapters, and they have historical ground
and many things in their favor, which those
at a remote distance do not enjoy. New
York ranks next in membership and has a
State society and sixteen chapters and have
been successful in many ways. New Jer-
sey and Pennsylvania come next in im- 
portance in the Society D. R’s. Utah,
though the youngest of the Daughters, pre-
sented a report that was specially well re-
ceived and was the only one that extended
an invitation to the General Society to come
and hold an Annual Meeting here sometime
in the near future. Nineteen States in all
were represented, most of the reports were
written and all were read by the Regent of
the State except Utah, which was read by
the delegate, Miss Snow, and two or three
reports that came by mail and were read
by the secretary.
	The election having been decided and the
announcements made, the Amendments to
the Constitution that had been sent out
previously were carefully considered,
some of them causing animated discussion,
and finally after a word of prayer and the
singing of “America,” the Convention ad-
journed after six p m., to meet in Brooklyn,
Long Island, in April, 1901.
D. R. ENTERTAINMENTS.
	Wednesday morning, April 25, at eleven
o’clock, the Daughters of the Revolution
were invited to be present at the unveiling
of a Historical Tablet to be given by the
New York State Society to the famous 
Old Dutch Reformed Collegiate Church, of
that city, Fifth Avenue and Forty-Eighth
Street. 
	The church is quite a handsome one in
its appointments and on this occasion there 
were perhaps two hundred of the D. R.
members assembled to witness the cere-
monies. The music was excellent, and the
tablet which was inscribed “In Memory of
the Officers, Soldiers and Sailors who felt in
the War of the Revolution,” was unveiled
by Miss Mary Gage Lambert of Rowley,
Mass., the youngest child of Jonathan

[Column 2]
Lambert, who when, sixteen years old,
took part in the battle of Bunker Hill.
Miss Lambert is now past eighty years of
age.
	The presentation address was made by
Mrs. Charles F. Roe, Regent of the New
York State D. R., and the wife of Major
General Roe. The tablet was received by
the minister of the Church, Rev. Mr. McKay,
who assured the ladies that it should be
sacredly kept in the archives of the Church.
	During his acknowledgement of the gift
he spoke of the denomination to which this
Church belongs, being the oldest in New
York, it having been founded in 1629 by
the Dutch settlers at Manhattan, and was
afterwards used for barracks in the time of
the Revolution. The present building is
erected on the same site as the original
church; he also stated that the bell which
now rings to call the people together is the
one hidden away in war time, and found by
the sexton when the war was over, and
used by the same sexton many years after-
wards.
	At one o’clock of the same day Mrs.
Henry Sanger Snow, the retiring President-
General of the General Society, gave an
elaborate breakfast at the Savoy Hotel to
both the retiring and incoming officers of
the D. R., and the Regents of the several
States represented at the Convention. The
banqueting hall was handsomely decorated,
the stars and stripes, as also the Society
colors, blue and buff, being effectively
used. The round table which seated fifty
guests was dazzlingly magnificent beyond
description. In the centre was an em-
bankment of green, literally piled with yel-
low lilies and blue corn flowers, and in
regular order around the circle were smaller
ones in circle and semicircle; elegant vases
of roses and carnations added to the artistic
decorations, and at each place a pretty silk
flag stood waving; but the bright appear-
ance of the brilliant women who surrounded
this unique “round table” was the most
pleasing picture of all, and one could not
help wondering why therewas not a photog-
rapher at hand, for it certainly would
have been a good opportunity, and, of
course, each guest would have purchased
a picture.
	During the breakfast Mrs. Snow, in a very
sweet and gracious manner, rose, and while
lifting the “Loving Cup,” which had been
previously brought in, turned to the new
President-General, Miss Sterling, saluting
her first, and then bowing to all seated at
the table, drank a toast to the assembled
guests in a few choice words of loving
tenderness, mentioning the fact that there
were represented nineteen states. The new
President-General also toasted her pre-
decessor in office and then the cup went
round, all the ladies tasting the old Moselle
wine from the new “Loving Cup.” After-
wards handsome little ornamental  favors
were given each guest, that were designated
by the ladies “Our little Juniors.”
	The breakfast was a perfect success, cer-
tainly these social functions bring women in
public life closer together in conversation
and acquaintance than is possible in Con-
gresses and Conventions.
	At three p. m. of the same afternoon the
New York State Society gave a reception to
the D. R. from all parts of the country in
the elegant parlors of the Waldorf-Astoria.
Mrs. Roe, State Regent, and her two
“Vices,” received from three to six p. m.,
in the most cordial and gracious manner;
about three hundred “Daughters” attended.

[Column 3]
	It is not possible to describe the dresses
(as the writer has been asked to do on
several occasions), but suffice to say, there
were many elderly daughters who were
quite as elaborately and as shiningly ar-
rayed, in costly apparel, wearing diamonds,
pearls and precious jewels, as the younger
portion of the D. R’s. There were also
others plainly dressed in simple costumes,
but no sort of distinction was apparent 
The elaborately dressed lady conversed with
those in plain attire and a general feeling
of good fellowship prevailed. The music
was of the highest order of excellence and
the delicate and delicious refreshments
were served in the daintiest style.
	Thursday morning two hundred or more
of the D. R.’s started on a trip up the Hud-
son by the West Shore R. R. to West Point.
All arrangements had been made before-
hand by a committee of New York State
and the General Society D. R., Mrs. Charles
W. Dayton, chairman. All the places of
interest were thrown open to the visitors,
the library and museum were the most at-
tractive places. After luncheon many of
the ladies were driven to interesting points
outside the college grounds. Col. Hein,
Commandant of Cadets, caused an exhibi-
tion of field artillery to be given in honor of
the ladies, and later on there was a grand
review of the corps of cadets. The day
was fine and the opportunity given the
ladies to visit and become acquainted with
each other was a source of pleasure and
gratification, as these busy women living
long distances apart, have many interests in
common, and to exchange ideas and com-
pare, is edifying and educational.
	Returning to New York on the evening
train, and in the boat when crossing the
Bay, there was opportunity for parting
messages and fervent farewells. The
writer had a most interesting conversation
with Miss Hunt, of Boston, on the return
trip. Miss M. Mabelle Snow, delegate 
from the Utah D. R., and Miss Abbie
Wells, daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Junius F.
Wells, (a Salt Lake girl now sojourning in
New York,) were the youngest members of
the party.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

['[WomansExponent | Vol25 | 1896 | August | Issue: 3 | Title: LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL. | Author: WOMANS EXPONENT]

LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL.
		Tuesday, Nov. 19, 1895.
	WE left Genoa by mid-day train, and
continuing along the Mediterranean Coast,
still found the scenery imposing. We
passed Chiavari, inclosed by cultivated
hills. It has several old towers, and in the
environs many handsome Villas. Our
guide pointed out the Anchovy fishery.
	We passed the renowned winter resort,
Spezzia, the largest Military seaport in 
Italy. Its ship yard is one of the largest in 
Europe.
	We saw the beautiful Carrara Mountains
in the Appennines, the quarries have been
worked for more than two thousand years.
It is said they were discovered by a herds-
man, and that these quarries furnished the
marble for Caesar’s Palaces.
	At six o’clock we arrived at Pisa. As we
neared the station we could see grouped to-
gether in one corner of the town, the
Cathedral. Baptistry and Leaning Tower.
Taking the diligence we drove to the
Grand Hotel, which was very clean and nice.
We are now away from rose embowered
hotels. This hotel entertains King Hum-
bert when he comes to Pisa. The host
showed us the apartments of the king;
they were very elegant. Our dinner was
excellent, the cooking was delicious.
	Looking from my window on the op-


posite side of the river, was an exqusite 
little marble Morgue. It was built for a
church, and then used for that purpose; the
first time in a strange city I ever saw one
of those places without a shudder. The
Arno reminded me more of one of the large
canals seen at Amsterdam, but an English
lady said to me "you should have seen the
river last week during the rains when it
was full to the brim, and seemed as if it
would tear everything before it.” The
river divides the city into two nearly
equal parts; the quays which run along the
banks are spacious, there are four bridges,
one is of marble.
NOV. 20. After breakfast we drove to
the Cathedral built of marble, which with
the Baptistry and Leaning Tower, is said
to be the finest specimen that exists of the
style called by Italians the Gotico Moresco.
The Cathedral was begun in 1063, conse-
crated in 1118. In 1560 it was nearly de-
stroyed by fire, except the choir, but was
restored. It is 311 feet long, 106 feet wide,
and the nave is 109 feet high. The facade
is magnificent, the interior is exqusite
in finish and very imposing. The bronze doors
were put up in 1602—after the fire, but
one of the earlier ones is to be seen in the
South Aisle. The great bronze lamp which
gave Galileo the idea of the pendulum,
hangs in the nave. We watched it and
could see it swing, but only by looking at
some object near it; then it was very
perceptible. The statues and paintings are
fine. The most remarkable pictures are,
Andrea del Sarto ‘‘St. Cecilia” ‘‘St. Peter”
and ‘‘St. Agnes.” There were two exqui-
site Madonnas, but I could not learn by
whom. The campanile or belfry, is the
most curious, a cylindrical tower of 178
feet in height, graceful in proportions and
constructed of successive rows of marble
pillars, remarkable for its inclination,
thirteen feet out of the perpiudicular,
from whence the name, Leaning Tower,
and also why it is called one of the
seven wonders of the world. The Tower
has a  peal of seven bells, the heaviest
weighing six tons.
	The Baptistry, completed in 1278, is
about 100 feet in diameter, and the height
of the dome 190 feet. The pulpit, the
masterpiece of Nicola Pesano’ (1260) and
the fount, quite as beautiful, would pay
you for the illness of the voyage, just to see
them. A priest gave us the wonderful
echo; first pianissimo, then in rich full
tones; it answered in the same rich tones.
	The Campo Santo, completed in 1283, is
a large oblong building or corridor, enclos-
ing the ‘‘holy ground” which contains
many shiploads of earth brought from
Mount Calvary. The funeral mounments,
sarcophagi and Greek and Roman Anti-
quities are very curious. One picture
represents the “Last Judgment”. It
would fill your soul with horror. One of
the party remarked “were I obliged to see
that every day I would live a pure life, for
I never wish to go there.” As we left the
buildings a man took our attention with a
cute, tiny, brown dog, Marengo, he said
he was named, after the village of Sardinia.
It stands on the borders of a great plain
where, on the 14th of June 1800, Bonaparte
defeated the Austrians under General
Melas. Doggie was bought by a member
of our party.
	The square of the University contains
marble buildings. The University is'the
oldest in Italy, and has a library, a botani-
cal garden, a cabinet of natural history, and
au observatory.
	Pisa is of great antiquity, having been
one of the twelve towns of Etruria, and
afterwards augmented by a colony from
Rome.
	After a substantial lunch we took the
train for Rome. The cars differ from those
of France, they are not the long cars with
a passage down one side, they are divided
into compartments with a door connecting
the two, a tiny bell between, and leading
into the next compartment.
	We had views on our right of the Medi-
terranean, it being clear after leaving
Cecina, a view of the Island ot Elba. The
train was packed. We had dinner on the
train between stations, one hour for meals,
the train stopped, we left our car, ran to
the diner next the engine, the meal was
excellent, we stopped, ran back to our car,
others went to the diner; it was all novel
and amusing.
	About midnight we arrive at Rome
“The Eternal City.” We were thankful
we had a manager and he attended to the
Custom Officers. Arrangements were
made for our stay at Hotel d’ Angleterre
de’ Paris, among the most comfortable in
the city. Our rooms were well aired, and
much fatigued we retired, to dream of the
beautiful fountain that met our gaze just
after leaving the station.
	Nov. 21, 1896.—In Rome. Our break-
fast the inevitable rolls and coffee, but
good. Carriages were ordered, we drove
along the Tiber, passed the Angel Bridge,
a bridge of the Emp. Hadrian, Castle St.
Angels, or tomb of Hadrian, the most im-
posing mansoleum  in Rome, begun by
Uadrian, completed by his successor, A.
D. 139. The bridge is the approach to the
tomb; it is wholly ancient except the para-
pets,—to St. Peters Church, we left the
carriages at the fountain in the space in
front of the Church. It is a large area of
oval form surrounded with a magnificent
colonnade, the work of Berinrei. Viewing
the building from the outside we then
entered the Church, which stands on the
site of the circus of Nero, where many
Christians were martyred, and where St.
Peter is said to have been buried after his
crucifixion.
	The crypt is all that remains of the early
basilica. The present edifice was begun in
1506, by Julius II, from designs by Bram-
ante. The work went on with various
changes and under several architects, until
Paul III, being “inspired by God” entrust-
ed it to Michael Angelo. The Church was
dedicated by Urban III, in 1626, the colon-
nade added by Alexander VII in 1667, and
the sacristy by Pius VI, in 1780. The
main building cost fifty million dollars; the
work was so heavy that Julius II and Leo
X, resorted to the sale of indulgences to
raise the money; this led to the Reforma-
tion. Go and see it, nothing can give you
an idea of its vastness, grandeur and plain
splendor; the arts of architecture, sculpture
and painting are in the highest perfection.
The most celebrated artists, Bramante,
Raphael, Vignola, Maderno, Bernni and
Michael Angelo, all worked upon it. The
patriarchal chair is a throne elevated to the
height of 70 feet. The high altar has be-
low it St. Peter’s tomb, above it is a can-
opy of brass towering to a height of 132
feet, before the door to the tomb is a lamp
always lighted. On the floor was marked
the length of all the large Cathedrals in
the world; this was much larger. In one
of the chapels was a painting of St. Paul
baptising his jailors. 
	Leaving the Church we crossed the An-
gel Bridge, passed the Fountain of Trevi,
the legend is “If you drink of the waters of
Trevi, it will ensure you a return to Rome”
to the hotel for lunch. Then a drive upon
the Janiculum Hill, where the gardens are
fine, and there is a splended view of Rome,
built upon seven hills, situated chiefly on
the left bank of the Tiber. The most regu-
larly built is near the northern gate Parte
del Popolo, and the quarter of Bargo. The
squares are numerous but small, and adorn-
ed with fountains, obelisks and monuments.
We drove on the Flaminian Way, the
northern road which led from Ancient
Rome through the gate of Paolo. Near
the gate is the tomb of Caius Cestius, en-
closed in the wall of Aurelian. It is a
brick pyramid cased with marble, 161 feet
high, with a base of 98 feet. In the Prot-
estant cemetery at its foot, is the grave of
Keats, and the tomb which contains the
heart of Shelley. The cypress trees are
very old. As we drove along by the old
walls of Rome, the beggar boys turned
cartwheels, for sue’s they were bright-eyed
laughing boys, attractive in spite of dirt
and rags, looking like Murillo’s Beggar
Boys. We reached the Basilica of St.
Paul, “S. Paolo fuori le Mura”, a magnifi-
cent church which marks the spot where
the Apostle is believed to have been
buried. It contains Mosaics of the 13th
Century; the interior is supported by eighty
granite columns. We judged it one of the
most beautiful churches we had seen. Re-
turning we visited the Mammertine prison,
where St, Peter was liberated by the an-
gel.
	It was now dark, and we returned to
the hotel for dinner.
KIM.
