LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL.

	WE now crossed over the river,
passing the home of Horace and had a view
of the cascades and falls, and of the plain
and hills and Rome, the Dome of St. Peter
above everything else, and the sun was just
over it so the effect was fine; then to Ha-
drian’s Villa, it covered a square mile, dates
from the later years of this far-traveled
Emperor. ’Tis said when he saw in any
country anything splendid he returned and
added one like it to this Palace; it was a
marvel of architecture and landscape
gardening. From the 16th Century to the
present day the ruins furnished works of
art, including many of the principal
treasures of the Vatican, and the ruins still
retain traces of mosaic and beautiful colored
marble, inlaying one marble with another,
and glass molded in ornamental relief,
while some of the magnificent grounds re-
main as they were. From here he could
look at Rome and the magnificent land-
scape he used to drive out in his chariot
with his slaves running at the horses heads,
and if one fell dead they passed on. He
built the Villa, paying his workmen with
the lash if they fainted under their burdens.
Do you wonder God buried it all? We
came home just at sunset when he went
down in a bank of gold; soon purple clouds
fringed the gold; it made me think of the
New Jerusalem where the walls are all
jasper and gold, and as the beautiful scene
receded from my view, I felt regret that the
day was ended. At night as the moon was
just right, I drove to the Colosseum and
Forum; there were no ugly shadows, the
Arches, Colosseum and Palaces were intox-
catingly, divinely beautiful. The driver
said “Ladies you don’t know how beauti-
ful Rome is until you see it by moonlight.”
	Tuesday, 26. Taking a last look at the
obelisk brought from Egypt and set up in
honor of Apollo by Augustus, then a drive
on the Pincian Hill, adorned with rostral
columns, statues, marble bas-reliefs, inter-
spersed with cypresses and pines.
	A winding road lined with memosas and
other flowering shrubs leads to the upper
platform now laid out in public drives and
gardens, but till twenty years ago a desert-
ed waste where the ghost of Nero was be-
lieved to wander in the middle ages. The
Eternal City is seen spread at our feet and
beyond it the wide spreading Campagna,
till a silver line marks the sea melting into
the horizon beyond Ostia. Then the Arch-
es which record important historical events;
Arch of Titus, the destruction of Jerusalem
the Jews loathe it; ’tis said to this day
no Jew has passed through it; Arch of
Septimius Severus, the victory over the
Parthians; that of Constantine, the end of
the tyrannical reign of Maxentius.
	The rest of the day was devoted to shop-
ping and packing trunks.

[Column 2]
	Wednesday, Nov. 27. Left at an early
hour for Naples 161 miles southeast from
Rome. Upon leaving the station we took
a regretful look at the enormous Dome of
St. Peter then made ourselves comfortable
for the day. As we traveled along I re-
marked to our guide, “We would call
those mountains, hills in America, what do
you call mountains?” these are 8,000 ft.
high; they did not seem so. The Appen-
nines traverse Italy in its whole length
from north to south dividing it nearly
equally; their highest point is Mount Car-
no, 9,521 feet above sea level; they are not
peaked but smooth and undulating their
bare rocks appearing only at their highest
point. The vegetation covers them up to
3,000 ft., on the lower belts the olive, palm,
citron and orange grow, which we saw on
the tops of some old mountains. We pass-
ed Caserta not far from Naples, noted for
its royal palaces built in 1752 by Charles
III, an immense pile with extensive gardens
called the Versailles of Naples. Excepting
a few Monastaries nothing of special interest
until the volcano of Vesuvius; it was
night and the view unsurpassed. A few
moments brought us to Naples the "City
of Sirens.”
	Arriving at Hotel Royal des Etrangers,
my bedroom was beautifully situated, the
evening was warm, I stood at the open
window and looked out upon the quay; the
beautiful Bay and beyond to the Promon-
tory then to the street below where soldiers
were marching, listening to their music,
thought it delightful to be here. The
city has a magnificent situation, rising like
an amphitheatre from the shore.
	On the west is the Castle of St. Elmo
once of immense strength, which covers
the hill of St. Erosmo. On the seaside are
the fortresses of Castle Nuovo often com-
pared to the Tower of London, and adorned
with a triumphal arch in honor of Alfonso
of Aragon, 1442. The Castle dell Ovo
with its traditions about Virgil probably
built by Norman Wm. I. 1150.
	The city is divided into the old and the
new town by the ridge extending from the
palace of Capodimonte to the sea, dividing
the city into a double crescent. Many of
the streets are crooked, narrow and steep;
the principal Via Roma (formerly Toledo)
winds along the curving shore of the Bay
and is the fashionable promenade of the
city and commands fine views; the lower
part of the street showed us the habits and
life of the lower classes. Some pictures
were striking and unlike anything I ever
saw except Indian scenes in early days.
The beggars annoy one so persistently and
seem happy; along the shore the fishermen
sell you the sea-horse for luck, and one
told me he would eat a devil fish for a sue,
a hidous looking fish they seem fond of.
I replied nothing would induce me to look
at him and he seemed to understand. Of
the large theatres we saw none, the Prof.
told us the San Carlo was the largest and
most elegant. The National Museum is one
of the most interesting and largest in the
world and contains many treasures from
Pompeii and the best bronzes found at Her-
culaneum. The aquarium is most inter-
esting containing a complete collection of
sea fishes. The church domes glitter with
gilded and colored tiles, the Cathedral San
Gennaro contains the priceless treasures of
the Saints and the small phials said to con-
tain the blood of St. Gennaro, which is be-
lieved to liquify twice every year. Naples


[Column 1]
has a number of squares but they are not
beautiful although decorated with obelisks
and fountains, they looked well trodden,
there was no sign “Keep off the grass.”
The Royal Palace at the extremity of the
Via Roma is home like, the young Prince
resides there; it contains old paintings and
many beautiful things, the Royal entrance
is magnificent, the ballroom is handsome;
leading from it on each side are a series of
beautiful rooms where the dancers sit or
promenade. The effect is like that of the
President’s room in the Capital at
Washington, D. C., looking into the mir-
rors the room each side is reflected a score
of times, a most beautiful sight; the theatre
is pretty but does not compare with those
of the Bourbon Princes. The double Balcony
was a dream of delight, with exquisite
views of the Bay and Vesuvius.
	Thursday Nov. 28. A fair day and calm
sea, we determined to visit the wonderful
Blue Grotto or “Grotto of the Nymphs,”
as should the sea be rough it would be im-
possible to enter there. Capri is an island
of Italy in the Mediterranean at the entrance
of the Bay of Naples, and twenty miles
south of the City of Naples.
	We started early and going aboard a
beautiful yacht steamed away to the Island,
the water beautifully calm and blue, the
views superb, the fiery crest of Vesuvius
a striking contrast. Arriving at Capri the
boats were waiting to take us into the
Grotto, two in a boat. As we neared the
entrance the men shouted to us to lie down,
Miss L. was in my boat, we could not sit
up as the opening was not high enough and
is only visible at low tide; the spectacle
was most beautiful. The chamber is 175
ft. by 100 and is 41 ft. high, illuminated
only from the entrance and by the reflection
from the bottom 60 ft. below; the walls ol
the cave, the boats and people were an in-
describably beautiful blue, the water like a
“Lambent sheet of blue flame,” the oars
looked like they were spangled with jewels,
the boys that dive, look when they come
to the surface, as if incased in blue armor.
	Returning to the yacht we now proceeded
to the picturesque village on the Island
where we were to lunch, then drive.
’Twas here Tiberius passed the last ten
years of his life, where he built twelve vil-
las the remains of which are still to be seen,
and the rock “II Salto” from which it is
said the tyrant used to throw his victims.
As we landed the natives urged us to ride
their donkeys up the steep incline to our
hotel on the edge of a hill with remarka-
ble views of the sea and Isles. The lunch
was served on the veranda surrounded with
orange, lemon and cition trees; the natives
sold us beautiful coral. After lunch we
drove about the Island. There were mar-
velous views of villas, garden, and castles
and the imposing volcano which we seem
never to lose sight of. Returning to the
yacht we resumed our voyage to Sorrento
where we were to remain the night. Arriv-
ing at Grand Hotel Victoria situated on the
very margin of the Cliffs we entered what
seemed like a tunnel where the porters were
waiting for us with the information, “The
lilt was out of repair;” so we walked up
this steep incline, entered the gardens and
passed into the hotel. The proprietor met
us and we were shown to our rooms, our
baggage soon followed and we dressed for
Table d' Hote at 7 o’clock.
	This part of Italy has a world-wide repu-
tation for beauty of scene and charm

[Column 2]
of climate. My window commands views
of the Bay and hills. I walked out upon
the terrace, the air was warm and filled
with perfume, the moonlight dazzling, and
down the Cliffs is the swish of the blue
waters; the scene was just heavenly. At
dinner there were other Americans who
came via Gibraltar, will remain two weeks.
The dinner was extremely fine, at dessert,
the waiter passed me something wrapped in
grape leaves “A specialite” of Sorrento,
they were dried grapes of delicious flavor
another “Indian fruit,” which is found to
be cactus fruit. I had eaten the fruit of
the cactus, this was sweeter.
	After dinner we went into the ballroom
where the natives danced and sang for us
dressed in their gorgeous costumes; they
were very graceful. Among the dances by
men and women was the Tarentalla and 
the Zingarella, very beautiful. Their
voices too were fine and they finished the
entertainment with “God save the Queen”
for the English guests, and “Yankee
Doodle” for the Americans; we all rose to
our feet while they sang. The evening
passed delightfully.
						KIM.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT


', '[WomansExponent | Vol25 | 1896 | December | Issue: 12 | Title: LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL. | Author: Unknown]

LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL.

		BOLOGNA, Italy.
		Friday, Dec. 6, 1895.
	Express train via Padua to Venice.
Padua, “Italian Padova,” was founded
according to “Ancient tradition” by
Antenor, the brother-in-law of Priam, and is
famous for its University. Among its
alumni were Savonarola, Tasso, Petrarch
and Galileo, whose statues adorn the Pizza
Vittosia Emanuele.
	The church S. Antonio begun in 1256, is
very imposing with seven domes, and is
rich in paintings, sculptures and works of
Art. The Scuola del Santo has frescoes by
Titian and his pupils. S. Maria dell’
Arena, or Giotto’s chapel contains frescoes
which are among his best works. In one
church is an altar piece by Paul Veronese.
	“Venice in June,” gave our approach
via Adriatic Sea.
	We now approach the city by a railway
bridge more than two miles long, consist-
ing of long embankments and 222 arches,
costing nearly a million dollars.
	We left the cars and entered the gon-
dolas waiting for us, a few moments'
brought us to the Grand Hotel. Although
the winter day was sombre and there were
no singers, the gondola ride and the Medie-
val Palaces were still enchanting.
	We dined, then spent the evening ramb-
ling through the quaint alleys and on the
Grand Square of St. Mark, the pigeons
were so numerous and tame, it was difficult
to walk and not step upon them.
	This square is the centre of all that is
loveliest in Venice. On the E. side is St.
Marks Church, the N. the Procuratie
Vecchi, with the clock tower, (La Torre
dell’ Orologio,) built in 1496, the hours
are struck on a bell by two bronze figures.
The Piazzetta is a small square opening to
the South near the angle it makes with the
Piazza, is the Campanile and on the E. side
is the Ducal Palace, on the W. the Library
and Mint, to the seaward the two columns
of granite one bearing the winged lion of St.
Mark, the other the statue of St. Theodore.
St. Mark was built in the 11th Cent.,
Byzantine style with Gothic additions of the
14th Cent. and Renaissance alterations of
the 17th Cent. Above the portal are the

[Column 2]
bronze horses which Constantine carried
from Rome to Constantinople, whence Zeno
broght back in 1205; they were taken to
Paris by Nap. in 1797 and restored to Venice
in 1815. The church has a great dome in
thecentre and four smaller ones crown the
arms of the cruciform structure the Mosaics
of the exterior and interior cover more than
an acre, are on a background of gold gild-
ing and are very beautiful, the bronze and
rich marbles are splendid.
	It is impossible to describe the Ducal
Palace. We entered by the Scala dei
Giganti or Giants staircase from the court-
yard. The interior is a rich gallery of
Venetian Art, the great Council Hall con-
tains portraits of 76 Doges and Tintorretto’s
Paradise, the Palace is connected on the E.
side with prison (Le Prigione) by the
Bridge of Sighs, so called because when the
prisoners crossed over for their trial, if
innocent they left the Palace by the
front door, if guilty were placed in the dun-
geons below and never again crossed the
bridge.
	Saturday, Dec. 7—Early morning found
us in our gondolas to see the Old Palaces
situated on the Grand Canal. Our hotel
was once the Palace Terro (14th Cent.) and
the Palace Tini Wimpffen, and next to it
is the pretty Palazzo Contarini Tasau,
known as the Palace of Desdemona, the
Palace Mocenigo was occupied by Byron.
	The Arsenal has a gateway of Renaissance
workmanship. “Sharp and expressive in
its ornamental sculpture,” in front are
four Greek lions brought from the Piraens
in 1687, the museum contains old arms,
armor and instruments of torture.
	We visited the factory which is very
interesting. The rest of the day we de-
voted to churches—that of the Jesuits, the
interior is curious, the walls being covered with
imitation of drapery in white marble inlaid
with green, it contains Titians Martyrdom
of St Laurence. St. Sebastine is famous for
pictures by P. Veronise who is buried here.
St. Rocco has pictures by Tintoretto and
Scuola di San Rocco is one of the three
most precious buildings in Italy, the others
being the Sistine Chapel at Rome and the
Campo Santo at Pisa.
	Sunday, Dec. 8—Attended Church San
Marco, the sermon was no doubt eloquent
but could not understand a word, the music
was fine, the organ filled the Church with
rich, full tones. After lunch went to the
Scotch Presbyterian Church but the Pastor’s
wife stood at the door saying, Dr. Robert-
son was ill and there would be no service,
she gave us her card, inviting us to tea on
Monday, saying on that day they received
strangers, and if they felt lonely might
make or meet friends, in a strange land all
the world was kin. Was sorry not to attend
but left Venice on that day.
	Monday, Dec. 9—Early morning train
for Milan via Verona, 72 miles from Venice.
On our other Tour we stopped at Verona.
The churches are very fine mostly Gothic,
the Cathedral is of the 14th Cent. and con-
tains an assumption by Titian and S.
Fermo Maggiore 14th Cent., has a carved
roof of larch wood and in one chapel a fine
altar-piece by Caroto, other churches con-
tain valuable pictures by Tintoretto, P.
Veronise and other artists. Our desire was
to see in this old city the tomb of Juliet.
A rude Sarcophagus was kept in the garden
of an old Franciscan Monastery on the right
bank of the Aolige, the house of the Capu-
lets is on the street San Sabastino.

[Column 3]
	From Verona to Milan is 93 miles. When
I came before took the other route, Turin
to Milan. That road passes through
Novara near which the battle was fought,
and through the memorable battle field of
Magenta. This is the most interesting
route. We pass the Castles of the Montec-
chi, the Montagues of Romeo and Juliet
and Lake Garda, near and toward this was
fought the battle of Solferino, 24th of June,
1859, Austrians defeated by Nap. III.
Saw the house at Villafranca, where the
treaty between the French and Austrian
Emperors was signed July 11, 1859. The
City of Milan, Capitol of Lombardy, is en-
tered through a beautiful arch. When last
here it was warm, the ladies were out on
the streets with lace veils or shawls draped
on their heads something like the Spanish
style. It is a walled city, the interior of
the walls laid out with gardens and planted
with trees, the streets are elegantly paved,
it is a pleasure to walk on them, the foot
paths next the houses are six feet wide of
smooth granite. We drove about the city
and to the suppressed Monastery of St.
Maria delle Grazie, to see Da Vincis Last
Supper. The painting is on the wall and
much defaced as the church was once used
as a stable in time of war; in spite of which
you can see how beautiful was the painting,
although of the 14th Cent. the beautiful
colors still remain. The Church St. Am-
brose founded in the 14th Cent. in its pre-
sent form to the 12th is most interesing.
Here the old Lombard kings received the
“Iron Crowns,” the frescoes and mosaics
are beautiful it has a golden altar-front.
The Brera, a Palace of Science and Art con-
tains in the picture gallery Raphael’s
Sposalizio, or the marriage of the Virgin.
Next we visited the Cathedral with its
delicate tracery and forest of pinnacles, built
in the form of a Latin cross, its interior is
490 feet in length, 185 feet in width, with
nave 155 feet in height, the dome is 220 ft.
in height from the pavement to the top of
the tower 360 feet. The roof has 98 Gothic
turrets, the exterior is adorned with 4,400
marble statues, its beautiful tower and
spires are all surmounted by a statue, the
nterior has double clustered pillars, lofty
arches, niches in the walls filled with figures
and grand monuments. The scene with
the sun shining through the magnificent
stained windows and rose window is strik-
ingly beautiful, just that wavy beautiful
effect of light and shade one gets in a for-
est.
	Among the treasures is a Veil from the
true cross, a silver altar, solid silver
statues, Bibles with the covers filled with
rare jewels, robes of cloth of gold and cloth
of silver, rare laces and wealth untold.
One room is nearly all silver. A Bishop of
Milan lies there in a crystal casket with
gold trimmings, a screen of gold and silver
covers this. He is robed in cloth of gold
with crown and scepter, crosses and rings
of dimaonds, rubies, emeralds and sapphires.
Upon a silver table the candles, and around
the room in solid silver bas-relief is his his-
tory from the cradle to the grave, while up-
on all the chairs is his monogram in silver.
We ascended to the roof of the Cathedral
from which we had superb views of the
plains of Lombardy and the snowy Alps.
To morrow we say farewell to sunny Italy.
							KIM.
	BARONESS HIRSCH has given $250,000 to
endow a home for Hebrew consumptives
in England.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
UTAH.

STILL shall New England classic hills be dear,
Where’er the children of the pilgrims go; 
Her sacred name our hearts shall still revere
Her story fill us with responsive glow.
Where smooth Potomac’s placid waters flow, 
Virginia’s stately homes in beauty rise—
Beloved for him who taught the world to know
His country's freedom is the patriot's prize,
Not dearly bought, though won with costliest
	sacrifice.

The downy peaches waft their fruity breath
From sun-kissed orchards of the Delaware;
And flowers that know no winter’s icy death,
Garland the plains of Florida the fair;
’Neath California’s skies of beauty rare,
Her yellow poppies nod and roses twine;
Sweet scent of orange-blooms perfumes the air,
And amber clusters load the fragrant vine—
Each sphere of chalice filled with sweet, trans-
	lucent wine.

Fair was the heritage our father’s bought,
Stretching o’er hill and vale, from sea to sea;
Each new-born state some separate charm has
	brought,
Some grace distinctive, as its birth-right fee.
From Maine’s cold pine-woods, where the winds
	blow free,
And whistling, rivet tyrant winter’s chains.
The sunny rice-fields, where the bird and bee,
In slumberous tones, chant summer’s sweet re-
	frains.
Love’s loyal faith, sincere, each part the best
	maintains.

Let others boast their prairies spreading wide
Whose lonesome plains the far horizon meet;
Still let them paint with fond exultant pride,
Their miles of corn-fields and their waving wheat;
Or, let them tell of vast woods’ dim retreat,
With vaulted arches thick with evergreen,
Where vines run wild and mosses’ velvet feet
Climb o’er each tangled bough—a living screen
Whose solemn gloom of shade no sun-ray falls be-
	tween.

How would our eyes grow dim o’er those broad
	plains,
With home-sick longing for our russet hills!
Nor could we bear the depths where twilight 
	reigns,
Whose deep recesses cloistered silence fills.
Dearer to us the cliff whose grandeur thrills,
With its mute eloquence, the heart’s unrest;
Dearer the water-falls and leaping rills,
And canyons wild of Utah’s caverned breast,
Though other scenes be fair, we love our Utah
	best.

As when through leagues of blistering, shimmer,
	ing sand,
Its toilsome way the caravan has made,
Sees o’er the waste the cool, green palm-tree
	stand,
And rapturous, hails their bliss of restful shade—
And as the ship by tempest long delayed,
Beaten by storm-waves, shattered by the blast,
Gains the sure harbor, and, with anchor weighed,
Rests on the peaceful billow, safe at last,
Happy in perils shunned and dangers overpast—

So with their faces toward the golden west,
Through weary leagues, toil on the pilgrim train ; 
Storm-worn, but dauntless in their weary quest,
Cross the broad river and the treeless plain,
Till Utah’s rock-built height their footsteps gain.
Circled by cliffs, like some great eagle’s nest,
Lies a lone valley—nature’s wild domain :
'Here," says their chieftain, “here may Zion
	rest;
Here shall God’s temple stand, and here His
	Saints be blest."

[Column 2]
As on New England’s cold, surf-beaten shore,
The earlier pilgrims built devotion’s shrine;
’Mid the loud anthem of the ocean’s roar,
To great Jehovah tuned their swelling line,
And saw His love o’er stern, hard duty shine—
So knelt these toil-worn  souls—faith’s struggling
	band—
Asking in earnest prayer, the power divine
To shield them in the wild, waste, barren land.
And guide them in their toil up heaven’s direct-
	ing hand.

The world is richer by such pioneers;
Faith, through their toil, its golden fruitage sees,
And life’s hard front in softer light appears;
That the slow-moving world may walk at ease,
Truth builds her highways broad, by such as
	these.
They found a desert—made a garden fair,
Where luscious fruits bend low the clustering
	trees,
And flowers of every tint perfume the air,
Where towns and cities broad and equal blessing
	share.
I’ve heard a story of the days of old,
Told by Ute warriors when the pale-face  came—
How long ago a spirit, overbold,
With mighty Manitou made rival claim
With silver, gold and gems of every name,
He built proud turrets on the Wasatch range;
But the Great Spirit from his mount of flame.
With thunder-bolts of fire and lightnings strange,
Made walls and tower and throne to shapeless
	ruins change.
I know not if the tale be strictly true;
But yet our Utah mountains strangely show
Bright lines of yellow gold, and still we view
Masses of silver, heaped and strewn as though
From some vast silver temple’s overthrow—
Marbles and precious ore that might bedeck
The halls of monarchs with resplendent glow—
Jasper and syenite and jeweled speck—
As though a world of mines had burst in plen-
	teous wreck.

Fair land of marvels! Where Aladdin’s lamp
And Ali Baba’s treasure-cave are ours!
Where giant force has left his Titan stamp,
And genial nature gemmed her vales with
	flowers!
Thy gushing spring shall send refreshing
	showers,
Till all thy waste lands ring with harvest glee.
The weary thousands seek the restful bowers
Where health springs, jocund, from thy island
	sea,
Thy glad streams turn the wheels of strong-
	armed industry.
In thy young days thy horoscope we draw,
And stars auspicious tell of fates benign.
Symbol of order, industry and law,
The murmurous hive is emblem fitly thine.
Still ’round that hive thy sego lilies twine—
Manna for man and nectar cup for bee;
Born in the mountain where the sunbeams shine.
Its peerless bloom is type of liberty;
Be loyal like thy bees, and, like thy lilies, free.

Where from the heights the mountain torrents
	spring,
The eagle builds aloft her craggy nest,
Meets the new morning, poised on steady wing.
And soars through ether from the topmost
	crest—
Thy eagle’s flight be thine, oh, Utah blest
In that, from earliest days, thy children stand
With equal power to seek the highest, best,
And noblest; knowing thy impartial hand
Fetters no God-given soul in all thy favored land.

Onward and upward! of the sister states
Youngest; but gifted with a priceless dower!
Fortune waits, knocking at thy opening gates;
Press to the front in thine auspicious hour!

[Column 3]
Give to the world the best work in thy power:
Reach to each noble cause a helping hand;
And should dark clouds above the nation lower,
Rise in thy might, for God and native land,
Wave wide thy stars and stripes, and with them
	conquering stand.
						ORIELLE CURTIS.


']

Representative_Docs:
["[WomansExponent | Vol27 | 1898 | December | Issue: 14 | Title: CHRISTMAS AGAIN. | Author: Unknown]

CHRISTMAS AGAIN.

	Here comes Christmas again with all its
delightful accompaniments and memories.
No day in all the year for the children like
Christmas with its wonders and its legends,
fairy tales and above all Santa Claus, for 
whom they long and wait, and even pray,

[Column 2]
so enthusiastic are they about his coming.
Dear old Santa Claus! What gladness he
brings to many thousands of homes, and
alas for those bereft of children or where
none ever came to bless and kindle new
life in human hearts. But the children,
Santa Claus belongs especially to them,
they watch for him carefully, continuously,
getting up at unseasonable hours to try and
surprise him and to see which way he
comes; whether over the roof with his rein-
deers and sled and down the chimney, or
through the window, or stealing in softly
when the door is open and hiding until all
are asleep in the house to make his raid as
the Brownies do. What fantastic visions
the little folks conjure up of the visits of
Santa Claus. Christmas is the day of all
days for the little folks, no other day in the
year is so attractive, not even the 4th of
July with its flags, banners, music, parades
and booming of cannon; nor yet the child’s
own birthday when he is “cock of the
walk,” so to speak. Is it because of the
delusion of Santa Claus that fires the child’s
imagination? partly so it must be. And
those of older growth, they, too, are fasci-
nated with delusions and fancies. And it
this only happened one day in the year one
might be more excusable.
	This is the day of love in every house-
hold, of peace on earth and good will to all
mankind, and every one who feels the im-
portance of the sweet charity the Savior
taught and practised should himself exer-
cise that sweet spirit of sublime and heaven-
ly grace which reflects as much upon the
giver as the receiver. The scripture says
“It is more blessed to give than to receive,”
and it is a divine truth,as many can testify.
	To know how to give acceptably is an
art in itself and one which many people do
not understand; to give ostentatiously is al-
ways an offense, even though the giver
may have the best of motives. It is true
that Christmas opens a wide door for gifts
to loved ones as well as for charity, but
nevertheless, careless giving especially of
charity, sometimes makes the recipient feel
he would rather starve. We are all, or al-
most all, too neglectful of the little courte-
sies and proprieties of life; we excuse our-
selves because there is so much to be done,
and we say this is an age of hurry and bus-
tle. But when one looks around and finds
those who do things of this sort beautifully,
in such a way that there is no possible
chance of criticism, then we wonder what is
the reason that others could not do as well.
A gift is not so much in itself as in the
manner of giving.
	Children should be taught to give in the
spirit of love and sweetness, and then there
would be greater pleasure and more satis-
faction in small things. As a people we
have grown so selfish ourselves with the
prosperity the Lord has blessed us with,
that we feel unless we can give largely we
must not give at all. Yet when we look
back and recall the early days in this valley
and the few little comforts we had for the
children at Christmas time and compare it
with the present, its loads of toys and beau-
tiful as well as useful things it seems in-
credible. And are we happier or better, or
are the children any more satisfied ? We
think not. The molasses candy, ginger
bread and dough-nuts Santa Claus brought
then were as acceptable as the best sweet-
meats and Hiler’s candy and wax dolls that
can open and shut their eyes instead of the
rag babies made by their own mamma’s


deft fingers, into which she put so many
loving stitches.
	Yes we have a different Christmas now,
and this year we have much to be thankful
for and to rejoice over. Many little ones
whose father went to battle have him home
with them for the jolly Christmas. And
some whose homes are not so blest, yet
know the war is over and look forward to
the home coming with joyful anticipation.
	There has been a bountiful harvest, the
Lord has blest His people wonderfully and
the children should be made to realize this,
and to comprehend how the Lord has pro-
vided for the needs of His people and given
them an abundance and to spare, so that
on this Christmas day in all the valleys of
this Western land, there should be no
scarcity in any home and certainly not
where there are those, blessed messengers of
relief, Bishops and sisters of the Relief
Society. The dear, old people who are so
much alone in the world, more even than
the children should be richly remembered
at Christmas. They are as fond of dainties
as the children, though they cannot keep
up the delusion of Santa Claus, and if the
children were taught to divide with the
aged people of their own locality, those who
have no grandchildren of their own to come
and bring them delicacies, it would be a
greater blessing to the children than to
those to whom they minister in innocence
and love. So let us learn the Christ lesson.
If we only give to those who can give to us
in return, we are not the true followers of
the lowly Nazarene whose birth we cele-
brate, (even though we have long since
learned that it was not the day of his birth.)
Christmas has so long been kept on this
particular day that a change all over the
world could scarcely be effected.
	This year, 1898, Christmas comes on the
Sabbath day and therefore festivities will
be observed upon Saturday or Monday, or
both as the case may be, but the children
will probably receive fruitful lessons at the
Christmas Sunday School.
	Already the city has put on its holiday
appearance, and never was there more dis-
play. Stores, shops and business houses of
all kinds are in gala dress, and the brighter,
gayer colors that have been the rage the
last year or more add to the brilliancy of
the decorations. In the midst of all this
display perhaps there is as much delusion
as attaches to Santa Claus. The home and
the fireside is always connected with Christ-
mas so closely that one invariably thinks of
the yule log, or the huge pine knots and
the joys of social or even jovial family inter-
course. “The holly round the Christmas
hearth” has become proverbial, and

	‘‘At Christmas time the open hand,
	Scatters bounty o’er sea and land.”

	It is good that we have the holidays,
what would life be without these seasons of
renewal? It makes us almost young again
to participate in such rejoicings.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
", '[WomansExponent | Vol34 | 1905 | February | Issue: 8 | Title: CHRISTMAS IN SCANDINAVIA. | Author: Unknown]

CHRISTMAS IN SCANDINAVIA.
	Christmas is the most important of all
feasts or “holy days” in the northern
countries. It was originally the feast of
the sun, a feast of rejoicing because the
shortest day of the season had passed,
and the sun had commenced its return to
the north. After the introduction of
Christianity into Scandinavia, its signifi-
cance was changed, and instead of being
the feast of the sun, it became a feast
in honor of the advent of the Son of Man,
and it was for many centuries the begin-
ning of the year.
	In olden times the people commenced
making preparations for Christmas sev-
eral weeks before the time, and among
the most important of these preparations
was the making of Christmas beer, which
was done with great care. Even among
the poorer classes, where the family beer
was ordinarily very weak, a much better
quality was made for Christmas, and suf-
ficient of it must be had to supply the
family liberally, and to treat every visitor
during the thirteen successive Christmas
days. During Christmas quarreling and
fighting was strictly forbidden; all must
be peace and good will, and all court
proceedings were suspended. Should
anyone do harm to his fellow man during
Christmas, great punishment would be
meted out to him later. Nor were the
people permitted to do any kind of hard
manual labor, only the most necessary
work, such as feeding the stock, etc., was
permissible.
	The so-called Christmas peace was also
extended to the brute creation. As long
as Christmas lasted man should keep
peace even with their natural enemies,
such as wolves, bears, rats and mice. To
avoid giving offence, these animals were
not even to be called by their right
names; but the wolf was during Christ-
mas called “grey leg” the bear “the old
one in furs,” mice the “little greys,” and
rats “long tails.”
	As the 24th of December drew nearer
the confusion and disorder, incident to
the preparations, became greater, and on
the 23rd of December it was at its height.
It was baking, cooking and a thorough gen-
real house cleaning that had to be done.
Everything had to be ready by sundown on
the 24th of December. Among the prepa-
rations for Christmas was also the rubbing
of the teeth of all domestic animals, and
that being done, horses, catte, sheep, etc.
were treated to a double portion of the
best of their usual allowance. An old
legend says that at midnight after Christ-
mas eve, at the exact hour that Christ
was supposed to have been born, all
animals received the power of speech,

[Column 2]
and could for a few moments converse
with each other like human beings,
	Everything else being ready, the peo-
ple turned their attention to their person-
al appearance; a bath and a complete
change of clothing on Christmas eve was
universal; and when attired in their best
the members of the household greeted
each other appropriately with “Merry
Christmas, health and strength and all
that is dear.” Then followed exchanges
of Christmas gifts, and then all sat down
to a bounteous Christmas supper in the
decorated room. The meal consisted
usually of dried fish and sweet porridge,
or ham with green cabbage and mush,
usually rice with well-to-do people. Af-
ter the table was once set Christmas eve,
it was never cleared until Christmas was
through, as every visitor in the house
during the time must, without exception,
partake of the hospitality of the same.
None were forgotten on Christmas eve;
all poor people were remembered; a large
dish was set out for the Christmas Saint
(Santa Claus), and the birds were provid-
ed for liberally.
	The Christmas cake was a most impor-
tant feature in every household; some-
times it was made in the shape of a man,
a ram, or a fat hog, otherwise perfectly
round. Sometimes a gold or silver coin
was placed in the center of the cake.
Christmas gifts commenced with ex-
changes of these cakes, but later they
were accompanied with gifts. The cus-
tom pertaining to Christmas cake was
introduced into England by the Vikings
from Scandinavia a thousand years ago,
and then “Yule cake” (yule or jul is the
Danish for Christmas) is well known to
this day.
	While eating the Christmas supper,
the head of the family would always
present a toast for Christ and the New
Year, and all would drink in honor there-
of from the oldest and most costly vessel
which the household possessed. Beer
and “mjod,’ (mead made of honey) were
the beverages used at Christmas, and this
was freely used by both men and women.
Whiskey and strong beverages were sel-
dom used on Christmas eve. After the
more solid meal, “Ebleskiver” was served
and the meal was prolonged as much as
possible, as there existed an old su-
perstitious legend to the effect that
the one who got through eating first
would die before the year was through.
At length, when the meal was over, the
head of the family offered a benediction,
and then gave the hand to the housewife,
saying, “Tak for Mad” (thanks for the
food), and after that shook hands with
all in the house; then follows a general
handshaking with “Tak for Mad,” coup-
led with many good wishes; the children
and the servants come in last; they com-
mence with the oldest person of the
household and end with the youngest.
	Many superstitious customs were asso-
ciated with Christmas eve proceedings:
Thus, in the glare of the Christmas can-
dles, which were to burn all night, young
people could see their future mate, and
everybody, to a greater or less extent,
see their future, at least what would hap-
pen to them the next year.
	Christmas day (December 25th) was
observed as a solemn holiday. Then ev-
erybody went to church, but spent the
rest of the day quietly at home, and it
was considered a very offensive breach of

[Column 3]
Christmas etiquette for any visits to be
made on that day. But on the 26th were
commenced the general Christmas fes-
tivities, aside from the solemn assemblies,
and they continued until the 5th of Janu-
ary. An important feature of the second
Christmas day was the horse feast, which
originated in the ancient Scandinavian
mythology, when the god Frey rode
around among his people on a holy ani-
mal; but when Christianity was intro-
duced in Scandinavia, they substituted
Stephen, the first Christian martyr, for
the god Frey, and the custom finally
dwindled down to general Christmas
races.
	Numerous and varied were the sports
and festivities of Christmas in the Scan-
dinavian countries in the olden times, but
these have considerably reduced of late
years. Yet Christmas, even today, is
looked forward to with much joy and an-
ticipation, and considerable good will is
manifested by the people, one toward
another, during the Christmas holidays,
and the exchange of presents and friendly
greetings is in order, as much now as it
ever was. The Christmas tree festivities
were almost unknown in Scandinavia un-
til seventy-five years ago, but were uni-
versal in France as early as the thirteenth
century.
		EMMA HOWELL JENSON.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol28 | 1899 | December | Issue: 14 | Title: CHRISTMAS FANCIES. | Author: Unknown]

CHRISTMAS FANCIES.
Peace and good will to all mankind.
	Blessed Christmas if indeed it would
bring peace and good-will to all mankind.
Nowadays the Christmas time seems more
of a festivity and giving and receiving
gifts, than a time to exercise good will and
good feeling toward one’s neighbors; to
forgive wrongs and make peace with those
with whom we have been at variance, if
there are any such, would indeed make the
day a happy one. Cheerfulness is better
than hilarity, and simplicity even in giv-
ing is in much better taste than making
elaborate presents, or parading one’s

[Column 2]
generosity, as really seems to have come
to be the custom in this age. We deal
largely in extremes, even in giving, and
we do not always deal wisely with our-
selves or our friends. “Kind hearts are
more than coronets,” and the scripture
tells us, “a word fitly spoken is like apples
of gold in pictures of silver.” Therefore,
though we have not wealth to lavish on
our loved ones, we can bestow kindness
even in words.
	As a people we are in the enjoyment of
many blessings, and we have great reason
to rejoice that prosperity abounds in these
vales of the mountains and that we have
peace here, though we read and hear of
wars in other lands; and we should not for-
get the Lord in the days of our prosperity.
	Feasting and enjoying the good things
of life does not make up the sum of happi-
ness, not even at Christmas time; elabor-
ate preparations are very wearisome, and
the mother is often too much exhausted to
share in the children’s enjoyment of games
or sports, or perhaps to answer their ques-
tions about the why’s and wherefore’s of
Christmas. The injunction placed upon
us all to take care of our bodies, is greatly
disregarded at holiday times, or overdone,
we work so hard for the pleasure of a few
hours, we cook so much and some of us
eat so much that we are not happy and we
show it in our faces, therefore we are not
beautiful, though we may have bestowed
abundant pains upon our decorations to
make the home beautiful. If the mother
is care-worn or exhausted, if she cannot
wear a smiling face, then the day is not a
success. It is not necessary to have all
the luxuries the market affords, not even
on Christmas, to bring happiness; light
hearts, cheerful faces and kind words are
a far better relish at the family dinner
than the most savory dish lacking these
graces. Yet one does not like to be always
sermonizing. There are many pretty 
fancies about Christmas that have come
down to us, that set it off, so to speak, as
a day apart from all others, in the minds
of the little folks at least; and perhaps
none of us are altogether without a taste
for the mystic, and the legends and folk-
lore of ye olden time and of other lands
and other people, their habits, customs
and beliefs, have a more intense interest
because of their remoteness and the mys-
tery in which they are necessarily en-
shrouded.
	From the early Christians who sang the
midnight mass on the eve of the nativity,
in commemoration of the birth of Christ,
we get the name Christmas (Christ’s mass.)
Formerly immense logs were brought from
the forests and, often with much ceremony,
burned at Christmas time with great re-
joicing. Although we do not hear of the
yule log these days, yet a great log is
chosen even now where wood is used for
fuel; we who use coal do not have the
same feeling about the Xmas log. The
pictures in the fire on Christmas were con-
sidered significant, and the old wrongs
were thought to die out in the blaze.
Numberless superstitions that cluster

[Column 3]
around Christmas observances in the olden
time, are interwoven with Pagan and some
of them even early Christian ceremonies.
	It is said that “in Sweden on Christmas
Eve the shoes of the family are placed in
a row side by side, that harmony may pre-
vail during the year to come.” 
	“Throughout Northern Germany the
tables are spread and the lights left burn-
ing, that the Virgin Mary and the angel 
who brings the presents may find refresh-
ments.”
	This to us seems very absurd, but if in
our day the children thought more of the
mother or father, sister, brother or friend,
who gives on these occasions, and paid
them the reverence which is their due, the
spirit of jealousy and contention would
cease, and greater reverence might be ac-
corded to those who are really the good
angels in disguise.
	Even to this day it is a belief in Corn-
wall, Devonshire, and in English villages,
that the bees hum and that the cattle kneel
on Christmas Eve in adoration of the
Holy Child; and it is said the powers of
darkness are unable to work evil on man-
kind. Shakespeare, who is more often
quoted than any other poet, says:

“Some say that ever ’gainst that season comes
Wherein our Savior’s birth is celebrated,
The bird of dawning singeth all night long;
And then, they say, no spirit can walk abroad;
No fairy takes, nor witch hath power to charm,
So hallowed and so precious is the time.”

	The customs connected with the chil-
dren’s idea of Christmas have their origin
in Germany and the Netherlands. Our
little ones imitate the child of Holland
when they hang their stockings in front of 
the fireplace, so Santa Claus will find
them when he descends the chimney. The
beautiful Christmas tree, its branches
laden and lights sparkling, a sine que non,
with children nowadays is distinctly a
heathen idea perpetuated by the German
and Scandinavian races. The floral deco-
rations, which add so much to the beauty
of the festival, were inaugurated in the
days of the Saturnalia when the Roman
temples and houses were decorated with
flowers and garlands in honor of the god
of light. In the days of early antiquity
the British Druids reverenced the mistletoe
as something holy, when they found it
growing on an oak, the favorite tree of 
their divinity, Tutanes, the sun. When
they celebrated the winter solstice the
ancient Britons, accompanied by Druids,
went with music and singing to gather the
sacred mistletoe, and it was believed to
have a healing power. Clothed in white
the chief priest cut the tree with a golden
knife and the mistletoe (a sort of parasite)
would fall into the robe of the priest stand-
ing below, who distributed it among the
people, who carried it home and hung it
under the roof as a protection and shelter
to the sylvan deities during the winter
frost and cold. The Druids regarded the
pearly berries of the plant as an emblem
of purity and used it in the marriage rite.
The mistletoe custom still observed of
snatching a kiss under the mistletoe is


[Column 1]
associated with the mystic traditions, and
is a relic of the days of Druidism. The old
song we used to sing says:
	“The mistleto hung in the castle hall,
	The holy branch shone on the old oak wall,
	And all the tenants were blithe and gay,
	Keeping their Christmas holiday.”
	Holly, evergreen and laurel were used
to decorate the churches in early days in
England, as they are now. Ivy, though
sometimes used, is considered more appro-
priate for solemn occasions. Late in the
17th century the Ecclesiastical Canon of
England issued a decree that all Christ-
mas decorations must be down by the last
of January, as it was thought to be un-
lucky to keep them up longer in private
houses.
	At the time when the Druids were gath-
ering their mistletoe and the Romans were
decorating their temples in honor of Sat-
urn, the Scandinavians kindled huge bon-
fires in honor of the sun god, Thor.
	The good cheer of old England at
Christmas time is proverbial, and little
wonder when one reads of the feasting.
The peacock with his gorgeous train was
a royal dish brought to the festal board by
some fair lady, and over him the knights
errant swore to do or die for their “ladyes
fayre.” From this custom comes Justice
Swallow’s oath, “By cock and pie.” In
Charles II. time turkeys and capons with
plum pudding became the regular dish,
and the mince pie still perpetuated, was a
special requisite at a Xmas dinner more
than a hundred years ago.
	The Newcastle Chronicle of 1770 gives the
contents of the famous “Christmas pye”
of the olden time. * * * Its ingredients
were 2 bushels of flour, 20 pounds of
butter, 4 geese, 2 turkeys, 2 rabbits, 4 wild
ducks, 2 woodcocks, 6 snipe, 4 partridges,
2 neats tongues, 2 curlews, 4 blackbirds
and six pigeons, with eggs, sugar, raisins,
lemons, oranges and various kinds of
spicery. “it weighed twelve stone.” One
would wonder where it could be baked;
and yet when one knows what great fire-
places and ovens they had in those old
halls and castles, one might imagine that
even such a pie could be put in and baked.
In the lower part of the Nottingham
Castle, which, by the by, is about all that
is left of it, we saw the largest ovens, just
like a great arch, one on each side of a
huge fireplace. Of course they are not
in very good preservation, but are still
standing; and at Kenilworth Castle we
counted the remains of nine or ten im-
mense fireplaces, in one area of the ruin,
doubtless there had been ovens there
to correspond with the size of the fire-
places. When one recalls the number of
the inmates of these great buildings and
the retainers, and that there were no bake
shops adjacent to buy food from, and
everything must be made on the premises,
one can fancy great quantities of food
cooked somewhere.
	The writer when standing in St. George’s
Hall, Windsor Castle, thought of the great
banquets that had been given there to the
English warriors, and also when the brave
heroes and men were initiated into the
order of the Knights of the Garter; what
eating, drinking and carousing must have
taken place, and what wonderful prepara-
tions in the way of cooking must have pre-
ceded these great occasions. At the pres-
ent time one can buy almost everything
ready cooked, and the facilities for prepar-

[Column 2]
ing food are altogether easier than they
were even fifty years ago. 
	Christmas in England this year cannot
be a very merrie one, judging from the
dispatches of the war in South Africa, and
although Queen Victoria is very heroic,
yet so many of Her Majesty’s officers have
been slain or wounded, that conditions
must necessarily have a depressing effect
upon the whole country. The Queen will
have the sympathy of all true Americans
in this time of anxiety, for she is justly
beloved by the people of the United States.
It is to be hoped that matters may be so
adjusted as to bring this cruel war to a
close; and now that the court of arbitration
is so near being established the outlook for
a final compact seems more likely to be
consummated.
	How ardently one wishes for the day
when war shall cease, and the angelic in-
junction, “On earth peace, good will to-
ward men,” find a hearty response from
all nations. The “Yule peace,” old as the
Runic stones of antiquity, a time of good
fellowship and brotherly love, quarrels all
healed, feuds forgotten, and the world ani-
mated by the universal spirit of love. Is
this too much to hope for? At any rate,
each one who strives to love his neighbor
as himself is doing his utmost to fufill the
law.  Our blessed Savior said:
	Love your enemies, do good to them which hate
you.
	Bless them that curse you, and pray for them
that despitefully use you and persecute you.
	How few there are who heed these
words in the least, yet desire to keep the
commandments and excel in many ways.
Jesus in His “Sermon on the Mount” said:
“Blessed are the peacemakers, for they
shall be called the children of God.” And
what more fitting time to strive to culti-
vate this in one’s heart than the day on
which we celebrate the nativity of the
Savior of the world?
	Many mothers and housewives are like
Martha, whom the Savior rebuked, they
work and worry and fret, over-burdened,
over-solicitous, “troubled about many
things.” It would be so much better if
there were less cooking, less variety, and
if mothers were not so weary and could
get a little more real enjoyment at Christ-
mas time.
	“At Christmas play and make good cheer,
	For Christmas comes but once a year.”
